🚀 3. Getting Started with Systems Manager

Follow these steps to start managing your anihpj Django servers with Systems Manager:

📋 Step 1: Prerequisites
  • EC2 instances running (anihpj-web-1, web-2, web-3)
  • Instances have internet connectivity or VPC endpoints
  • Ubuntu 22.04/Amazon Linux (SSM Agent pre-installed)
  • IAM permissions to use Systems Manager
🔐 Step 2: Create IAM Role
  • Create IAM role: anihpj-SSM-Role
  • Attach policy: AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore
  • Attach role to all anihpj EC2 instances
  • Instances will appear in Fleet Manager
✅ Step 3: Verify Connection
  • Open Systems Manager console
  • Go to Fleet Manager
  • Verify all 3 instances show "Online" status
  • Start using Session Manager, Run Command
🔧 Step 4: Configure Features
  • Set up Parameter Store for secrets
  • Configure Patch Manager baselines
  • Enable Session Manager logging
  • Create maintenance windows

🖥️ 4. Session Manager - Secure Browser-Based Shell

Session Manager provides secure, auditable instance management without the need for SSH keys, bastion hosts, or open inbound ports.

🎯 Why Session Manager is Perfect for anihpj
Traditional SSH Session Manager Benefit for anihpj
❌ Requires SSH keys to manage ✅ No SSH keys needed No key rotation, no key loss risk
❌ Must open port 22 (security risk) ✅ No inbound ports required Reduced attack surface
❌ Requires bastion host ($$) ✅ Direct access from browser Save money, reduce complexity
❌ Limited audit logs ✅ Full audit in CloudTrail Compliance, security forensics
❌ Manual access control ✅ IAM-based access control Fine-grained permissions per user
🚀 Connect to anihpj Server via Session Manager
Method 1: AWS Console (Click & Connect)
1. Open AWS Systems Manager console
2. Navigate to: Session Manager → Start session
3. Select instance: anihpj-web-1
4. Click "Start session"
5. ✅ Browser-based terminal opens instantly!
Method 2: AWS CLI (Terminal)
# Install Session Manager plugin first (one-time setup)
# macOS/Linux:
curl "https://s3.amazonaws.com/session-manager-downloads/plugin/latest/mac/sessionmanager-bundle.zip" -o "sessionmanager-bundle.zip"
unzip sessionmanager-bundle.zip
sudo ./sessionmanager-bundle/install -i /usr/local/sessionmanagerplugin -b /usr/local/bin/session-manager-plugin

# Windows: Download and run SessionManagerPluginSetup.exe from AWS

# Connect to instance
aws ssm start-session --target i-1234567890abcdef0

# Output:
# Starting session with SessionId: admin-0123456789abcdef0
# 
# ubuntu@ip-10-0-1-42:~$ whoami
# ubuntu
# ubuntu@ip-10-0-1-42:~$ cd /var/www/anihpj
# ubuntu@ip-10-0-1-42:/var/www/anihpj$ ls
# manage.py  requirements.txt  anihpj/  jobpost/
Method 3: Port Forwarding for Local Development
# Forward remote Django server port 8000 to local port 8000
aws ssm start-session \
    --target i-1234567890abcdef0 \
    --document-name AWS-StartPortForwardingSession \
    --parameters '{"portNumber":["8000"],"localPortNumber":["8000"]}'

# Now access Django admin locally:
# http://localhost:8000/admin

# Perfect for debugging production Django without exposing ports!
✅ Pro Tip: Session Manager automatically logs all commands to CloudWatch Logs and S3. Enable session logging for compliance and security auditing.

🔐 5. Parameter Store - Secure Secrets Management

Parameter Store provides secure, hierarchical storage for configuration data and secrets management. Store database passwords, API keys, and configuration strings that your anihpj Django application can retrieve at runtime.

📊 Parameter Store Types
Type Features anihpj Use Case Cost
Standard • Up to 10,000 parameters
• 4 KB value size
• No cost
• Standard throughput 		• Database passwords
• API keys
• Django SECRET_KEY
• Configuration values 		FREE ✅
Advanced • 100,000+ parameters
• 8 KB value size
• Parameter policies
• Higher throughput 		• Large config files
• Expiration policies
• Change notifications
• High-traffic apps 		\.05/param/month
💡 Recommendation for anihpj: Use Standard parameters (FREE). They're perfect for storing your database password, SECRET_KEY, and API keys. You'll likely only need 5-10 parameters total.
🔧 Store anihpj Django Secrets in Parameter Store
# Store database password (encrypted with AWS KMS)
aws ssm put-parameter \
    --name "/anihpj/production/db/password" \
    --value "your-strong-db-password-here" \
    --type "SecureString" \
    --description "PostgreSQL database password for anihpj production"

# Store Django SECRET_KEY
aws ssm put-parameter \
    --name "/anihpj/production/django/secret-key" \
    --value "django-insecure-your-secret-key-50-chars-long" \
    --type "SecureString" \
    --description "Django SECRET_KEY for anihpj"

# Store external API keys
aws ssm put-parameter \
    --name "/anihpj/production/api/sendgrid-key" \
    --value "SG.your-sendgrid-api-key" \
    --type "SecureString"

# Store non-secret configuration
aws ssm put-parameter \
    --name "/anihpj/production/config/allowed-hosts" \
    --value "anihpj.com,www.anihpj.com" \
    --type "String"

# ✅ All secrets encrypted at rest with AWS KMS (default key is FREE)
🐍 Retrieve Secrets in Django settings.py
# anihpj/settings.py
import boto3
import os

# Initialize SSM client
ssm = boto3.client('ssm', region_name='us-east-1')

def get_parameter(name, decrypt=True):
    """Retrieve parameter from AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store"""
    try:
        response = ssm.get_parameter(
            Name=name,
            WithDecryption=decrypt
        )
        return response['Parameter']['Value']
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error retrieving parameter {name}: {e}")
        return None

# Fetch secrets from Parameter Store
SECRET_KEY = get_parameter('/anihpj/production/django/secret-key')
DB_PASSWORD = get_parameter('/anihpj/production/db/password')
SENDGRID_API_KEY = get_parameter('/anihpj/production/api/sendgrid-key')

# Fetch configuration values
ALLOWED_HOSTS = get_parameter('/anihpj/production/config/allowed-hosts', decrypt=False).split(',')

# Database configuration using Parameter Store values
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'NAME': 'anihpjdb',
        'USER': 'anihpj_admin',
        'PASSWORD': DB_PASSWORD,  # From Parameter Store
        'HOST': 'anihpj-db.abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com',
        'PORT': '5432',
    }
}

# Email configuration
EMAIL_BACKEND = 'django.core.mail.backends.smtp.EmailBackend'
EMAIL_HOST = 'smtp.sendgrid.net'
EMAIL_HOST_USER = 'apikey'
EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD = SENDGRID_API_KEY  # From Parameter Store
EMAIL_PORT = 587
EMAIL_USE_TLS = True
🔍 Retrieve Secrets via AWS CLI
# Get decrypted parameter value
aws ssm get-parameter \
    --name "/anihpj/production/db/password" \
    --with-decryption \
    --query "Parameter.Value" \
    --output text

# List all anihpj parameters
aws ssm get-parameters-by-path \
    --path "/anihpj/production" \
    --recursive

# Get multiple parameters at once
aws ssm get-parameters \
    --names "/anihpj/production/db/password" "/anihpj/production/django/secret-key" \
    --with-decryption
🔒 Security Benefits
  • No secrets in code or .env files
  • Encrypted at rest with AWS KMS
  • IAM-controlled access
  • Audit trail in CloudTrail
  • Automatic rotation support
💰 Cost Savings
  • Standard parameters are FREE
  • 10,000 parameters limit (way more than needed)
  • No additional infrastructure
  • No third-party secrets manager fees
⚡ Easy Integration
  • Simple boto3 API calls
  • Works with Django, Flask, any Python app
  • Hierarchical naming (/app/env/key)
  • Versioning for rollback

🔄 6. Patch Manager - Automated OS Patching

Patch Manager automates the process of patching managed instances with security updates and other types of updates. Keep your anihpj Django servers secure without manual intervention.

🎯 Patch Manager Workflow
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    PATCH MANAGER WORKFLOW FOR ANIHPJ                    │
│                                                                         │
│  Step 1: Define Patch Baseline                                         │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Patch Baseline: anihpj-ubuntu-baseline                           │ │
│  │  • Operating System: Ubuntu                                       │ │
│  │  │  • Approve critical security patches: Automatically            │ │
│  │  • Approve important patches: Automatically                       │ │
│  │  • Approve low/medium patches: After 7 days                       │ │
│  │  • Rejected patches: None                                         │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                            │                                            │
│                            ▼                                            │
│  Step 2: Create Maintenance Window                                     │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Window: anihpj-patching-window                                   │ │
│  │  • Schedule: Every Sunday at 2:00 AM UTC (low traffic time)      │ │
│  │  • Duration: 4 hours                                              │ │
│  │  • Target: All anihpj-web-* instances (tag-based)                 │ │
│  │  • Reboot: If required                                            │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                            │                                            │
│                            ▼                                            │
│  Step 3: Automated Execution (Every Sunday 2 AM)                       │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  2:00 AM - Patch Manager starts                                   │ │
│  │  2:01 AM - Scan anihpj-web-1 for missing patches                  │ │
│  │  2:02 AM - Install 12 security updates on web-1                   │ │
│  │  2:15 AM - Reboot web-1 (if needed)                               │ │
│  │  2:20 AM - web-1 back online, scan web-2                          │ │
│  │  2:35 AM - web-2 patched and rebooted                             │ │
│  │  2:50 AM - web-3 patched and rebooted                             │ │
│  │  3:00 AM - ✅ All servers patched successfully                     │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                            │                                            │
│                            ▼                                            │
│  Step 4: Compliance Reporting                                          │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Compliance Dashboard                                              │ │
│  │  • anihpj-web-1: ✅ Compliant (0 missing patches)                  │ │
│  │  • anihpj-web-2: ✅ Compliant (0 missing patches)                  │ │
│  │  • anihpj-web-3: ✅ Compliant (0 missing patches)                  │ │
│  │  Last scan: 2026-01-12 02:00 UTC                                  │ │
│  │  Next window: 2026-01-19 02:00 UTC                                │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🚀 Set Up Patch Manager for anihpj
Step 1: Create Patch Baseline
# Create custom patch baseline for Ubuntu
aws ssm create-patch-baseline \
    --name "anihpj-ubuntu-baseline" \
    --description "Security patch baseline for anihpj Django servers" \
    --operating-system "UBUNTU" \
    --approval-rules '{
        "PatchRules": [{
            "PatchFilterGroup": {
                "PatchFilters": [{
                    "Key": "PRIORITY",
                    "Values": ["Critical", "Important"]
                }]
            },
            "ApproveAfterDays": 0,
            "ComplianceLevel": "CRITICAL"
        }]
    }' \
    --approved-patches-compliance-level "HIGH"

# Output:
# {
#     "BaselineId": "pb-0123456789abcdef0"
# }
Step 2: Register Patch Baseline as Default
# Set as default baseline for Ubuntu
aws ssm register-default-patch-baseline \
    --baseline-id pb-0123456789abcdef0

# Verify
aws ssm get-default-patch-baseline \
    --operating-system UBUNTU
Step 3: Create Maintenance Window
# Create maintenance window (Every Sunday at 2 AM UTC)
aws ssm create-maintenance-window \
    --name "anihpj-patching-window" \
    --description "Weekly patching for anihpj Django servers" \
    --schedule "cron(0 2 ? * SUN *)" \
    --duration 4 \
    --cutoff 1 \
    --allow-unassociated-targets \
    --no-enabled

# Output:
# {
#     "WindowId": "mw-0123456789abcdef0"
# }

# Register targets (all anihpj EC2 instances with tag)
aws ssm register-target-with-maintenance-window \
    --window-id mw-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --target-type "INSTANCE" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --owner-information "anihpj Django web servers"

# Register patch task
aws ssm register-task-with-maintenance-window \
    --window-id mw-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --target-id "target-id-from-previous-command" \
    --task-type "RUN_COMMAND" \
    --task-arn "AWS-RunPatchBaseline" \
    --priority 1 \
    --max-concurrency "1" \
    --max-errors "1" \
    --task-invocation-parameters '{
        "RunCommand": {
            "Parameters": {
                "Operation": ["Install"]
            }
        }
    }'

# Enable the maintenance window
aws ssm update-maintenance-window \
    --window-id mw-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --enabled
Step 4: Manual Patch Scan (On-Demand)
# Scan for missing patches on all anihpj servers
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunPatchBaseline" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters '{"Operation":["Scan"]}'

# Output:
# {
#     "Command": {
#         "CommandId": "cmd-0123456789abcdef0"
#     }
# }

# Check scan results
aws ssm list-command-invocations \
    --command-id cmd-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --details

# View compliance status
aws ssm describe-instance-patch-states \
    --instance-ids i-1234567890abcdef0
Step 5: Immediate Patch Installation (Emergency)
# Install patches immediately on specific instance
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunPatchBaseline" \
    --targets "Key=instanceids,Values=i-1234567890abcdef0" \
    --parameters '{"Operation":["Install"],"RebootOption":["RebootIfNeeded"]}'

# Monitor patching progress
aws ssm get-command-invocation \
    --command-id cmd-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --instance-id i-1234567890abcdef0
⚠️ Important for anihpj Production:
  • Schedule patching during low-traffic hours (2-4 AM UTC recommended)
  • Use --max-concurrency "1" to patch one server at a time (no downtime with ALB)
  • Always test patches in staging environment first
  • Keep Django app stateless so server reboots don't affect users
📊 Patch Compliance Dashboard
Instance Patch State Missing Patches Last Patched Action
anihpj-web-1 ✅ Compliant 0 2026-01-12 02:15 UTC None required
anihpj-web-2 ✅ Compliant 0 2026-01-12 02:35 UTC None required
anihpj-web-3 ⚠️ Non-Compliant 3 (2 critical, 1 important) 2026-01-05 02:50 UTC Patch immediately

🔧 7. Run Command - Execute Commands Across Fleet

Run Command lets you remotely and securely manage the configuration of your managed instances at scale. Execute commands on multiple anihpj servers simultaneously without SSH.

🚀 Common Use Cases for anihpj
🚀 Deploy Code

Pull latest Django code from Git and restart services on all servers in 30 seconds
🔄 Restart Services

Restart Gunicorn/Nginx across all servers simultaneously with one command
📊 Collect Data

Gather logs, check disk space, or run Django management commands fleet-wide
🔧 Configuration Updates

Update Nginx configs, environment variables, or system settings
🎯 Real-World Run Command Examples for anihpj
Example 1: Deploy Latest Django Code to All Servers
# Deploy to all anihpj web servers tagged with Application=anihpj
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "cd /var/www/anihpj",
        "git pull origin main",
        "source venv/bin/activate",
        "pip install -r requirements.txt",
        "python manage.py collectstatic --noinput",
        "python manage.py migrate",
        "sudo systemctl restart gunicorn",
        "sudo systemctl restart nginx",
        "echo Deployment completed at \01/11/2026 10:22:17"
    ]' \
    --comment "Deploy anihpj v2.1.0"

# Output:
# {
#     "Command": {
#         "CommandId": "cmd-deploy-abc123",
#         "TargetCount": 3,
#         "Status": "Pending"
#     }
# }

# ✅ All 3 servers deploy simultaneously in ~30 seconds!
Example 2: Restart Django/Gunicorn on All Servers
# Restart services across fleet
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "sudo systemctl restart gunicorn",
        "sudo systemctl status gunicorn",
        "echo Server restarted: \Anihs-Canvas"
    ]'

# Monitor command execution
aws ssm list-command-invocations \
    --command-id cmd-deploy-abc123 \
    --details

# Check which servers succeeded
aws ssm list-command-invocations \
    --command-id cmd-deploy-abc123 \
    --filters key=Status,value=Success
Example 3: Run Django Management Command Fleet-Wide
# Clear expired sessions on all Django servers
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "cd /var/www/anihpj",
        "source venv/bin/activate",
        "python manage.py clearsessions",
        "echo Sessions cleared on \Anihs-Canvas"
    ]'

# Create superuser on specific server
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --instance-ids "i-1234567890abcdef0" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "cd /var/www/anihpj",
        "source venv/bin/activate",
        "python manage.py createsuperuser --noinput --email admin@anihpj.com"
    ]'
Example 4: Check System Health Across Fleet
# Check disk space on all servers
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "df -h /",
        "free -h",
        "uptime",
        "systemctl status gunicorn | grep Active"
    ]' \
    --output-s3-bucket-name "anihpj-ssm-logs" \
    --output-s3-key-prefix "health-checks"

# Get command output
aws ssm get-command-invocation \
    --command-id cmd-health-abc123 \
    --instance-id i-1234567890abcdef0 \
    --query "StandardOutputContent" \
    --output text

# Output:
# Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
# /dev/xvda1       20G  8.2G   11G  43% /
# 
#               total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
# Mem:           3.8Gi       1.2Gi       1.1Gi       8.0Mi       1.5Gi       2.4Gi
# Swap:            0B          0B          0B
# 
#  03:45:12 up 23 days,  4:32,  0 users,  load average: 0.15, 0.12, 0.08
# 
# Active: active (running) since Sun 2026-01-12 02:35:18 UTC; 1h 10min ago
Example 5: Update Configuration File on All Servers
# Update Nginx configuration
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "sudo sed -i \"s/worker_connections 1024/worker_connections 2048/\" /etc/nginx/nginx.conf",
        "sudo nginx -t",
        "sudo systemctl reload nginx",
        "echo Nginx configuration updated"
    ]'

# Set environment variable in systemd service
aws ssm send-command \
    --document-name "AWS-RunShellScript" \
    --targets "Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj" \
    --parameters 'commands=[
        "sudo sed -i \"/\\[Service\\]/a Environment=DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE=anihpj.settings.production\" /etc/systemd/system/gunicorn.service",
        "sudo systemctl daemon-reload",
        "sudo systemctl restart gunicorn"
    ]'
📊 Run Command Execution States
State Description What It Means for anihpj
Pending Command sent, waiting for instances to pick it up SSM Agents on servers retrieving command
In Progress Command currently executing on instances Django deployment or restart happening now
Success Command completed successfully (exit code 0) ✅ Deployment or operation successful
Failed Command returned non-zero exit code ❌ Check logs, fix issue, retry
TimedOut Command exceeded timeout limit Increase timeout or check for hung processes
Cancelled Command cancelled by user Manual cancellation (emergency stop)
✅ Pro Tips for anihpj:
  • Use tags: Target servers with Key=tag:Application,Values=anihpj instead of instance IDs
  • Save output to S3: Use --output-s3-bucket-name for audit trails
  • Rate control: Use --max-concurrency "1" to deploy one server at a time (zero downtime)
  • Error handling: Set --max-errors "0" to stop on first failure

🏆 9. Systems Manager Best Practices

🔒 Security Best Practices
🔐 IAM Permissions
  • Use least-privilege IAM roles
  • Separate roles for read vs write operations
  • Use condition keys to restrict access
  • Example: Limit Session Manager to specific instances
📝 Audit Logging
  • Enable CloudTrail for all API calls
  • Send Session Manager logs to CloudWatch
  • Store Run Command output in S3
  • Set up alerts for suspicious activity
🔒 Secrets Management
  • Always use SecureString for sensitive data
  • Use AWS KMS customer managed keys
  • Rotate secrets regularly (90 days)
  • Never log secrets in Run Command output
⚡ Operational Best Practices
🏷️ Tagging Strategy
  • Tag instances: Application=anihpj
  • Environment tags: prod, staging, dev
  • Use tags for Run Command targeting
  • Consistent naming: anihpj-web-1, web-2
🔄 Automation
  • Automate patching with maintenance windows
  • Use State Manager for consistent configs
  • Create runbooks for common tasks
  • Schedule regular compliance scans
📊 Monitoring
  • Monitor SSM Agent health in CloudWatch
  • Set up alerts for failed commands
  • Track patch compliance dashboard
  • Review session logs weekly
🚀 Performance & Reliability
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   SYSTEMS MANAGER BEST PRACTICES FOR ANIHPJ              │
│                                                                          │
│  ✅ DO                                          ❌ DON'T                │
│  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────│
│                                                                          │
│  Use Session Manager instead of SSH            Hardcode secrets in code │
│  Tag all instances consistently                Open port 22 publicly    │
│  Enable CloudWatch logging                     Ignore patch compliance  │
│  Automate patching in maintenance windows      Patch during peak hours  │
│  Test patches in staging first                 Skip backup before patch │
│  Use --max-concurrency for zero downtime       Deploy to all servers    │
│  Store secrets in Parameter Store (encrypted)     simultaneously        │
│  Use IAM roles, not access keys                Store passwords in .env  │
│  Set up CloudTrail for audit                   Grant admin to everyone  │
│  Document runbooks and procedures              Manual configuration     │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 anihpj-Specific Recommendations:
  1. Start Simple: Begin with Session Manager + Parameter Store (both FREE)
  2. Add Patching: Set up Patch Manager for weekly Sunday 2 AM updates
  3. Deploy with Run Command: Create shell scripts for Django deployments
  4. Monitor Compliance: Check patch status dashboard weekly
  5. Secure Secrets: Move all passwords/keys from .env to Parameter Store
  6. Enable Logging: Send all sessions and commands to CloudWatch
  7. Automate Backups: Use Automation runbooks for RDS snapshots

🔗 10. Integration with Other AWS Services

AWS Service Integration Benefit for anihpj
EC2 SSM Agent pre-installed on Amazon Linux/Ubuntu AMIs Instant management without additional setup
CloudWatch Session logs, command output, compliance metrics Centralized monitoring and alerting
CloudFormation Create Parameter Store values, automation documents Infrastructure as Code for complete setup
IAM Fine-grained access control for all SSM operations Secure, auditable permission management
S3 Store Run Command output, session logs Long-term log retention and compliance
Lambda Trigger automation based on events Automated incident response workflows
EventBridge Trigger actions based on SSM events Automated patching notifications via Slack

🔧 11. Troubleshooting Common Issues

❌ Instance Not Showing in Fleet Manager
Problem: EC2 instance doesn't appear in Systems Manager Fleet Manager

Solutions:
  • ✅ Verify IAM role attached: AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore
  • ✅ Check SSM Agent is running: sudo systemctl status amazon-ssm-agent
  • ✅ Ensure instance has internet access or VPC endpoints for SSM
  • ✅ Wait 5-10 minutes after attaching IAM role
  • ✅ Check region: Instance and console must be in same region
❌ Parameter Store Access Denied
Problem: Django can't retrieve secrets from Parameter Store

Solutions:
  • ✅ Add SSM permissions to EC2 IAM role: ssm:GetParameter, ssm:GetParameters
  • ✅ For SecureString: Add KMS decrypt permission: kms:Decrypt
  • ✅ Check parameter name matches exactly (case-sensitive)
  • ✅ Verify parameter exists in correct region
❌ Run Command Timeout
Problem: Commands timing out before completion

Solutions:
  • ✅ Increase timeout: --timeout-seconds 600 (default is 3600)
  • ✅ Check for hung processes on instance
  • ✅ Break long commands into smaller steps
  • ✅ Use background jobs for long-running tasks
❌ Session Manager "Access Denied"
Problem: Can't start Session Manager session

Solutions:
  • ✅ Verify IAM user/role has ssm:StartSession permission
  • ✅ Check instance has correct IAM role attached
  • ✅ Ensure Session Manager plugin installed locally (AWS CLI)
  • ✅ Verify no restrictive security group rules blocking SSM endpoints

🐳 Container Services

AWS Container Services provide scalable, managed solutions for containerized applications. Deploy your Django anihpj job portal using modern containerization technologies with full orchestration, scaling, and management capabilities.

Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service)

Category: Containers, Compute  |  Use Case: Container Orchestration, Microservices, Web Applications  |  Pricing: Pay for EC2/Fargate Compute + Management (FREE)  |  Last Updated: January 2026  |  Documentation: AWS ECS Docs

Highly scalable, high-performance container orchestration service that supports Docker containers and allows you to run and scale containerized applications on AWS. Perfect for deploying your Django anihpj job portal as microservices with automatic scaling and load balancing.

📋 Overview & Introduction

Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) is a fully managed container orchestration service that helps you deploy, manage, and scale containerized applications. ECS eliminates the need for you to install, operate, and scale your own cluster management infrastructure.

🎯 Key Benefits
  • Fully Managed: No control plane to manage - AWS handles cluster management
  • Serverless Option: Run on AWS Fargate for serverless container execution
  • Integrated Security: VPC networking, IAM roles, CloudTrail integration
  • Auto Scaling: Automatic scaling based on demand
  • Load Balancing: Application Load Balancer integration for high availability
  • Cost Effective: Pay only for resources used, no management fees
  • Docker Compatible: Standard Docker containers and registries
💡 When to Use ECS for anihpj: Perfect when you want to containerize your Django job portal for consistent deployments across environments, need automatic scaling during traffic spikes (job posting deadlines), want to implement microservices architecture (separate user service, job service, notification service), or require zero-downtime deployments with rolling updates.
⚖️ ECS vs. Other Container & Compute Services
ECS: Managed container orchestration, AWS-native
EKS: Managed Kubernetes, more complex, more flexible
Fargate: Serverless containers, no server management
EC2: Full server control, traditional deployment
Lambda: Serverless functions, event-driven, short execution
Elastic Beanstalk: Platform-as-a-Service, less container control
🏗️ ECS Complete Architecture for anihpj Django Job Portal

🔧 Core Concepts & Components

Understanding ECS core concepts is essential for deploying your anihpj Django job portal effectively. Master these components to design a robust containerized architecture.

🏭 Clusters

A cluster is a logical grouping of tasks or services. ECS downloads your container images from a registry and runs those images within your cluster.

📊 Cluster Types Comparison for anihpj
Feature Fargate Cluster EC2 Cluster
Management ✅ Fully managed (serverless) ⚠️ You manage EC2 instances
Cost Model Pay per vCPU/memory per second Pay for EC2 instances (hourly)
Scaling ⚡ Instant (no provisioning) 🔄 Slower (EC2 launch time)
Cost at Low Traffic 💰 More expensive 💸 More cost-effective
Best For New projects, microservices, variable load Steady load, custom configs, cost optimization
anihpj Recommendation ✅ Start here - simpler operations 🔄 Migrate later for cost savings
🏗️ Cluster Architecture

│                    anihpj-production-cluster                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  FARGATE LAUNCH TYPE (Recommended for anihpj)                   │

│  │                                                          │   │

│  │  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐       │   │   │
│  │  │  │ Task 1   │  │ Task 2   │  │ Task 3   │       │   │   │
│  │  │  │Container │  │Container │  │Container │       │   │   │
│  │  │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘       │   │   │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │   │
│  │                                                          │   │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │   │
│  │  │         EC2 Instance (t3.large)                  │   │   │
│  │  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐                      │   │   │
│  │  │  │ Task 4   │  │ Task 5   │                      │   │   │
│  │  │  │Container │  │Container │                      │   │   │
│  │  │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘                      │   │   │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │   │
│  │                                                          │   │
│  │         You manage EC2 instances                         │   │
│  │         Better bin packing for cost savings              │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 Cluster Types
⚡

Fargate Cluster

  • Serverless compute for containers
  • No EC2 instances to manage
  • Pay per task execution
  • Perfect for anihpj MVP
🖥️

EC2 Cluster

  • Full control over EC2 instances
  • Custom instance types
  • Lower cost for sustained workloads
  • Good for production at scale
⚙️ Services

Services ensure that you have a desired number of tasks running at all times. If a task stops running, the service scheduler launches another instance based on the task definition.

🔄 Service Features for anihpj
Service Benefits:
✅ Auto-healing: Replaces failed containers
✅ Load balancing: Distributes traffic across tasks  
✅ Rolling deployments: Zero-downtime updates
✅ Auto scaling: Scale based on demand
✅ Service discovery: Route traffic to healthy instances

Example: anihpj-web-service
- Desired count: 3 tasks
- Load balancer: Application Load Balancer
- Health checks: Django /health/ endpoint
- Auto scaling: CPU > 70% → add tasks
📋 Tasks & Task Definitions

A task definition is a blueprint for your application. It specifies which Docker image to use, CPU/memory requirements, networking mode, logging configuration, and more. A task is the running instantiation of a task definition.

🔄 Task Lifecycle in ECS
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      ECS TASK LIFECYCLE                          │
│                    (anihpj Django Container)                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                 │
│  PHASE 1: PROVISIONING                                          │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  1. ECS Scheduler selects placement                       │  │
│  │     - Fargate: AWS selects compute                        │  │
│  │     - EC2: ECS selects optimal instance                   │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  2. Network Configuration                                 │  │
│  │     - ENI attached to task                                │  │
│  │     - Security groups applied                             │  │
│  │     - Private/Public IP assigned                          │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  ⏱️ Duration: 30-60 seconds                                │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                          │                                      │
│                          ▼                                      │
│  PHASE 2: PENDING                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  3. Pull container image from ECR                         │  │
│  │     docker pull 123456.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/  │  │
│  │                 anihpj:latest                             │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  4. Extract secrets from Secrets Manager                  │  │
│  │     - DATABASE_URL                                        │  │
│  │     - SECRET_KEY                                          │  │
│  │     - AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID                                   │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  ⏱️ Duration: 20-40 seconds (depends on image size)       │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                          │                                      │
│                          ▼                                      │
│  PHASE 3: RUNNING                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  5. Container starts                                       │  │
│  │     CMD: gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application                 │  │
│  │          --bind 0.0.0.0:8000                              │  │
│  │          --workers 4                                      │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  6. Health check begins                                   │  │
│  │     - Initial delay: 30 seconds                           │  │
│  │     - Check interval: 10 seconds                          │  │
│  │     - Path: /health/                                      │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  7. Task registered with ALB target group                 │  │
│  │     - Once health checks pass                             │  │
│  │     - Starts receiving traffic                            │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  8. Application serves requests                           │  │
│  │     - Job listings, applications, user auth               │  │
│  │     - CloudWatch logs streaming                           │  │
│  │     - Metrics published                                   │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  ⏱️ Duration: Minutes to hours/days                       │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                          │                                      │
│                          ▼                                      │
│  PHASE 4: STOPPING (Deployment or Scaling)                      │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  9. ECS sends SIGTERM to container                        │  │
│  │     - Django receives shutdown signal                     │  │
│  │     - Graceful shutdown begins                            │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  10. Deregistration from ALB                              │  │
│  │     - No new requests routed                              │  │
│  │     - Existing connections drain (30s default)            │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  11. Application cleanup                                  │  │
│  │     - Close DB connections                                │  │
│  │     - Complete in-flight requests                         │  │
│  │     - Save state if needed                                │  │
│  │                                                            │  │
│  │  ⏱️ Duration: 30 seconds (stopTimeout)                    │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                          │                                      │
│                          ▼                                      │
│  PHASE 5: STOPPED                                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  12. Container terminated                                  │  │
│  │  13. ENI released                                          │  │
│  │  14. Logs persisted to CloudWatch                         │  │
│  │  15. Task marked as STOPPED in ECS                        │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 anihpj Task Definition Structure
{
  "family": "anihpj-web",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "512",
  "memory": "1024",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "taskRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpjECSTaskRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "anihpj-django",
      "image": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2",
      "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000, "protocol": "tcp"}],
      "environment": [
        {"name": "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", "value": "anihpj.settings.production"},
        {"name": "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION", "value": "us-east-1"}
      ],
      "secrets": [
        {"name": "DATABASE_URL", "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/db-credentials"},
        {"name": "SECRET_KEY", "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/django-secret-key"}
      ]
    }
  ]
}
📦 Containers

Containers are the running instances of your Docker images within tasks. Each container definition in a task definition creates a container within the task.

🚀 Container Configuration Best Practices
🎯 Resource Allocation
  • CPU: 512 units (0.5 vCPU)
  • Memory: 1024 MB (1 GB)
  • Storage: 20 GB ephemeral
  • Network: awsvpc mode
🔍 Health Checks
  • Command: curl localhost:8000/health/
  • Interval: 30 seconds
  • Timeout: 5 seconds
  • Retries: 3 times
📦 Task Definition

Blueprint for running containers, similar to a Docker Compose file but for AWS

  • Container specifications: Image URI, CPU, memory
  • Network configuration: Port mappings, network mode
  • IAM roles: Permissions for your anihpj containers
  • Environment variables: Django settings, database connections
🏃 Task

Running instance of a task definition, like a pod in Kubernetes

  • Container runtime: Your Django app running in production
  • Resource allocation: CPU and memory assigned
  • Networking: Private IP, security groups
  • Health monitoring: Application health checks
📋 Service

Manages desired number of tasks with auto-healing and load balancing

  • Desired count: Number of anihpj instances to maintain
  • Auto scaling: Scale based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics
  • Service discovery: Internal DNS for microservices
  • Rolling deployments: Zero-downtime updates
🌐 Cluster

Logical grouping of compute resources where tasks run

  • Resource management: CPU and memory capacity
  • Launch types: EC2, Fargate, or External
  • Networking: VPC integration, subnets
  • Monitoring: CloudWatch container insights
📋 anihpj Django Task Definition Example
{
  "family": "anihpj-web",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "512",
  "memory": "1024",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "taskRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpjECSTaskRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "anihpj-django",
      "image": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2",
      "portMappings": [
        {
          "containerPort": 8000,
          "protocol": "tcp"
        }
      ],
      "environment": [
        {
          "name": "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE",
          "value": "anihpj.settings.production"
        },
        {
          "name": "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION",
          "value": "us-east-1"
        }
      ],
      "secrets": [
        {
          "name": "DATABASE_URL",
          "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/db-credentials"
        },
        {
          "name": "SECRET_KEY",
          "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/django-secret-key"
        }
      ],
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/anihpj-web",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
        }
      },
      "healthCheck": {
        "command": ["CMD-SHELL", "curl -f http://localhost:8000/health/ || exit 1"],
        "interval": 30,
        "timeout": 5,
        "retries": 3,
        "startPeriod": 60
      }
    }
  ]
}
⚖️ ECS Launch Types Comparison
Feature EC2 Launch Type Fargate Launch Type anihpj Recommendation
Server Management You manage EC2 instances AWS manages infrastructure Fargate for simplicity
Pricing Model Pay for EC2 instances Pay for vCPU/memory per second Fargate for predictable costs
Cost Efficiency Lower cost at scale Higher cost, no idle resources EC2 for high-traffic production
Scalability Manual cluster scaling Automatic scaling Fargate for dynamic workloads
Security You patch host OS AWS handles security patches Fargate for security automation

🚀 Getting Started Guide

Deploy your anihpj Django job portal to ECS with this comprehensive step-by-step guide. We'll containerize your application and deploy it to production-ready infrastructure.

🏭 Step 1: Set Up ECS Cluster

Create a cluster to logically group your containerized services. For anihpj, we'll start with Fargate for simplicity and zero server management.

🚀 Create Fargate Cluster for anihpj
# Step 1: Create ECS cluster using Fargate (serverless)
aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name anihpj-production-cluster \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --tags key=Environment,value=Production key=Application,value=anihpj

# Expected Output:
{
  "cluster": {
    "clusterArn": "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-production-cluster",
    "clusterName": "anihpj-production-cluster",
    "status": "ACTIVE",
    "registeredContainerInstancesCount": 0,
    "runningTasksCount": 0,
    "pendingTasksCount": 0,
    "activeServicesCount": 0
  }
}

# Step 2: Enable Container Insights for detailed monitoring
aws ecs update-cluster-settings \
  --cluster anihpj-production-cluster \
  --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled

# Step 3: Create CloudWatch log group for application logs
aws logs create-log-group --log-group-name /ecs/anihpj-web

# Step 4: Verify cluster status
aws ecs describe-clusters --clusters anihpj-production-cluster

# Step 5: Create VPC for ECS (if not existing)
aws ec2 create-vpc \
  --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=vpc,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-vpc}]'

# ✅ Cluster is now ready for service deployments!
📋 Create Task Definition
📝 Register anihpj Task Definition
# Create task definition file: anihpj-task-definition.json
{
  "family": "anihpj-web",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "512",
  "memory": "1024",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "anihpj-django",
      "image": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest",
      "portMappings": [{"containerPort": 8000}],
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/anihpj-web",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

# Register the task definition
aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json file://anihpj-task-definition.json
⚙️ Deploy Service
🚀 Create ECS Service
# Create ECS Service with 3 replicas
aws ecs create-service \
  --cluster anihpj-production-cluster \
  --service-name anihpj-web-service \
  --task-definition anihpj-web:1 \
  --desired-count 3 \
  --launch-type FARGATE \
  --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-12345,subnet-67890],securityGroups=[sg-abcdef],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}"

# Check service status
aws ecs describe-services \
  --cluster anihpj-production-cluster \
  --services anihpj-web-service
🔄 Configure Load Balancer
⚖️ Application Load Balancer Setup
# Create Application Load Balancer
aws elbv2 create-load-balancer \
  --name anihpj-alb \
  --subnets subnet-12345 subnet-67890 \
  --security-groups sg-alb-security

# Create target group for Django app
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name anihpj-web-tg \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 8000 \
  --vpc-id vpc-12345 \
  --target-type ip \
  --health-check-path /health/

# Create listener for ALB
aws elbv2 create-listener \
  --load-balancer-arn arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:loadbalancer/app/anihpj-alb/1234567890123456 \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 80 \
  --default-actions Type=forward,TargetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-web-tg/1234567890123456
📈 Set Up Auto Scaling
📊 Configure Auto Scaling Policies
# Register scalable target
aws application-autoscaling register-scalable-target \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --resource-id service/anihpj-production-cluster/anihpj-web-service \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --min-capacity 2 \
  --max-capacity 10

# Create CPU-based scaling policy
aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --resource-id service/anihpj-production-cluster/anihpj-web-service \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --policy-name anihpj-cpu-scaling \
  --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \
  --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration file://cpu-scaling-policy.json

# cpu-scaling-policy.json
{
  "TargetValue": 70.0,
  "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
    "PredefinedMetricType": "ECSServiceAverageCPUUtilization"
  }
}
Step 1: 📦 Containerize Your Django Application

Create Docker configuration for your anihpj Django job portal

📁 Dockerfile 
# anihpj Django Job Portal Dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE 1
ENV PYTHONUNBUFFERED 1
ENV DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE anihpj.settings.production

# Set work directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install system dependencies
RUN apt-get update \\
    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \\
        postgresql-client \\
        gettext \\
        curl \\
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install Python dependencies
COPY requirements.txt /app/
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy project
COPY . /app/

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Create non-root user
RUN adduser --disabled-password --gecos '' anihpj
RUN chown -R anihpj:anihpj /app
USER anihpj

# Health check
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \\
    CMD curl -f http://localhost:8000/health/ || exit 1

# Expose port
EXPOSE 8000

# Start server
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "3", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]
📁 docker-compose.yml (for local development) 
version: '3.8'

services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DEBUG=False
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://anihpj:password@db:5432/anihpj_db
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
    volumes:
      - ./:/app
      - media_volume:/app/media

  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: anihpj_db
      POSTGRES_USER: anihpj
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    
  worker:
    build: .
    command: celery -A anihpj worker -l info
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgresql://anihpj:password@db:5432/anihpj_db
      - REDIS_URL=redis://redis:6379/0
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis
    volumes:
      - ./:/app

volumes:
  postgres_data:
  media_volume:
Step 2: 📁 Build and Push to ECR

Store your anihpj container images in AWS Elastic Container Registry

AWS ECR - Create Repository
Repository name: anihpj
Visibility: Private
Tag immutability: Enabled
Scan on push: Enabled
Encryption: AES-256
🔧 ECR Commands 
# 1. Create ECR repository
aws ecr create-repository --repository-name anihpj --region us-east-1

# 2. Get login token
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# 3. Build your image
docker build -t anihpj:latest .

# 4. Tag your image
docker tag anihpj:latest 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2

# 5. Push the image
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2

# 6. Tag as latest
docker tag anihpj:latest 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest
Step 3: 🌐 Create ECS Cluster

Set up the compute environment for your anihpj containers

ECS Console - Create Cluster
Environment: production
Project: anihpj
Application: job-portal
🔧 AWS CLI Command 
# Create ECS Cluster
aws ecs create-cluster \\
    --cluster-name anihpj-production-cluster \\
    --capacity-providers FARGATE \\
    --default-capacity-provider-strategy capacityProvider=FARGATE,weight=1 \\
    --tags key=Environment,value=production key=Project,value=anihpj
Step 4: 📋 Create Task Definition

Define how your anihpj Django containers should run

📁 anihpj-task-definition.json 
{
  "family": "anihpj-web",
  "networkMode": "awsvpc",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "512",
  "memory": "1024",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "taskRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpjECSTaskRole",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "anihpj-django",
      "image": "123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest",
      "portMappings": [
        {
          "containerPort": 8000,
          "protocol": "tcp"
        }
      ],
      "environment": [
        {
          "name": "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE",
          "value": "anihpj.settings.production"
        },
        {
          "name": "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION",
          "value": "us-east-1"
        },
        {
          "name": "ALLOWED_HOSTS",
          "value": "anihpj.com,*.anihpj.com,*.elb.amazonaws.com"
        }
      ],
      "secrets": [
        {
          "name": "DATABASE_URL",
          "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/db-url"
        },
        {
          "name": "SECRET_KEY",
          "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/secret-key"
        },
        {
          "name": "EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD",
          "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/email-password"
        }
      ],
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/anihpj-web",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs",
          "awslogs-create-group": "true"
        }
      },
      "healthCheck": {
        "command": ["CMD-SHELL", "curl -f http://localhost:8000/health/ || exit 1"],
        "interval": 30,
        "timeout": 10,
        "retries": 3,
        "startPeriod": 60
      }
    }
  ]
}
🔧 Register Task Definition 
# Register the task definition
aws ecs register-task-definition --cli-input-json file://anihpj-task-definition.json

# Verify registration
aws ecs describe-task-definition --task-definition anihpj-web:1
Step 5: 🚀 Create ECS Service

Deploy and maintain your anihpj Django application with auto-scaling

ECS Console - Create Service
Environment
Compute environment: AWS Fargate
Platform version: LATEST
Deployment configuration
Application type: Service
Task definition: anihpj-web:1
Service name: anihpj-web-service
Desired tasks: 3
Networking
VPC: vpc-12345678
Subnets: Private subnets (2 selected)
Security groups: anihpj-ecs-sg
Public IP: DISABLED
Load balancing
Load balancer type: Application Load Balancer
Load balancer: anihpj-alb
Target group: anihpj-web-tg
Health check path: /health/
🔧 Create Service Command 
# Create ECS Service with Load Balancer
aws ecs create-service \\
    --cluster anihpj-production-cluster \\
    --service-name anihpj-web-service \\
    --task-definition anihpj-web:1 \\
    --desired-count 3 \\
    --launch-type FARGATE \\
    --network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[subnet-12345,subnet-67890],securityGroups=[sg-abcdef],assignPublicIp=DISABLED}" \\
    --load-balancers "targetGroupArn=arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:us-east-1:123456789012:targetgroup/anihpj-web-tg/1234567890123456,containerName=anihpj-django,containerPort=8000" \\
    --health-check-grace-period-seconds 300
Step 6: ⚙️ Configure Auto Scaling

Set up automatic scaling for your anihpj service based on demand

🔧 Auto Scaling Configuration 
# 1. Register scalable target
aws application-autoscaling register-scalable-target \\
    --service-namespace ecs \\
    --resource-id service/anihpj-production-cluster/anihpj-web-service \\
    --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \\
    --min-capacity 2 \\
    --max-capacity 10

# 2. Create scaling policy for CPU
aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \\
    --service-namespace ecs \\
    --resource-id service/anihpj-production-cluster/anihpj-web-service \\
    --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \\
    --policy-name anihpj-cpu-scaling \\
    --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \\
    --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration '{
        "TargetValue": 70.0,
        "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
            "PredefinedMetricType": "ECSServiceAverageCPUUtilization"
        },
        "ScaleOutCooldown": 300,
        "ScaleInCooldown": 300
    }'

# 3. Create scaling policy for Memory
aws application-autoscaling put-scaling-policy \\
    --service-namespace ecs \\
    --resource-id service/anihpj-production-cluster/anihpj-web-service \\
    --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \\
    --policy-name anihpj-memory-scaling \\
    --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \\
    --target-tracking-scaling-policy-configuration '{
        "TargetValue": 80.0,
        "PredefinedMetricSpecification": {
            "PredefinedMetricType": "ECSServiceAverageMemoryUtilization"
        },
        "ScaleOutCooldown": 300,
        "ScaleInCooldown": 300
    }'
🎉 Congratulations! Your anihpj Django job portal is now running on ECS with auto-scaling, load balancing, and health monitoring. Monitor your deployment in the ECS console and CloudWatch for optimal performance.

💰 Pricing & Cost Optimization

Understand ECS pricing models and optimize costs for your anihpj Django job portal. Make informed decisions about resource allocation and scaling strategies.

💸 Pricing Models
🆓 ECS Management
FREE

No additional charges for ECS cluster management and orchestration

  • Container orchestration
  • Service discovery
  • Load balancer integration
  • Auto scaling
🖥️ EC2 Launch Type
Pay for EC2 instances

Standard EC2 pricing for underlying infrastructure

  • t3.medium: ~$30/month
  • m5.large: ~$70/month
  • c5.xlarge: ~$140/month
  • Reserved Instances: 40-60% savings
☁️ Fargate Launch Type
Pay per vCPU/GB-second

Per-second billing for containers without managing servers

  • vCPU: $0.04048 per hour
  • Memory: $0.004445 per GB-hour
  • No idle capacity charges
  • Automatic scaling
🧮 anihpj Cost Calculator
💼 Production Scenario
Application: anihpj Django Job Portal
Traffic: 10,000 daily active users
Architecture: 3 web containers + 2 workers
Resources: 0.5 vCPU, 1GB memory per container
Monthly Cost Breakdown (Fargate)
Web Containers (3 × 0.5 vCPU) $88.70
Web Memory (3 × 1GB) $9.76
Worker Containers (2 × 0.5 vCPU) $59.13
Worker Memory (2 × 1GB) $6.50
Application Load Balancer $22.50
Total ECS Monthly Cost $186.59
🚀 Startup Scenario
Application: anihpj MVP
Traffic: 1,000 daily active users
Architecture: 1 web container + 1 worker
Resources: 0.25 vCPU, 0.5GB memory per container
Monthly Cost Breakdown (Fargate)
Web Container (1 × 0.25 vCPU) $14.78
Web Memory (1 × 0.5GB) $1.63
Worker Container (1 × 0.25 vCPU) $14.78
Worker Memory (1 × 0.5GB) $1.63
Application Load Balancer $22.50
Total ECS Monthly Cost $55.32
🧮 Cost Scenarios

Typical cost scenarios for anihpj Django job portal on ECS:

Scenario Configuration Monthly Cost
Startup MVP 1 web (0.5 vCPU, 1GB) + 1 worker $45/month
Small Business 2 web (1 vCPU, 2GB) + 2 workers $187/month
Production Scale 5 web (2 vCPU, 4GB) + 3 workers + ALB $650/month
⚡ Cost Optimization Strategies
🎯 Right-sizing Resources
  • Monitor metrics: Use CloudWatch to track CPU/memory usage
  • Start small: Begin with 0.25 vCPU, 0.5GB for anihpj MVP
  • Scale gradually: Increase resources based on actual demand
  • Review monthly: Adjust task definitions based on usage patterns
📊 Auto Scaling Configuration
  • Target tracking: Set CPU target at 70% for optimal performance
  • Schedule scaling: Scale up during peak hours (9 AM - 6 PM)
  • Minimum capacity: Set minimum to 1 for development, 2 for production
  • Maximum capacity: Prevent cost spikes with reasonable maximums
🔄 Deployment Strategies
  • Blue/Green: Zero downtime but 2x cost during deployment
  • Rolling update: Cost-effective with minimal downtime
  • Spot capacity: Use EC2 Spot for development environments
  • Dev/Test shutdown: Schedule non-production environments off-hours
💾 Storage & Networking
  • Ephemeral storage: Free 20GB per Fargate task
  • EFS optimization: Use EFS Infrequent Access for logs
  • Data transfer: Keep containers and databases in same AZ
  • NAT Gateway: Use VPC endpoints for AWS services
📈 Fargate vs EC2 Cost Analysis for anihpj
Scenario Fargate Monthly EC2 Monthly Break-even Point Recommendation
MVP (Low traffic) $55 $85 N/A ✅ Fargate
Production (Medium) $186 $140 ~70% utilization ⚖️ Consider EC2
High Scale $500+ $280 50% utilization ✅ EC2 with Reserved Instances
Variable workloads Variable Fixed N/A ✅ Fargate for flexibility

🔒 Security & Best Practices

Implement comprehensive security measures for your anihpj Django job portal on ECS. Follow AWS security best practices to protect your containerized applications.

✅ Security Checklist
🛡️ Container Security
🔐 Access Control
🌐 Network Security
📊 Monitoring & Compliance
🔐 anihpj Security Configuration
# ECS Task Role Policy for anihpj Django
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-static-files/*",
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-user-uploads/*"
      ]
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:*:secret:anihpj/*"
      ]
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "sqs:SendMessage",
        "sqs:ReceiveMessage",
        "sqs:DeleteMessage"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:sqs:us-east-1:*:anihpj-*"
      ]
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ses:SendEmail",
        "ses:SendRawEmail"
      ],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "ses:FromAddress": "noreply@anihpj.com"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
🔐 Security Configuration
🛡️ IAM Task Role for anihpj
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:*:*:secret:anihpj/*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ses:SendEmail",
        "ses:SendRawEmail"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
🚨 Security Monitoring
📊 CloudWatch Alarms for Security Events
# Create alarm for task failures
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name anihpj-ecs-task-failures \
  --alarm-description "Alert on ECS task failures" \
  --metric-name TaskFailed \
  --namespace AWS/ECS \
  --statistic Sum \
  --period 300 \
  --threshold 3 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --evaluation-periods 1 \
  --dimensions Name=ServiceName,Value=anihpj-web-service

# Create alarm for high CPU usage
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name anihpj-ecs-high-cpu \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --namespace AWS/ECS \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --threshold 80 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --evaluation-periods 2

🔗 Integrations & Ecosystem

⚖️ Application Load Balancer Integration

Distribute traffic across ECS tasks with Application Load Balancer:

# Create target group for ECS service
aws elbv2 create-target-group \
  --name anihpj-web-tg \
  --protocol HTTP \
  --port 8000 \
  --vpc-id vpc-12345 \
  --target-type ip \
  --health-check-path /health/ \
  --health-check-interval-seconds 30 \
  --health-check-timeout-seconds 5 \
  --healthy-threshold-count 2 \
  --unhealthy-threshold-count 3
📦 Amazon ECR Integration

Store and manage container images in Amazon Elastic Container Registry:

# Create ECR repository
aws ecr create-repository --repository-name anihpj

# Login to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Build and push image
docker build -t anihpj .
docker tag anihpj:latest 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:latest
📊 Amazon CloudWatch Integration

Monitor ECS tasks and services with CloudWatch metrics and logs:

# View ECS service metrics
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace AWS/ECS \
  --metric-name CPUUtilization \
  --dimensions Name=ServiceName,Value=anihpj-web-service \
  --start-time 2026-01-13T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2026-01-14T00:00:00Z \
  --period 3600 \
  --statistics Average
🔐 AWS Secrets Manager Integration

Securely store and retrieve secrets in ECS tasks:

# Create secret
aws secretsmanager create-secret \
  --name anihpj/db-credentials \
  --secret-string '{"username":"anihpj_user","password":"SecureP@ss123","host":"anihpj-db.cluster-xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com"}'

# Reference in task definition
"secrets": [
  {
    "name": "DATABASE_URL",
    "valueFrom": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/db-credentials"
  }
]
🌐 Amazon VPC Integration

Deploy ECS tasks in your VPC for network isolation:

# Network configuration for ECS service
"networkConfiguration": {
  "awsvpcConfiguration": {
    "subnets": ["subnet-12345", "subnet-67890"],
    "securityGroups": ["sg-anihpj-ecs"],
    "assignPublicIp": "DISABLED"
  }
}

⌨️ API & CLI Reference

🔧 Essential CLI Commands
# List clusters
aws ecs list-clusters

# Describe service
aws ecs describe-services --cluster anihpj-production-cluster --services anihpj-web-service

# List tasks
aws ecs list-tasks --cluster anihpj-production-cluster --service-name anihpj-web-service

# Update service
aws ecs update-service --cluster anihpj-production-cluster --service anihpj-web-service --desired-count 5

# View task logs
aws logs tail /ecs/anihpj-web --follow
🐍 Python SDK (Boto3) Examples
import boto3

ecs = boto3.client('ecs', region_name='us-east-1')

# List services in cluster
def list_services(cluster_name):
    response = ecs.list_services(cluster=cluster_name)
    return response['serviceArns']

# Get service details
def get_service_info(cluster, service):
    response = ecs.describe_services(
        cluster=cluster,
        services=[service]
    )
    return response['services'][0]

# Update service task count
def scale_service(cluster, service, desired_count):
    response = ecs.update_service(
        cluster=cluster,
        service=service,
        desiredCount=desired_count
    )
    return response['service']

# Example usage
services = list_services('anihpj-production-cluster')
info = get_service_info('anihpj-production-cluster', 'anihpj-web-service')
print(f"Current tasks: {info['runningCount']}")

# Scale to 5 tasks
scale_service('anihpj-production-cluster', 'anihpj-web-service', 5)
🏆 Implementation Best Practices
  • ✅ Use Fargate for simpler operations and serverless benefits
  • ✅ Implement health checks for all containers
  • ✅ Use secrets manager for sensitive data
  • ✅ Enable container insights for detailed monitoring
  • ✅ Implement proper logging with CloudWatch
  • ✅ Use ALB for traffic distribution
  • ✅ Configure auto-scaling policies
  • ✅ Regular security scanning of container images
  • ✅ Tag all resources for cost allocation
  • ✅ Use multiple availability zones for high availability

Amazon ECS provides a powerful, scalable platform for running your containerized Django job portal with minimal operational overhead.
