🚀 Next Steps for anihpj Deployment
  1. Containerize your Django app: Create Dockerfile for anihpj
  2. Push to ECR: Store container images in Amazon ECR
  3. Create task definition: Define CPU, memory, environment variables
  4. Set up ALB: Configure load balancer for traffic distribution
  5. Deploy service: Launch ECS service with desired task count
  6. Configure auto-scaling: Set scaling policies based on CPU/memory
  7. Monitor with CloudWatch: Track metrics and set up alarms
  8. Implement CI/CD: Use CodePipeline for automated deployments

Pro Tip: Start with Fargate for simplicity, then consider EC2 launch type once you understand your workload patterns for potential cost optimization. 💡

Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service)

Category: Containers, Compute, Orchestration  |  Use Case: Kubernetes Orchestration, Microservices, Cloud-Native Apps  |  Pricing: $0.10/hour per cluster + EC2/Fargate compute costs  |  Last Updated: January 2026  |  Documentation: AWS EKS Docs

Fully managed Kubernetes service that makes it easy to run Kubernetes on AWS without needing to install, operate, and maintain your own Kubernetes control plane. Perfect for deploying your anihpj Django job portal using industry-standard Kubernetes with AWS integration, automatic updates, and enterprise-grade security.

📋 Overview & Introduction

Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) is a managed Kubernetes service that eliminates the complexity of operating Kubernetes at scale. EKS automatically manages the availability and scalability of the Kubernetes control plane nodes responsible for scheduling containers, managing application availability, storing cluster data, and other key tasks.

🎯 Key Benefits
🎛️

Fully Managed Control Plane

AWS manages Kubernetes masters across multiple AZs

  • 99.95% SLA uptime
  • Multi-AZ deployment
  • Automatic upgrades
☸️

Certified Kubernetes

Run standard Kubernetes applications without modifications

  • CNCF certified
  • Full K8s API support
  • Compatible with K8s tools
🔄

Automatic Updates

Kubernetes version updates managed by AWS

  • Zero-downtime updates
  • Patch management
  • Version lifecycle support
⚡

High Availability

Control plane spans 3 availability zones automatically

  • Multi-AZ masters
  • Automatic failover
  • 99.95% availability SLA
🔗

AWS Integration

Native integration with AWS services

  • IAM authentication
  • VPC networking
  • ELB, CloudWatch, ECR
🔒

Enterprise Security

Encrypted control plane with IAM integration

  • Envelope encryption
  • VPC network isolation
  • Private endpoint support
🚀

Deployment Flexibility

Run on EC2, Fargate, or on-premises

  • EC2 worker nodes
  • Fargate serverless pods
  • EKS Anywhere (on-prem)
🌐

Rich Ecosystem

Full Kubernetes ecosystem support

  • Helm charts
  • Operators & CRDs
  • Service meshes (Istio)
💡 When to Use EKS for anihpj: Perfect when you need full Kubernetes capabilities for complex microservices architecture, want portability across cloud providers, need advanced orchestration features (StatefulSets, DaemonSets, CRDs), require compatibility with existing Kubernetes tools and ecosystem, or plan to implement GitOps workflows with tools like ArgoCD or Flux.
⚖️ EKS vs. Other Container & Compute Services
Feature EKS ECS Fargate EC2
Orchestration Kubernetes (industry standard) AWS proprietary N/A (serverless) Manual/custom
Complexity ⚠️ High (Kubernetes learning curve) ✅ Medium (simpler) ✅ Low (serverless) ⚠️ Variable
Portability ✅ Excellent (multi-cloud) ⚠️ AWS-only ⚠️ AWS-only ✅ Good
Management Overhead Medium (nodes only) Low (AWS managed) None (serverless) High (full management)
Ecosystem ✅ Massive (K8s ecosystem) Limited (AWS tools) Limited Variable
Cost $0.10/hr cluster + compute FREE management + compute Pay per vCPU/memory Instance costs only
Best For anihpj Complex microservices, K8s expertise Simple containers, AWS-native Serverless containers Full control needed
🏗️ EKS Complete Architecture for anihpj Django Job Portal
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          AWS EKS ECOSYSTEM ARCHITECTURE                             │
│                           anihpj Kubernetes Job Portal                              │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                              🌐 INGRESS & ROUTING LAYER                             │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                    │
│  👨‍💻 Users                    🔗 Route 53            📱 CloudFront CDN              │
│  │                           │                       │                             │
│  │ anihpj.com               │ DNS routing            │ Global edge cache          │
│  │ api.anihpj.com           │ Health checks          │ Static assets              │
│  └───────────────────────────┴───────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┘
│                                       │                                             │
│                                       ▼                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                    ⚖️ AWS LOAD BALANCER (ALB)                                │  │
│  │                   Managed by AWS Load Balancer Controller                    │  │
│  │                                                                             │  │
│  │  Ingress Rules:                                                             │  │
│  │  ├─ anihpj.com/*          → anihpj-web-service:8000                        │  │
│  │  ├─ api.anihpj.com/*      → anihpj-api-service:8001                        │  │
│  │  └─ admin.anihpj.com/*    → anihpj-admin-service:8002                      │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                       │                                             │
│                                       ▼                                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                          ☸️ AWS EKS CONTROL PLANE                                   │
│                       (Fully Managed by AWS - Multi-AZ)                            │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                    │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  🎛️ Kubernetes Control Plane Components (AWS Managed)                    │    │
│  │                                                                          │    │
│  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐    │    │
│  │  │   etcd      │  │ API Server  │  │  Scheduler  │  │  Controller │    │    │
│  │  │   (AZ-A)    │  │   (AZ-A)    │  │   (AZ-B)    │  │  Manager    │    │    │
│  │  │             │  │             │  │             │  │   (AZ-C)    │    │    │
│  │  │ Cluster     │  │ kubectl     │  │ Pod         │  │ Node        │    │    │
│  │  │ state       │  │ endpoint    │  │ placement   │  │ lifecycle   │    │    │
│  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘    │    │
│  │                                                                          │    │
│  │  📊 Kubernetes Version: 1.28 (auto-updated by AWS)                       │    │
│  │  🔒 Private API endpoint (VPC only) + Public endpoint (optional)         │    │
│  │  🔐 IAM authentication via aws-iam-authenticator                         │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                       │                                           │
│                                       ▼                                           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         🖥️ EKS WORKER NODES (Data Plane)                           │
│                              anihpj-node-group                                     │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                    │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                     MANAGED NODE GROUP (EC2)                              │    │
│  │                     (Auto Scaling Group: 2-10 nodes)                      │    │
│  │                                                                          │    │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │    │
│  │  │  Node 1: t3.large (AZ-A)                                         │    │    │
│  │  │  ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Pod: anihpj-web│ │ Pod: anihpj-web│ │ Pod: anihpj-api│      │    │    │
│  │  │  │                │ │                │ │                │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Container:     │ │ Container:     │ │ Container:     │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ anihpj:v1.2    │ │ anihpj:v1.2    │ │ anihpj-api:v1.0│      │    │    │
│  │  │  │                │ │                │ │                │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Django 5.0     │ │ Django 5.0     │ │ FastAPI        │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Gunicorn       │ │ Gunicorn       │ │ Uvicorn        │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ CPU: 500m      │ │ CPU: 500m      │ │ CPU: 250m      │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Memory: 1Gi    │ │ Memory: 1Gi    │ │ Memory: 512Mi  │      │    │    │
│  │  │  └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘      │    │    │
│  │  │                                                                 │    │    │
│  │  │  Kubelet │ kube-proxy │ Container Runtime (containerd)         │    │    │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │    │
│  │                                                                          │    │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │    │
│  │  │  Node 2: t3.large (AZ-B)                                         │    │    │
│  │  │  ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Pod: anihpj-web│ │Pod: anihpj-work│ │Pod: anihpj-work│      │    │    │
│  │  │  │                │ │                │ │                │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Container:     │ │ Container:     │ │ Container:     │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ anihpj:v1.2    │ │ anihpj-worker  │ │ anihpj-worker  │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │                │ │                │ │                │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Django 5.0     │ │ Celery Worker  │ │ Celery Worker  │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Gunicorn       │ │ Job processing │ │ Email tasks    │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ CPU: 500m      │ │ CPU: 250m      │ │ CPU: 250m      │      │    │    │
│  │  │  │ Memory: 1Gi    │ │ Memory: 512Mi  │ │ Memory: 512Mi  │      │    │    │
│  │  │  └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘      │    │    │
│  │  │                                                                 │    │    │
│  │  │  Kubelet │ kube-proxy │ Container Runtime (containerd)         │    │    │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │    │
│  │                                                                          │    │
│  │  Instance Type: t3.large (2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM)                             │    │
│  │  AMI: Amazon EKS Optimized Amazon Linux 2                               │    │
│  │  Security Group: eks-node-sg (22, 443, 10250, 1025-65535)              │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                                  │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │                     FARGATE PROFILES (Serverless Pods)                    │    │
│  │                                                                          │    │
│  │  ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐                                  │    │
│  │  │ Fargate Pod 1  │ │ Fargate Pod 2  │  (No EC2 management)            │    │
│  │  │ Namespace: jobs│ │ Namespace: jobs│                                  │    │
│  │  │ anihpj-cron    │ │ anihpj-cron    │                                  │    │
│  │  └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘                                  │    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                           📊 SUPPORTING AWS SERVICES                                │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                    │
│  📦 Amazon ECR                    💾 Amazon RDS                                    │
│  ├─ anihpj/web:v1.2              ├─ PostgreSQL 15                                 │
│  ├─ anihpj/api:v1.0              ├─ Multi-AZ deployment                           │
│  └─ anihpj/worker:v1.1           └─ Automated backups                             │
│                                                                                    │
│  📁 Amazon EFS                    🔐 AWS Secrets Manager                           │
│  ├─ Persistent storage           ├─ Database credentials                          │
│  ├─ Shared across pods           ├─ API keys                                      │
│  └─ Auto-scaling                 └─ Django SECRET_KEY                             │
│                                                                                    │
│  📊 CloudWatch                    🔗 AWS VPC                                       │
│  ├─ Container Insights           ├─ Private subnets (nodes)                       │
│  ├─ Application logs             ├─ Public subnets (ALB)                          │
│  └─ Cluster metrics              └─ Security groups                               │
│                                                                                    │
│  📨 Amazon SQS                    🔐 IAM Roles                                     │
│  ├─ Job processing queues        ├─ EKS cluster role                              │
│  └─ Email task queues            ├─ EKS node role                                 │
│                                  └─ Pod service accounts (IRSA)                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔧 Core Concepts & Components

Understanding EKS and Kubernetes core concepts is essential for deploying your anihpj Django job portal effectively. Master these components to design a robust, scalable architecture.

🎛️ Control Plane

The EKS control plane is fully managed by AWS and runs across multiple availability zones for high availability. It consists of Kubernetes master components that manage the cluster.

🏗️ Control Plane Components
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           EKS CONTROL PLANE ARCHITECTURE                     │
│              (AWS Managed - Multi-AZ)                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Availability Zone A                                    │ │
│  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │ │
│  │  │     etcd     │  │  API Server  │  │  Scheduler   │ │ │
│  │  │  (Primary)   │  │              │  │              │ │ │
│  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘ │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Availability Zone B                                    │ │
│  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │ │
│  │  │     etcd     │  │  API Server  │  │ Controller   │ │ │
│  │  │  (Replica)   │  │              │  │  Manager     │ │ │
│  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘ │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Availability Zone C                                    │ │
│  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐ │ │
│  │  │     etcd     │  │  API Server  │  │ Cloud        │ │ │
│  │  │  (Replica)   │  │              │  │ Controller   │ │ │
│  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘ │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                              │
│  💰 Cost: $0.10 per hour per cluster                        │
│  🔒 Security: Automatic encryption, IAM integration         │
│  🔄 Updates: Managed by AWS (manual approval required)      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🗄️

etcd

Distributed key-value store for all cluster data

  • Stores cluster state
  • Replicated across 3 AZs
  • Automatic backups by AWS
🌐

API Server

Frontend for Kubernetes control plane

  • kubectl command endpoint
  • IAM authentication
  • RBAC authorization
📅

Scheduler

Assigns pods to nodes

  • Resource-based placement
  • Affinity/anti-affinity rules
  • Taints and tolerations
🎮

Controller Manager

Runs controller processes

  • Node lifecycle
  • Replication management
  • Service accounts
🖥️ Node Groups

Node groups are collections of EC2 instances that run your containerized applications. EKS supports managed node groups, self-managed nodes, and Fargate profiles.

📊 Node Group Types Comparison
Feature Managed Node Groups Self-Managed Nodes Fargate Profiles
Management ✅ AWS managed ⚠️ You manage ✅ Fully serverless
Updates One-click node updates Manual updates Automatic
Cost EC2 instance costs EC2 instance costs Per vCPU/memory/sec
Scaling Auto Scaling Groups Manual/custom ASG Automatic
Customization Limited (launch templates) Full control None
Best For anihpj ✅ Recommended (simplicity) Advanced customization Specific workloads
📦 Pods & Deployments

Pods are the smallest deployable units in Kubernetes, containing one or more containers. Deployments manage the lifecycle of pod replicas.

📋 anihpj Deployment Example
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      component: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        component: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: database-url
        - name: SECRET_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: django-secret-key
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /ready/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 5
        volumeMounts:
        - name: static-files
          mountPath: /app/static
      volumes:
      - name: static-files
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: anihpj-static-pvc
🌐 Services & Networking

Kubernetes Services provide stable networking endpoints for pod groups, enabling service discovery and load balancing.

🔵

ClusterIP

Internal cluster communication

  • Default service type
  • Internal DNS name
  • Pod-to-pod communication
🟢

NodePort

External access via node IP

  • Opens port on all nodes
  • Port range: 30000-32767
  • Development/testing
⚖️

LoadBalancer

AWS ELB/ALB integration

  • Creates AWS Load Balancer
  • Public IP address
  • Production workloads
🔗

Ingress

HTTP/HTTPS routing rules

  • Path-based routing
  • TLS termination
  • Single ALB for multiple services

🚀 Getting Started Guide

Deploy your anihpj Django job portal on EKS with these step-by-step instructions. We'll create a production-ready cluster with managed node groups.

🏭 Step 1: Create EKS Cluster

Create an EKS cluster using eksctl (the official CLI tool) or AWS Console.

🚀 Create Cluster with eksctl
# Install eksctl (if not already installed)
# macOS
brew install eksctl

# Linux
curl --silent --location "https://github.com/weaveworks/eksctl/releases/latest/download/eksctl_Linux_amd64.tar.gz" | tar xz -C /tmp
sudo mv /tmp/eksctl /usr/local/bin

# Create cluster configuration file
cat <<EOF > anihpj-cluster.yaml
apiVersion: eksctl.io/v1alpha5
kind: ClusterConfig

metadata:
  name: anihpj-production
  region: us-east-1
  version: "1.28"

vpc:
  cidr: 10.0.0.0/16
  nat:
    gateway: HighlyAvailable

managedNodeGroups:
  - name: anihpj-node-group
    instanceType: t3.large
    desiredCapacity: 2
    minSize: 2
    maxSize: 10
    privateNetworking: true
    labels:
      role: worker
      environment: production
    tags:
      Application: anihpj
      Environment: production
    iam:
      withAddonPolicies:
        autoScaler: true
        cloudWatch: true
        ebs: true
        efs: true
        albIngress: true

addons:
  - name: vpc-cni
  - name: coredns
  - name: kube-proxy
  - name: aws-ebs-csi-driver

cloudWatch:
  clusterLogging:
    enableTypes: ["api", "audit", "authenticator", "controllerManager", "scheduler"]
EOF

# Create the cluster (takes 15-20 minutes)
eksctl create cluster -f anihpj-cluster.yaml

# Expected Output:
# [ℹ]  eksctl version 0.167.0
# [ℹ]  using region us-east-1
# [ℹ]  setting availability zones to [us-east-1a us-east-1b us-east-1c]
# [ℹ]  subnets for us-east-1a - public:10.0.0.0/19 private:10.0.96.0/19
# [ℹ]  subnets for us-east-1b - public:10.0.32.0/19 private:10.0.128.0/19
# [ℹ]  subnets for us-east-1c - public:10.0.64.0/19 private:10.0.160.0/19
# [✔]  EKS cluster "anihpj-production" in "us-east-1" region is ready
⚙️ Step 2: Configure kubectl

Configure kubectl to communicate with your EKS cluster.

# Update kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig --region us-east-1 --name anihpj-production

# Expected Output:
# Added new context arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/anihpj-production to /home/user/.kube/config

# Verify connection
kubectl get nodes

# Expected Output:
# NAME                              STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
# ip-10-0-96-123.ec2.internal      Ready       5m    v1.28.5-eks-5e0fdde
# ip-10-0-128-45.ec2.internal      Ready       5m    v1.28.5-eks-5e0fdde

# Check cluster info
kubectl cluster-info

# Create namespace for anihpj
kubectl create namespace production
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=production
🚀 Step 3: Deploy Application

Deploy the anihpj Django application to EKS using Kubernetes manifests.

📦 Create Deployment
# Create secrets for sensitive data
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secrets \
  --from-literal=database-url='postgresql://user:pass@anihpj-db.xxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com/anihpj' \
  --from-literal=django-secret-key='your-secret-key-here' \
  --from-literal=aws-access-key-id='AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE' \
  --from-literal=aws-secret-access-key='wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY' \
  -n production

# Create deployment
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      component: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        component: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: database-url
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health/
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
EOF

# Check deployment status
kubectl get deployments -n production
kubectl get pods -n production

# View logs
kubectl logs -f deployment/anihpj-web -n production
🌐 Step 4: Expose Service

Expose your application using Kubernetes Service and AWS Load Balancer.

# Create Service
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-service
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "nlb"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    component: web
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
EOF

# Get load balancer URL
kubectl get service anihpj-web-service -n production

# Expected Output:
# NAME                  TYPE           CLUSTER-IP       EXTERNAL-IP                                      PORT(S)        AGE
# anihpj-web-service    LoadBalancer   10.100.123.45    a1b2c3-123456.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com       80:31234/TCP   2m

# Test the service
LOAD_BALANCER_URL=$(kubectl get service anihpj-web-service -n production -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].hostname}')
curl http://$LOAD_BALANCER_URL
📈 Step 5: Configure Auto Scaling

Set up Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) and Cluster Autoscaler for automatic scaling.

📊 Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
# Install Metrics Server (required for HPA)
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

# Create HPA
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 50
        periodSeconds: 60
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300
      policies:
      - type: Percent
        value: 10
        periodSeconds: 60
EOF

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa -n production
kubectl describe hpa anihpj-web-hpa -n production

💰 Pricing & Cost Optimization

💸 Pricing Models

EKS pricing consists of cluster management fees plus compute costs for worker nodes.

🎛️

EKS Control Plane

$0.10/hour

  • Per cluster, per hour
  • $73/month per cluster
  • Includes multi-AZ control plane
  • Unlimited API calls
🖥️

EC2 Worker Nodes

Standard EC2 pricing

  • t3.large: ~$0.0832/hour
  • $60.24/month per node
  • EBS volume costs extra
  • Data transfer charges apply
⚡

Fargate Pods

Per vCPU/memory

  • $0.04048/vCPU/hour
  • $0.004445/GB/hour
  • Billed per second
  • No idle capacity costs
🧮 Cost Scenarios for anihpj
Scenario Configuration Monthly Cost
🌱 Development 1 cluster + 2 t3.small nodes
2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM total 		~$103/month
$73 (cluster) + $30 (nodes)
📈 Small Production 1 cluster + 3 t3.large nodes
6 vCPU, 24 GB RAM total 		~$254/month
$73 (cluster) + $181 (nodes)
🚀 Production 1 cluster + 5 t3.xlarge nodes
20 vCPU, 80 GB RAM total 		~$674/month
$73 (cluster) + $601 (nodes)
⚡ Fargate Only 1 cluster + Fargate pods
Average: 3 pods (0.5 vCPU, 1 GB each) 		~$163/month
$73 (cluster) + $90 (Fargate)
⚡ Cost Optimization Strategies
💰 Top Cost-Saving Tips for anihpj
  • ✅ Use Spot Instances: Save up to 90% on compute costs for fault-tolerant workloads 
    instancesDistribution:
  onDemandBaseCapacity: 1
  onDemandPercentageAboveBaseCapacity: 25
  spotInstancePools: 4
  • ✅ Right-size Worker Nodes: Match instance types to workload requirements
    • Use t3.medium for light workloads ($30/month)
    • Use compute-optimized (c5) for CPU-intensive tasks
    • Use memory-optimized (r5) for memory-intensive tasks
  • ✅ Implement Cluster Autoscaler: Scale nodes based on pod requirements to avoid idle capacity
  • ✅ Use Fargate for Batch Jobs: Pay only for execution time, not idle nodes
  • ✅ Enable Container Insights Selectively: Costs $0.50 per GB ingested
  • ✅ Use Single Cluster for Multiple Environments: Use namespaces instead of separate clusters
    • Save $73/month per avoided cluster
    • Use labels and RBAC for isolation
  • ✅ Reserved Instances/Savings Plans: Save up to 72% for predictable workloads
  • ✅ Optimize Pod Resource Requests: Prevent over-provisioning 
    resources:
  requests:
    memory: "512Mi"  # Don't over-request
    cpu: "250m"      # Match actual usage
  limits:
    memory: "1Gi"
    cpu: "500m"

🔒 Security & Best Practices

✅ Security Checklist
🔐 Cluster Security
🛡️ Network Security
📦 Pod Security
🔐 IAM & RBAC Configuration

Configure IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA) and Kubernetes RBAC for fine-grained access control.

🎫 IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)
# Create IAM OIDC provider for cluster
eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \
  --cluster anihpj-production \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --approve

# Create IAM policy for anihpj app
cat > anihpj-policy.json <<EOF
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ses:SendEmail",
        "ses:SendRawEmail"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:*:*:secret:anihpj/*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
  --policy-name AnihpjAppPolicy \
  --policy-document file://anihpj-policy.json

# Create service account with IAM role
eksctl create iamserviceaccount \
  --cluster anihpj-production \
  --namespace production \
  --name anihpj-sa \
  --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/AnihpjAppPolicy \
  --approve

# Use service account in deployment
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2
        # AWS credentials automatically injected via IRSA
EOF
👥 Kubernetes RBAC Example
# Create Role for anihpj developers
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: anihpj-developer
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["", "apps"]
  resources: ["pods", "deployments", "services"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/log"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods/exec"]
  verbs: ["create"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-developer-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: developer@anihpj.com
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: anihpj-developer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
EOF
🛡️ Network Security

Implement network policies and security groups to control pod-to-pod communication.

🌐 Network Policy Example
# Install Calico for Network Policy support
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/v3.26.1/manifests/calico.yaml

# Create Network Policy for anihpj
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web-policy
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      component: web
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
  ingress:
  # Allow traffic from Load Balancer
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: ingress-nginx
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8000
  egress:
  # Allow DNS
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: kube-system
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
  # Allow PostgreSQL
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: postgresql
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432
  # Allow Redis
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: redis
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 6379
  # Allow outbound HTTPS
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector: {}
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 443
EOF

# Verify network policy
kubectl get networkpolicies -n production
kubectl describe networkpolicy anihpj-web-policy -n production

🔗 Integrations & Ecosystem

⚖️ AWS Load Balancer Controller

Automatically provision ALB/NLB for Kubernetes Ingress resources.

# Install AWS Load Balancer Controller
kubectl apply -k "github.com/aws/eks-charts/stable/aws-load-balancer-controller//crds?ref=master"

helm repo add eks https://aws.github.io/eks-charts
helm install aws-load-balancer-controller eks/aws-load-balancer-controller \
  -n kube-system \
  --set clusterName=anihpj-production

# Create Ingress for anihpj
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/ingress.class: alb
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme: internet-facing
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/target-type: ip
    alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/certificate-arn: arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:123456789012:certificate/abc123
spec:
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: anihpj-web-service
            port:
              number: 80
EOF
📦 Amazon ECR Integration

Store and pull container images from Amazon Elastic Container Registry.

# Authenticate Docker to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Build and push image
docker build -t anihpj:v1.2 .
docker tag anihpj:v1.2 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2
docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj:v1.2

# EKS automatically has access to ECR in same account
# No image pull secrets needed!
📊 CloudWatch Container Insights

Monitor cluster and pod metrics with CloudWatch Container Insights.

# Install CloudWatch agent
ClusterName=anihpj-production
RegionName=us-east-1

curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aws-samples/amazon-cloudwatch-container-insights/latest/k8s-deployment-manifest-templates/deployment-mode/daemonset/container-insights-monitoring/quickstart/cwagent-fluentd-quickstart.yaml | sed "s/{{cluster_name}}/${ClusterName}/;s/{{region_name}}/${RegionName}/" | kubectl apply -f -

# View metrics in CloudWatch
aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics \
  --namespace ContainerInsights \
  --metric-name pod_cpu_utilization \
  --dimensions Name=ClusterName,Value=anihpj-production \
  --start-time 2026-01-13T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2026-01-14T00:00:00Z \
  --period 3600 \
  --statistics Average
💾 Amazon EFS for Persistent Storage

Use EFS for shared persistent storage across pods.

# Install EFS CSI Driver
kubectl apply -k "github.com/kubernetes-sigs/aws-efs-csi-driver/deploy/kubernetes/overlays/stable/?ref=master"

# Create StorageClass for EFS
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: efs-sc
provisioner: efs.csi.aws.com
parameters:
  fileSystemId: fs-1234567890abcdef
  directoryPerms: "700"
EOF

# Use EFS in deployment
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: anihpj-media-pvc
  namespace: production
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany
  storageClassName: efs-sc
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 100Gi
EOF
⚡ AWS Fargate for EKS

Run pods on Fargate for serverless pod execution.

# Create Fargate profile
eksctl create fargateprofile \
  --cluster anihpj-production \
  --name anihpj-batch-jobs \
  --namespace batch \
  --labels app=batch-job

# Pods in 'batch' namespace will automatically run on Fargate
kubectl create namespace batch
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-data-export
  namespace: batch
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: batch-job
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: export
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-export:v1.0
        command: ["python", "export_jobs.py"]
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
EOF

⌨️ API & CLI Reference

🔧 Essential kubectl Commands
# Cluster info
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl describe node <node-name>

# Deployments
kubectl get deployments -n production
kubectl describe deployment anihpj-web -n production
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-web -n production
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj-web -n production
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj-web -n production

# Pods
kubectl get pods -n production
kubectl get pods -n production -o wide
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n production
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n production
kubectl logs -f <pod-name> -n production --tail=100
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n production -- /bin/bash

# Services
kubectl get services -n production
kubectl describe service anihpj-web-service -n production

# Scaling
kubectl scale deployment anihpj-web --replicas=5 -n production
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj-web --min=3 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70 -n production

# Debugging
kubectl get events -n production --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods -n production
☁️ AWS CLI for EKS
# List clusters
aws eks list-clusters --region us-east-1

# Describe cluster
aws eks describe-cluster --name anihpj-production --region us-east-1

# Update kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig --name anihpj-production --region us-east-1

# List node groups
aws eks list-nodegroups --cluster-name anihpj-production --region us-east-1

# Describe node group
aws eks describe-nodegroup \
  --cluster-name anihpj-production \
  --nodegroup-name anihpj-node-group \
  --region us-east-1

# Update cluster version
aws eks update-cluster-version \
  --name anihpj-production \
  --kubernetes-version 1.28 \
  --region us-east-1
📦 Helm Package Manager
# Install Helm
curl https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 | bash

# Add repositories
helm repo add stable https://charts.helm.sh/stable
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm repo update

# Install PostgreSQL with Helm
helm install anihpj-db bitnami/postgresql \
  --namespace production \
  --set auth.username=anihpj \
  --set auth.password=SecurePassword123 \
  --set auth.database=anihpj_db \
  --set primary.persistence.size=50Gi

# Install Redis
helm install anihpj-redis bitnami/redis \
  --namespace production \
  --set auth.password=RedisPassword123

# List installed charts
helm list -n production

# Upgrade release
helm upgrade anihpj-db bitnami/postgresql \
  --namespace production \
  --set primary.persistence.size=100Gi

# Uninstall
helm uninstall anihpj-db -n production

🎉 You're Ready to Deploy anihpj on EKS!

Amazon EKS provides enterprise-grade Kubernetes with AWS integration, making it perfect for complex microservices and cloud-native applications.

☸️
Kubernetes Industry-standard orchestration
🎛️
Managed Control Plane AWS handles master nodes
🔗
AWS Integration Native IAM, VPC, ALB support
📈
Auto Scaling HPA & Cluster Autoscaler
📚 Additional Resources
📖 Documentation
AWS EKS Docs
☸️ Kubernetes Docs
Official K8s Documentation
🛠️ eksctl
eksctl Documentation
💬 Community
AWS re:Post
🎓 Workshops
EKS Workshop
🎥 Videos
EKS Tutorials
🚀 Next Steps for anihpj EKS Deployment
  1. Install tools: eksctl, kubectl, Helm, AWS CLI
  2. Create EKS cluster: Use eksctl or AWS Console
  3. Containerize Django app: Create Dockerfile and push to ECR
  4. Create Kubernetes manifests: Deployment, Service, Ingress
  5. Set up IRSA: IAM roles for service accounts
  6. Deploy application: kubectl apply your manifests
  7. Install AWS Load Balancer Controller: For Ingress support
  8. Configure auto-scaling: HPA and Cluster Autoscaler
  9. Enable Container Insights: CloudWatch monitoring
  10. Implement CI/CD: GitHub Actions or AWS CodePipeline
💡 Key Decision: EKS vs ECS?
Choose EKS if you:
  • Need Kubernetes-specific features
  • Want multi-cloud portability
  • Have complex microservices architecture
  • Need advanced orchestration (StatefulSets, CRDs)
  • Have Kubernetes expertise in team
Choose ECS if you:
  • Want simpler container orchestration
  • Prefer AWS-native solution
  • Have straightforward container needs
  • Want to avoid Kubernetes complexity
  • No multi-cloud requirement

Pro Tip: Start with managed node groups for simplicity. Add Fargate profiles for batch jobs and cost optimization once you're comfortable with EKS basics. 💡

💼 Common Use Cases

Amazon EKS excels in various scenarios. Here are the most common use cases, specifically tailored for deploying the anihpj job portal and similar applications.

🏗️ Microservices Architecture

Deploy anihpj as multiple independent microservices for better scalability and maintainability.

📦 anihpj Microservices Deployment
# Frontend Service
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-frontend
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      tier: frontend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        tier: frontend
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: frontend
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-frontend:v1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 3000
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
---
# API Service
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-api
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      tier: api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        tier: api
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-api-sa
      containers:
      - name: api
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-api:v1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: database-url
        - name: REDIS_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: redis-url
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
---
# Worker Service (Celery)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-worker
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      tier: worker
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        tier: worker
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-worker:v1.0
        command: ["celery", "-A", "anihpj", "worker", "--loglevel=info"]
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "512Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"

Benefits: Independent scaling, isolated failures, easier debugging, technology diversity

⚙️ Batch Processing with Fargate

Use Kubernetes Jobs and Fargate for cost-effective batch processing of job applications, CV parsing, and email campaigns.

🔄 Batch Job for Resume Processing
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: resume-processor
  namespace: batch-jobs
spec:
  completions: 100  # Process 100 resumes
  parallelism: 10   # 10 parallel workers
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        component: resume-processor
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-batch-sa
      restartPolicy: OnFailure
      containers:
      - name: processor
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-batch:v1.0
        command:
        - python
        - manage.py
        - process_resumes
        - --batch-size=10
        env:
        - name: S3_BUCKET
          value: anihpj-resumes
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secrets
              key: database-url
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "1Gi"
            cpu: "500m"
          limits:
            memory: "2Gi"
            cpu: "1000m"
---
# CronJob for Daily Digest Emails
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: daily-digest
  namespace: production
spec:
  schedule: "0 8 * * *"  # 8 AM daily
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: digest
            image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-mailer:v1.0
            command:
            - python
            - manage.py
            - send_daily_digest
            resources:
              requests:
                memory: "512Mi"
                cpu: "250m"

Use Cases: Resume parsing, bulk email sending, report generation, data cleanup

🏢 Multi-Tenant Applications

Use namespaces and RBAC to host multiple clients or environments in a single EKS cluster.

🌍 Multi-Environment Setup
# Create namespaces for different environments
kubectl create namespace dev
kubectl create namespace staging
kubectl create namespace production

# Apply resource quotas per namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: dev
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "10"
    requests.memory: "20Gi"
    limits.cpu: "20"
    limits.memory: "40Gi"
    pods: "50"
---
# Network policies for isolation
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-from-other-namespaces
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  # Only allow traffic from same namespace
  - from:
    - podSelector: {}

Benefits: Cost savings ($73/month for one cluster vs $219/month for three), unified management, resource sharing

🤖 Machine Learning & AI Workloads

Deploy ML models for resume matching, job recommendations, and candidate scoring on EKS with GPU support.

🧠 ML Model Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ml-service
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: ml-service
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: ml-service
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        instance-type: p3.2xlarge  # GPU instance
      containers:
      - name: ml-api
        image: 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-ml:v1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5000
        resources:
          limits:
            nvidia.com/gpu: 1  # Request 1 GPU
        env:
        - name: MODEL_PATH
          value: /models/resume-matcher
        volumeMounts:
        - name: models
          mountPath: /models
      volumes:
      - name: models
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: ml-models-pvc

Use Cases: Resume parsing with NLP, job-candidate matching, salary predictions, skills extraction

💡 Best Use Case for anihpj: Start with a monolithic Django deployment, then gradually break into microservices (frontend, API, workers) as traffic grows. Use Fargate for batch jobs and keep web services on managed node groups for predictable costs.

🔧 Troubleshooting & FAQs

⚠️ Common Issues & Solutions
🔴 Issue: Pods Stuck in Pending State

Symptoms: Pods show "Pending" status and never start

Causes:

  • Insufficient cluster capacity (not enough nodes)
  • Resource requests too high (CPU/memory)
  • Node selector mismatch
  • PersistentVolumeClaim not bound

Solutions:

# Check pod events
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n production

# Check node capacity
kubectl get nodes
kubectl describe node <node-name>

# Check cluster autoscaler logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system -l app=cluster-autoscaler

# Scale node group manually if needed
eksctl scale nodegroup --cluster=anihpj-cluster --name=ng-1 --nodes=5

# Reduce resource requests if too high
resources:
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"  # Instead of 2Gi
    cpu: "250m"      # Instead of 2000m
🔴 Issue: Unable to Connect to Cluster (kubectl timeout)

Symptoms: kubectl get nodes times out or shows "connection refused"

Causes:

  • kubectl not configured properly
  • IAM authentication issues
  • API server endpoint not accessible
  • Security group blocking access

Solutions:

# Update kubeconfig
aws eks update-kubeconfig --name anihpj-cluster --region us-east-1

# Verify AWS credentials
aws sts get-caller-identity

# Check cluster status
aws eks describe-cluster --name anihpj-cluster --region us-east-1

# Test API server endpoint
ENDPOINT=$(aws eks describe-cluster --name anihpj-cluster --query 'cluster.endpoint' --output text)
curl -k $ENDPOINT/healthz

# Check security group rules
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --group-ids <cluster-sg-id>
🔴 Issue: Nodes Not Joining Cluster

Symptoms: EC2 instances running but not showing in kubectl get nodes

Causes:

  • Incorrect IAM role for nodes
  • Nodes can't reach API server
  • aws-auth ConfigMap misconfigured
  • Wrong AMI or bootstrap script

Solutions:

# SSH to node and check kubelet logs
ssh ec2-user@<node-ip>
sudo journalctl -u kubelet -f

# Verify aws-auth ConfigMap
kubectl get configmap aws-auth -n kube-system -o yaml

# Check node IAM role has required policies
aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name eksctl-anihpj-nodegroup-ng-1-NodeInstanceRole

# Required policies:
# - AmazonEKSWorkerNodePolicy
# - AmazonEKS_CNI_Policy
# - AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryReadOnly
🔴 Issue: ImagePullBackOff Error

Symptoms: Pods fail to start with "ImagePullBackOff" or "ErrImagePull"

Causes:

  • Image doesn't exist in ECR
  • Wrong image tag
  • ECR authentication issue
  • No IAM permissions to pull from ECR

Solutions:

# Check if image exists
aws ecr describe-images --repository-name anihpj-web --region us-east-1

# Verify image name in deployment
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n production

# Test ECR access from worker node
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Add ECR policy to node role
aws iam attach-role-policy \
  --role-name eksctl-anihpj-nodegroup-ng-1-NodeInstanceRole \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryReadOnly
🔴 Issue: LoadBalancer Service Stuck in Pending

Symptoms: Service type LoadBalancer never gets EXTERNAL-IP

Causes:

  • AWS Load Balancer Controller not installed
  • Insufficient IAM permissions
  • Subnet tags missing
  • Security group issues

Solutions:

# Install AWS Load Balancer Controller
helm repo add eks https://aws.github.io/eks-charts
helm install aws-load-balancer-controller eks/aws-load-balancer-controller \
  -n kube-system \
  --set clusterName=anihpj-cluster \
  --set serviceAccount.create=false \
  --set serviceAccount.name=aws-load-balancer-controller

# Check controller logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system deployment/aws-load-balancer-controller

# Tag subnets for ELB
aws ec2 create-tags --resources subnet-xxx \
  --tags Key=kubernetes.io/role/elb,Value=1

# Check service events
kubectl describe service anihpj-web-service -n production
❓ Frequently Asked Questions
💰 How much does EKS cost for a small production app?

Answer: For a small production app like anihpj:

  • EKS cluster: $73/month
  • 2 × t3.medium nodes (24/7): ~$60/month
  • Fargate for batch jobs (10 hours/day): ~$30/month
  • Total: ~$163/month

Compare to ECS Fargate only: ~$90/month (but less control)

🔄 Can I upgrade Kubernetes version without downtime?

Answer: Yes! EKS supports in-place cluster upgrades with zero downtime:

  • Control plane updates automatically across multiple AZs
  • Node groups can be updated using rolling updates
  • Process: Upgrade control plane → Update node groups → Update add-ons
  • Best practice: Test in staging environment first
# Upgrade cluster control plane
aws eks update-cluster-version --name anihpj-cluster --kubernetes-version 1.28

# Update node group (creates new nodes, drains old ones)
eksctl upgrade nodegroup --name=ng-1 --cluster=anihpj-cluster --kubernetes-version=1.28
🌍 Should I use one cluster or multiple clusters?

Answer: For most cases, use one cluster with multiple namespaces:

Single Cluster (Recommended):

  • ✅ Cost-effective ($73/month vs $219+ for 3 clusters)
  • ✅ Easier to manage and operate
  • ✅ Shared resources (nodes, add-ons)
  • ✅ Use namespaces for dev/staging/prod isolation

Multiple Clusters (When to use):

  • ✅ Strict compliance requirements (complete isolation)
  • ✅ Different teams with full autonomy
  • ✅ Different Kubernetes versions needed
  • ✅ Blast radius concerns (prod separate from non-prod)
🔐 How do I manage secrets securely in EKS?

Answer: Multiple options, from basic to advanced:

Option 1: Kubernetes Secrets (Basic):

kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secrets \
  --from-literal=database-password=MySecurePassword123 \
  -n production

Option 2: AWS Secrets Manager (Recommended):

# Install Secrets Store CSI Driver
helm repo add secrets-store-csi-driver https://kubernetes-sigs.github.io/secrets-store-csi-driver/charts
helm install -n kube-system csi-secrets-store secrets-store-csi-driver/secrets-store-csi-driver

# Mount secrets from AWS Secrets Manager
volumeMounts:
- name: secrets
  mountPath: "/mnt/secrets"
  readOnly: true
volumes:
- name: secrets
  csi:
    driver: secrets-store.csi.k8s.io
    readOnly: true
    volumeAttributes:
      secretProviderClass: "anihpj-secrets"

Option 3: External Secrets Operator (Advanced): Automatically syncs secrets from AWS Secrets Manager to Kubernetes Secrets

📊 How do I monitor and debug my EKS cluster?

Answer: Use these AWS-native tools:

  • CloudWatch Container Insights: Metrics, logs, performance data
  • CloudWatch Logs: Centralized application logs
  • X-Ray: Distributed tracing
  • kubectl top: Real-time resource usage
  • Kubernetes Dashboard: Web UI for cluster
# Enable Container Insights
eksctl utils update-cluster-logging --cluster=anihpj-cluster --enable-types=all --approve

# View logs in CloudWatch
aws logs tail /aws/eks/anihpj-cluster/cluster --follow

# Check resource usage
kubectl top nodes
kubectl top pods -n production

# Port forward to access dashboard
kubectl port-forward -n kubernetes-dashboard service/kubernetes-dashboard 8080:443
🚀 How do I implement blue-green or canary deployments?

Answer: Use native Kubernetes features or Flagger:

Blue-Green Deployment:

# Deploy green version alongside blue
kubectl apply -f deployment-green.yaml

# Test green deployment
kubectl port-forward service/anihpj-green 8080:80

# Switch traffic by updating service selector
kubectl patch service anihpj-web -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"green"}}}'

Canary Deployment (with Flagger):

apiVersion: flagger.app/v1beta1
kind: Canary
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-web
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 60
  service:
    port: 80
  analysis:
    interval: 1m
    threshold: 5
    maxWeight: 50
    stepWeight: 10
    metrics:
    - name: request-success-rate
      thresholdRange:
        min: 99
      interval: 1m

🛡️ Compliance & Governance

📜 Security & Compliance Certifications

Amazon EKS meets a wide range of compliance standards, making it suitable for regulated industries including healthcare, finance, and government.

🏥

HIPAA Compliant

Run healthcare applications with PHI (Protected Health Information)

  • Sign BAA with AWS
  • Enable encryption at rest/transit
  • Use private endpoints
  • Enable comprehensive logging
💳

PCI DSS Level 1

Process, store, or transmit payment card data securely

  • Network segmentation with VPC
  • Encryption for cardholder data
  • Access control with IAM & RBAC
  • Audit trails with CloudTrail
🌍

GDPR Ready

Deploy applications handling EU citizen data

  • Data residency in EU regions
  • Right to erasure support
  • Data portability
  • DPA available from AWS
🏛️

SOC 1, 2, 3

Service Organization Controls compliance

  • SOC 2 Type II certified
  • Annual third-party audits
  • Security & availability controls
  • Reports available in Artifact
🔒

ISO 27001

Information security management certified

  • ISO 27001:2013 certified
  • ISO 27017 (cloud security)
  • ISO 27018 (privacy)
  • Annual recertification
🏛️

FedRAMP

U.S. government cloud security program

  • FedRAMP Moderate authorized
  • AWS GovCloud available
  • FIPS 140-2 validated
  • Suitable for federal agencies

📋 Additional Certifications: ISO 9001, ISO 27017, ISO 27018, ISO 22301, CSA STAR, IRAP, MTCS, K-ISMS, ENS High, OSPAR, HITRUST CSF, and more. View full list in AWS Compliance Programs.

✅ Production Readiness Checklist

Before deploying anihpj to production on EKS, ensure you've completed these critical items:

🔐 Security
🏗️ High Availability
📊 Monitoring & Observability
📈 Scalability
💾 Backup & Disaster Recovery
🚀 CI/CD & Deployment
💰 Cost Optimization

📖 Glossary

Essential Kubernetes and EKS terminology for the anihpj deployment:

Cluster

A set of worker machines (nodes) that run containerized applications. An EKS cluster consists of a managed control plane and worker nodes.

Control Plane

The Kubernetes master components that manage the cluster. In EKS, AWS fully manages the control plane across multiple availability zones.

Node

A worker machine in Kubernetes (EC2 instance in EKS). Nodes run pods and are managed by the control plane.

Pod

The smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. A pod encapsulates one or more containers (e.g., your Django application container).

Deployment

A Kubernetes resource that manages a replicated application (e.g., 5 replicas of anihpj web app). Handles rolling updates and rollbacks.

Service

An abstract way to expose an application running on a set of pods as a network service. Types: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer.

Namespace

A virtual cluster within a Kubernetes cluster for resource isolation (e.g., dev, staging, production namespaces).

Ingress

Manages external access to services, typically HTTP/HTTPS. Provides load balancing, SSL termination, and name-based virtual hosting.

ConfigMap

Kubernetes object to store non-sensitive configuration data in key-value pairs (e.g., Django settings).

Secret

Kubernetes object to store sensitive data like passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys (base64 encoded).

kubectl

Command-line tool for interacting with Kubernetes clusters. Used to deploy apps, inspect resources, and view logs.

eksctl

CLI tool for creating and managing EKS clusters. Simplifies cluster creation with sensible defaults.

Helm

Package manager for Kubernetes. Helm charts define, install, and upgrade complex Kubernetes applications.

HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)

Automatically scales the number of pods based on observed CPU/memory utilization or custom metrics.

Cluster Autoscaler

Automatically adjusts the number of nodes in your cluster when pods fail to launch due to insufficient resources.

IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)

Associates an IAM role with a Kubernetes service account, allowing pods to access AWS services securely.

Node Group

A set of EC2 instances with the same configuration (instance type, AMI, IAM role) managed as a group in EKS.

Fargate Profile

Defines which pods should run on AWS Fargate instead of EC2 nodes. Specified by namespace and labels.

CNI (Container Network Interface)

Specification and libraries for configuring network interfaces in Linux containers. EKS uses Amazon VPC CNI.

PVC (PersistentVolumeClaim)

Request for storage by a user. PVCs consume PersistentVolume (PV) resources (e.g., EBS volumes in EKS).

RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)

Method of regulating access to Kubernetes resources based on the roles of individual users or service accounts.

StatefulSet

Manages stateful applications with persistent storage and stable network identities (e.g., databases, message queues).

DaemonSet

Ensures all (or some) nodes run a copy of a pod (e.g., logging agents, monitoring agents).

Job

Creates one or more pods and ensures a specified number of them successfully complete (e.g., batch processing jobs).

CronJob

Creates Jobs on a repeating schedule (e.g., daily database backups, hourly report generation).

📚 Resources & Next Steps

  • 📗 EKS API Reference
  • 📕 Kubernetes Documentation
  • 📙 eksctl Command Reference
✨ Best Practices
  • ⚡ EKS Best Practices Guide
  • 🔒 EKS Security Best Practices
  • 💰 AWS Containers Blog
🔗 Related Services
  • 📦 Amazon ECR - Container Registry
  • ⚡ AWS Fargate - Serverless Compute
  • ⚖️ Elastic Load Balancing - Load Balancers
  • 📊 CloudWatch - Container Insights
🚀 Get Started with EKS for anihpj
  1. Create EKS Cluster: Use eksctl or AWS Console to create your first cluster
  2. Configure kubectl: Set up kubectl to communicate with your EKS cluster
  3. Build Container Images: Containerize your Django application and push to ECR
  4. Create Kubernetes Manifests: Define deployments, services, and ingress for anihpj
  5. Deploy Application: Apply manifests to deploy your application to EKS
  6. Configure Auto Scaling: Set up HPA for pods and Cluster Autoscaler for nodes
  7. Set Up Load Balancer: Deploy AWS Load Balancer Controller for external access
  8. Configure Monitoring: Enable CloudWatch Container Insights for observability
  9. Implement CI/CD: Integrate with CodePipeline for automated deployments
  10. Test & Optimize: Load test your application and optimize resource requests/limits

💡 Pro Tip

Start with Fargate for serverless node management, then migrate to managed node groups as you scale. This approach minimizes operational overhead while you learn Kubernetes!

✅ Production Checklist Summary
☸️
Cluster Setup

Multi-AZ control plane, managed node groups configured

🔒
Security

IAM roles, RBAC, network policies, secrets encryption

📊
Monitoring

Container Insights, logging, metrics, alerting configured

📈
Scaling

HPA, Cluster Autoscaler, resource limits defined

💰
Cost Control

Spot instances, right-sizing, resource quotas

🛡️
Compliance

Audit logging, compliance checks, policy enforcement

📦 Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry)

Category: Containers, Developer Tools  |  Use Case: Container Image Management, CI/CD  |  Pricing: $0.10/GB/month storage + $0.09/GB data transfer  |  Last Updated: January 2026  |  Documentation: AWS ECR Docs

Fully managed Docker container registry that makes it easy to store, share, and deploy container images and artifacts. Integrated with Amazon ECS, EKS, and Lambda for seamless deployment of your anihpj Django application containers.

📋 Overview & Introduction

Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) is a fully managed container registry offering high-performance hosting, so you can reliably deploy application images and artifacts anywhere. ECR eliminates the need to operate your own container repositories or worry about scaling the underlying infrastructure. It integrates with IAM for resource-level control and supports both public and private repositories.

🎯 Key Benefits
🔒

Fully Secure

Images encrypted at rest and in transit with IAM integration

  • AES-256 encryption at rest
  • HTTPS for data in transit
  • IAM resource-level permissions
⚡

High Performance

Fast image push and pull with global availability

  • Low latency image transfers
  • Available in all AWS regions
  • S3-backed storage
🔍

Image Scanning

Automated vulnerability scanning for container images

  • CVE database scanning
  • Scan on push capability
  • Detailed vulnerability reports
♻️

Lifecycle Management

Automated cleanup of old images to reduce costs

  • Rule-based image expiration
  • Count and age-based policies
  • Automated cleanup execution
🔗

Native AWS Integration

Seamless integration with ECS, EKS, and Lambda

  • Direct ECS/EKS deployment
  • Lambda container support
  • CodeBuild/CodePipeline integration
🌐

Public & Private Registries

Host both public and private container images

  • Private repos for internal use
  • Public repos for open source
  • Fine-grained access control
🔄

Cross-Region Replication

Replicate images across multiple AWS regions

  • Automatic replication
  • Reduced latency globally
  • Disaster recovery support
💰

Cost-Effective

Pay only for storage and data transfer you use

  • No upfront costs
  • No minimum fees
  • Competitive pricing
💡 When to Use ECR: Perfect for your anihpj Django job portal when deploying containerized applications on ECS, EKS, or Lambda. Store your Django app, Celery workers, and any microservices as Docker images in ECR for seamless deployment.
⚖️ ECR vs Other Container Registries
Feature Amazon ECR Docker Hub GitHub Container Registry Azure Container Registry
Hosting ✅ Fully managed by AWS ✅ Fully managed ✅ Fully managed ✅ Fully managed by Azure
Free Tier ✅ 500 MB storage (12 months) ✅ Unlimited public repos ✅ Free for public repos ❌ No free tier
Private Repositories ✅ Unlimited ⚠️ 1 free (paid plans required) ✅ Unlimited ✅ Unlimited
Vulnerability Scanning ✅ Built-in (Basic & Enhanced) ✅ Available (Pro/Team plans) ✅ Via GitHub Advanced Security ✅ Built-in with Defender
Image Encryption ✅ At rest (AES-256) & in transit ✅ In transit only ✅ In transit only ✅ At rest & in transit
IAM Integration ✅ Native AWS IAM ⚠️ Basic access tokens ✅ GitHub PAT/OAuth ✅ Azure AD integration
Geo-Replication ✅ Cross-region replication ❌ Not available ❌ Not available ✅ Geo-replication (Premium)
Pricing Model $0.10/GB storage + data transfer Free to $7/month (Pro) Free for public, $0.008/GB $0.167/day (Basic tier)
Best For AWS deployments, ECS/EKS Public images, community projects GitHub-based workflows Azure deployments, AKS

│                   🎯 USE AMAZON ECR WHEN:                                      │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                │
│  ✅ Deploying to ECS/EKS/Fargate                                              │
│     • Native integration, no extra configuration                               │
│     • Free data transfer within same region                                    │
│     • Fastest image pull times                                                 │
│                                                                                │
│  ✅ Need strong security & compliance                                          │
│     • IAM role-based access control                                            │
│     • Encryption at rest (AES-256/KMS)                                        │
│     • VPC endpoint support for private networking                              │
│     • Meets HIPAA, SOC, ISO 27001 requirements                                 │
│                                                                                │
│  ✅ Want automated vulnerability scanning                                      │
│     • CVE detection on push                                                    │
│     • Continuous monitoring                                                    │
│     • Integration with AWS Security Hub                                        │
│                                                                                │
│  ✅ Need lifecycle management                                                  │
│     • Automated cleanup of old images                                          │
│     • Reduce storage costs by 60-80%                                          │
│     • Rule-based image retention                                               │
│                                                                                │
│  ✅ Multi-region deployment required                                           │
│     • Cross-region replication built-in                                        │
│     • Reduced latency globally                                                 │
│     • Disaster recovery support                                                │
│                                                                                │

│     • Want maximum compatibility                                               │

│                                                                                │

│     • Using GitHub Actions for CI/CD                                           │
│     • Need public packages for open source                                     │
│     • Free: 500MB storage, 1GB transfer                                       │
│                                                                                │
│  ☁️ USE AZURE CONTAINER REGISTRY WHEN:                                        │
│     • Deploying to Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)                              │

│     • Need ACR Tasks for image building                                        │
│                                                                                │
│  🌐 USE GOOGLE CONTAINER REGISTRY WHEN:                                       │

│     • GCP-based infrastructure                                                 │
