│  │          └─→ Reads/writes S3 uploads (has S3 permissions)                        │ │
│  │          └─→ Sends logs to CloudWatch (has CloudWatch permissions)               │ │
│  │                                                                                   │ │
│  │  4️⃣  Lambda function processes job applications                                  │ │
│  │      └─→ Lambda assumes anihpj-lambda-role                                       │ │
│  │          └─→ Queries RDS for job data                                            │ │
│  │          └─→ Sends email via SES (has SES permissions)                           │ │
│  │                                                                                   │ │
│  │  ✅ No credentials stored in code!                                                │ │
│  │  ✅ Each service has minimum required permissions (least privilege)               │ │
│  │  ✅ All actions logged in CloudTrail                                              │ │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                                        │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │                         SECURITY FEATURES ENABLED                                 │ │
│  │                                                                                   │ │
│  │  🔐 MFA: Required for all human users                                             │ │
│  │  🔐 Password Policy: 14+ chars, rotation every 90 days                            │ │
│  │  🔐 Access Keys: Rotated every 90 days, encrypted at rest                         │ │
│  │  🔐 Session Duration: 1 hour for roles, re-authenticate required                  │ │
│  │  🔐 IP Restrictions: Developers can only access from office/VPN                   │ │
│  │  🔐 CloudTrail: All IAM actions logged and monitored                              │ │
│  │  🔐 AWS Organizations: SCPs prevent accidental resource deletion                  │ │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

KEY PRINCIPLES FOR ANIHPJ IAM SECURITY:
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✅ Least Privilege: Grant minimum permissions needed (start restrictive, expand as needed)
✅ Role-Based Access: Use roles for services (EC2, Lambda, ECS) instead of embedding credentials
✅ Separation of Duties: Developers can deploy, but can't delete production databases
✅ MFA Everywhere: Require MFA for console access and sensitive operations
✅ Regular Audits: Review permissions quarterly, remove unused users/roles
✅ No Root Account: Use root only for billing, enable MFA, don't create access keys
🌳 IAM Decision Tree - Do You Need IAM?
START: Using AWS for your application?
│
├─ YES → ✅ USE IAM (Mandatory)
│  │
│  └─ IAM is FREE and REQUIRED for:
│     ├─ Creating users for team members (no shared root!)
│     ├─ Granting EC2 access to RDS/S3 (via roles)
│     ├─ Securing production resources (MFA, policies)
│     ├─ Meeting compliance requirements (audit trails)
│     └─ Automating deployments (service roles)
│
├─ How many people access your AWS account?
│  ├─ Just me (solo developer)?
│  │  └─ ✅ USE IAM (Essential)
│  │     ├─ DON'T use root account for daily work
│  │     ├─ Create admin user with MFA
│  │     ├─ Create EC2 role for anihpj app
│  │     └─ Result: Secure, no hardcoded credentials
│  │
│  ├─ 2-5 team members?
│  │  └─ ✅ USE IAM (Critical)
│  │     ├─ Create IAM user for each person
│  │     ├─ Organize into groups (Developers, DevOps)
│  │     ├─ Custom policies per group
│  │     └─ Benefit: Individual accountability + CloudTrail audit
│  │
│  └─ 5+ team members or multiple apps?
│     └─ ✅ USE IAM + Organizations (Mandatory)
│        ├─ Separate AWS accounts (Dev/Staging/Prod)
│        ├─ Service Control Policies (SCPs)
│        ├─ Cross-account roles for deployments
│        └─ Centralized billing + governance
│
├─ What deployment method do you use?
│  ├─ Manual SSH to EC2, hardcoded credentials?
│  │  └─ 🚨 CRITICAL: Switch to IAM Roles
│  │     ├─ Current risk: Credentials in code/config
│  │     ├─ Solution: Create anihpj-ec2-role
│  │     ├─ Attach to EC2 instance
│  │     └─ Remove ALL hardcoded AWS keys!
│  │
│  ├─ Using CI/CD (CodePipeline, GitHub Actions)?
│  │  └─ ✅ USE IAM Roles
│  │     ├─ CodeBuild role for building
│  │     ├─ CodeDeploy role for deployment
│  │     ├─ OIDC for GitHub Actions (no long-term keys!)
│  │     └─ Automatic credential rotation
│  │
│  └─ Container-based (ECS, EKS, Fargate)?
│     └─ ✅ USE IAM Task/Pod Roles
│        ├─ Each task gets temporary credentials
│        ├─ Fine-grained permissions per container
│        └─ Automatic rotation every 6 hours
│
├─ Do you store sensitive data (passwords, API keys)?
│  ├─ In environment variables or config files?
│  │  └─ ⚠️ USE IAM + Secrets Manager
│  │     ├─ Store RDS password in Secrets Manager
│  │     ├─ IAM role grants read access
│  │     ├─ Django reads secret via boto3
│  │     └─ Automatic rotation, encrypted at rest
│  │
│  └─ In S3 buckets (user uploads, company logos)?
│     └─ ✅ USE IAM Policies
│        ├─ Bucket policy: Allow EC2 role only
│        ├─ Deny public access (via IAM policy)
│        ├─ Pre-signed URLs for temporary access
│        └─ CloudTrail logs all S3 access
│
├─ Need to share access with external contractors?
│  ├─ For limited time (3 months)?
│  │  └─ ✅ USE IAM Temporary Credentials
│  │     ├─ Create IAM user with expiration policy
│  │     ├─ Set password to expire after 90 days
│  │     ├─ Grant minimal permissions (ReadOnly?)
│  │     └─ Disable user when contract ends
│  │
│  └─ Cross-account access (client's AWS account)?
│     └─ ✅ USE IAM Cross-Account Roles
│        ├─ No password sharing!
│        ├─ Client creates role, trusts your account
│        ├─ You assume role when needed
│        └─ Client can revoke anytime
│
└─ Compliance requirements (HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOC 2)?
   └─ ✅ USE IAM + CloudTrail + Config (Mandatory)
      ├─ IAM: Access control + MFA
      ├─ CloudTrail: Audit logs (who did what, when)
      ├─ AWS Config: Resource compliance checks
      ├─ Access Analyzer: Find public resources
      └─ GuardDuty: Threat detection

🎯 RECOMMENDATION FOR ANIHPJ:

Your Django job portal needs:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Environment: Dev + Staging + Production              │
│ Team: 3-5 developers + 1 DevOps engineer             │
│ Stack: Django (EC2/ECS) + PostgreSQL (RDS) + S3      │
│ Deployments: CI/CD via CodePipeline                  │
│ Security: Resume uploads, company data, PII          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ IAM Setup Required:

1️⃣  Users & Groups
   ├─ IAM users: alice.admin, john.dev, sarah.devops, bob.readonly
   ├─ Groups: Developers, DevOps, Admins, ReadOnly
   └─ MFA: Required for ALL users

2️⃣  Service Roles
   ├─ anihpj-ec2-role → EC2 accesses RDS + S3
   ├─ anihpj-lambda-role → Lambda sends emails (SES)
   ├─ anihpj-codebuild-role → CI/CD pipeline
   └─ anihpj-ecs-task-role → Container permissions

3️⃣  Custom Policies
   ├─ anihpj-s3-uploads-policy (resume bucket access)
   ├─ anihpj-rds-connect-policy (database access)
   └─ anihpj-developer-policy (dev environment only)

4️⃣  Security Measures
   ├─ Root account: MFA enabled, no access keys
   ├─ Password policy: 14+ chars, 90-day rotation
   ├─ CloudTrail: All API calls logged
   └─ Access Analyzer: Weekly scans

Time to setup: ~2 hours initial, 30 min/quarter for audits
Cost: $0 (IAM is free!)
Security improvement: 10x safer than shared credentials

🔧 Core Concepts

Understanding IAM's building blocks is crucial for securing your anihpj infrastructure. Let's explore each component in detail.

👤 IAM Users

An IAM user is an entity that represents a person or service that interacts with AWS. Each user has unique security credentials.

User Types for anihpj Team
User Type Purpose Access Method Example
Developer Code deployment, testing Console + AWS CLI john.dev@anihpj.com
DevOps Infrastructure management Console + CLI + IaC sarah.devops@anihpj.com
Admin Account-wide management Console (MFA required) alice.admin@anihpj.com
ReadOnly Auditing, monitoring Console (read-only) bob.readonly@anihpj.com
CI/CD Service Automated deployments Programmatic (access keys) github-actions-user
🔑 Best Practice:

Create individual IAM users for each team member. Never share credentials! Each user should have their own access keys and password. For anihpj, create users like john.dev, sarah.devops rather than generic developer accounts.

👥 IAM Groups

Groups are collections of IAM users. Use groups to specify permissions for multiple users, simplifying permission management for your anihpj team.

Recommended Group Structure for anihpj
anihpj AWS Account Groups:

📁 Developers
   ├─ Permissions: Deploy to dev/staging, read production
   ├─ Members: john.dev, mike.dev, emma.dev
   └─ Policies:
      ├─ EC2: Launch instances (dev/staging only)
      ├─ RDS: Connect to dev/staging databases
      ├─ S3: Read/write anihpj-dev-uploads, anihpj-staging-uploads
      ├─ CodeCommit/CodeBuild: Full access
      └─ CloudWatch: Read logs

📁 DevOps
   ├─ Permissions: Full infrastructure management
   ├─ Members: sarah.devops, tom.ops
   └─ Policies:
      ├─ EC2: Full access (all environments)
      ├─ RDS: Create, modify, backup (cannot delete prod)
      ├─ S3: Full access to all buckets
      ├─ VPC: Full network configuration
      ├─ Route53: DNS management
      ├─ CloudFormation: Stack deployment
      └─ IAM: Limited (create roles, not users)

📁 Admins
   ├─ Permissions: Account-wide administration
   ├─ Members: alice.admin
   └─ Policies:
      ├─ AdministratorAccess (with MFA)
      ├─ Billing access
      ├─ IAM full access
      └─ Can delete production resources (with approval workflow)

📁 ReadOnly-Auditors
   ├─ Permissions: View everything, change nothing
   ├─ Members: bob.auditor, compliance-team
   └─ Policies:
      ├─ ViewOnlyAccess
      ├─ ReadOnlyAccess
      └─ SecurityAudit
🎭 IAM Roles

Roles are identities that AWS services and applications can assume to obtain temporary security credentials. Critical for anihpj: Your EC2 instances, Lambda functions, and ECS tasks should use roles, NOT hardcoded access keys!

Role Types & Use Cases for anihpj
Role Name Trusted Entity Permissions anihpj Use Case
anihpj-ec2-role EC2 Service RDS, S3, CloudWatch, Parameter Store Django app on EC2 accesses RDS & uploads to S3
anihpj-lambda-role Lambda Service RDS, SES, CloudWatch Logs Email notifications for new job posts
anihpj-ecs-task-role ECS Tasks Service ECR Pull, RDS, S3, Secrets Manager Containerized Django app on ECS/Fargate
anihpj-codebuild-role CodeBuild Service ECR Push, S3 Artifacts, CloudWatch CI/CD pipeline builds & pushes Docker images
anihpj-github-actions-role GitHub OIDC Provider ECR, ECS, S3 (scoped to repo) GitHub Actions deploys without long-term keys
🔄 How Role Assumption Works (anihpj EC2 Example)
SCENARIO: EC2 instance running Django needs to upload user resume to S3

Step 1: EC2 Instance Launch
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EC2 Instance: anihpj-web-server-1     │
│  Instance Profile: anihpj-ec2-profile  │  ← Profile contains anihpj-ec2-role
│  Role: anihpj-ec2-role                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         │ (1) Instance metadata service provides temporary credentials
         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Temporary Security Credentials         │
│  - Access Key: ASIAXXXXXXXXXXX         │
│  - Secret Key: (temporary)             │
│  - Session Token: (temporary)          │
│  - Expiration: 6 hours                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         │ (2) Django app uses boto3 SDK (automatically uses instance credentials)
         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Django Code (views.py):                │
│                                        │
│ import boto3                           │
│ s3 = boto3.client('s3')                │  ← No credentials in code!
│ s3.upload_file(                        │
│     'resume.pdf',                      │
│     'anihpj-uploads',                  │
│     f'resumes/{user_id}/resume.pdf'    │
│ )                                      │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         │ (3) AWS SDK calls STS (Security Token Service)
         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ AWS STS verifies:                      │
│  ✅ Instance has anihpj-ec2-role       │
│  ✅ Role trust policy allows EC2       │
│  ✅ Role has S3 permissions             │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
         │
         │ (4) Request goes to S3 with temporary credentials
         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ S3 Bucket: anihpj-uploads              │
│  ✅ Upload successful!                  │
│  📁 resumes/user_123/resume.pdf         │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ NO credentials stored in code
✅ Credentials automatically rotate every 6 hours
✅ If instance is compromised, credentials expire quickly
✅ CloudTrail logs who (role) did what action
📜 IAM Policies

Policies are JSON documents that define permissions. They specify what actions are allowed or denied on which resources. Policies are attached to users, groups, or roles.

Policy Types
AWS Managed Policies

Created and maintained by AWS. Cover common use cases.

AmazonEC2FullAccess
AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess
Customer Managed Policies

Created by you for specific anihpj needs. Reusable across users/groups/roles.

anihpj-developer-policy
anihpj-s3-uploads-policy
Inline Policies

Attached directly to a single user/group/role. Not reusable.

Use for one-off permissions (e.g., temp contractor access)

📝 Policy Structure & Real anihpj Examples

Example 1: S3 Uploads Policy (Customer Managed)

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowListBucket",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:ListBucket",
        "s3:GetBucketLocation"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "AllowObjectOperations",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringLike": {
          "s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption": "AES256"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

Policy Name: anihpj-s3-uploads-policy
Attached to: anihpj-ec2-role, anihpj-ecs-task-role
Use Case: Django app uploads user resumes, company logos (encrypted only)

Example 2: RDS Connect Policy (Customer Managed)

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowRDSConnect",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "rds-db:connect"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:rds-db:us-east-1:123456789012:dbuser:db-ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP/anihpj_app_user"
      ]
    },
    {
      "Sid": "AllowDescribeDBInstances",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "rds:DescribeDBInstances",
        "rds:DescribeDBClusters"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}

Policy Name: anihpj-rds-connect-policy
Attached to: anihpj-ec2-role, anihpj-lambda-role
Use Case: Applications connect to PostgreSQL using IAM database authentication

Example 3: Developer Policy with Conditions (Customer Managed)

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "EC2DevStagingAccess",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ec2:RunInstances",
        "ec2:StartInstances",
        "ec2:StopInstances",
        "ec2:RebootInstances",
        "ec2:TerminateInstances"
      ],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "ec2:ResourceTag/Environment": ["dev", "staging"]
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "EC2ProductionReadOnly",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ec2:Describe*",
        "ec2:Get*"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyProductionTermination",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": [
        "ec2:TerminateInstances",
        "rds:DeleteDBInstance",
        "s3:DeleteBucket"
      ],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "aws:RequestedRegion": "us-east-1",
          "ec2:ResourceTag/Environment": "production"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "RequireMFAForSensitiveOps",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": [
        "ec2:TerminateInstances",
        "rds:ModifyDBInstance"
      ],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "BoolIfExists": {
          "aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent": "false"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

Policy Name: anihpj-developer-policy
Attached to: Developers Group
Features:
  ✅ Full control over dev/staging EC2 instances
  ✅ Read-only access to production
  ✅ Cannot delete production resources
  ✅ Must use MFA for terminating instances

Example 4: Cross-Account Access Policy (Trust Policy + Permissions)

// Trust Policy (who can assume this role)
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::987654321098:root"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "sts:ExternalId": "anihpj-prod-deploy-2026"
        },
        "IpAddress": {
          "aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.0/24"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

// Permissions Policy (what the role can do)
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowECSDeploy",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ecs:UpdateService",
        "ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition",
        "ecs:DescribeServices"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:service/anihpj-prod/*",
        "arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:123456789012:task-definition/anihpj-web:*"
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Use Case: Dev account (987654321098) deploys to Prod account (123456789012)
Security: External ID prevents confused deputy, IP restriction to office VPN
⚠️ Common IAM Policy Mistakes to Avoid:
  • Overly Permissive: "Resource": "*" with "Action": "*" grants full access!
  • Missing Conditions: Always add MFA requirements for sensitive operations
  • Hardcoded Credentials: Use roles instead of access keys in EC2/Lambda/ECS
  • No Expiration: Set MaxSessionDuration for roles (default 1 hour)
  • Root Account Usage: Enable MFA, don't create access keys, use only for billing
🔍 How IAM Evaluates Permissions
Permission Evaluation Logic (Decision Flow)
When john.dev@anihpj.com tries to terminate EC2 instance i-abc123:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Step 1: Is there an EXPLICIT DENY?                          │
│                                                              │
│ Check:                                                       │
│  - User inline policies                                     │
│  - Group policies (Developers)                              │
│  - SCPs (Service Control Policies from Organizations)       │
│                                                              │
│ Result: ✅ Deny found: "DenyProductionTermination"          │
│         (instance has tag Environment=production)            │
│                                                              │
│ ❌ REQUEST DENIED - Stop evaluation                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

IF no explicit deny, continue:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Step 2: Is there an EXPLICIT ALLOW?                         │
│                                                              │
│ Check:                                                       │
│  - User inline policies                                     │
│  - Group policies                                           │
│  - Resource-based policies                                  │
│                                                              │
│ Result: ✅ Allow found: "EC2DevStagingAccess"               │
│         (instance has tag Environment=dev)                   │
│                                                              │
│ ✅ REQUEST ALLOWED                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

IF no explicit allow:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Step 3: IMPLICIT DENY (default)                             │
│                                                              │
│ ❌ REQUEST DENIED                                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

KEY PRINCIPLE: Deny always wins!
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. Explicit Deny → DENIED (even if there's an allow)
2. Explicit Allow → ALLOWED (if no deny)
3. No Explicit Allow → DENIED (implicit deny)

🚀 Getting Started with IAM for anihpj

Let's set up a complete IAM structure for your anihpj Django job portal. We'll create users, groups, roles, and policies step by step.

📋 Prerequisites:
  • AWS account with root access (one-time setup)
  • AWS CLI installed and configured
  • Basic understanding of JSON
  • MFA device (phone app like Google Authenticator, Authy)
Step 1: Secure Your Root Account
🔐 Root Account Security Checklist
# 1. Enable MFA on root account (AWS Console)
Navigate to: IAM Dashboard → Security Recommendations → Add MFA to root
- Use virtual MFA app (Google Authenticator, Authy)
- Scan QR code
- Enter two consecutive MFA codes
- Save recovery codes in secure location

# 2. Delete root account access keys (if any exist)
aws iam list-access-keys --user-name root
# If keys exist, delete them:
aws iam delete-access-key --access-key-id AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE --user-name root

# 3. Create strong root password
- Minimum 14 characters
- Mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols
- Store in password manager (1Password, LastPass)

# 4. Set up account alias for easier login
aws iam create-account-alias --account-alias anihpj-production

# Login URL becomes: https://anihpj-production.signin.aws.amazon.com/console

✅ Root account is now secure. Use it ONLY for:
   - Changing account settings
   - Viewing billing
   - Closing the account
   - Restoring IAM user permissions (emergency)
Step 2: Create IAM Groups
Create Groups for anihpj Team
# Create Developers group
aws iam create-group --group-name anihpj-developers

# Create DevOps group
aws iam create-group --group-name anihpj-devops

# Create Admins group
aws iam create-group --group-name anihpj-admins

# Create ReadOnly group
aws iam create-group --group-name anihpj-readonly

# Verify groups created
aws iam list-groups
Step 3: Create Custom Policies
Create anihpj S3 Uploads Policy
# Create policy JSON file
cat > anihpj-s3-uploads-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "ListBucket",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:ListBucket", "s3:GetBucketLocation"],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "ObjectAccess",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Create the policy
aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-s3-uploads-policy \
    --policy-document file://anihpj-s3-uploads-policy.json \
    --description "Allow read/write access to anihpj uploads bucket"

# Output:
# {
#     "Policy": {
#         "PolicyName": "anihpj-s3-uploads-policy",
#         "PolicyId": "ANPA1234567890EXAMPLE",
#         "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-s3-uploads-policy",
#         "CreateDate": "2026-01-14T10:30:00Z"
#     }
# }
Create anihpj Developer Policy
cat > anihpj-developer-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "EC2DevStagingFull",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "ec2:*",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "ec2:ResourceTag/Environment": ["dev", "staging"]
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "EC2ProductionReadOnly",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["ec2:Describe*", "ec2:Get*"],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "RDSDevStaging",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["rds:Describe*", "rds-db:connect"],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringLike": {
          "rds:db-tag/Environment": ["dev", "staging"]
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CodeCommitAndBuild",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "codecommit:*",
        "codebuild:*",
        "codepipeline:*"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["logs:*", "cloudwatch:*"],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-developer-policy \
    --policy-document file://anihpj-developer-policy.json
Step 4: Attach Policies to Groups
# Attach to Developers group
aws iam attach-group-policy \
    --group-name anihpj-developers \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-developer-policy

aws iam attach-group-policy \
    --group-name anihpj-developers \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-s3-uploads-policy

# Attach to DevOps group (Administrator access)
aws iam attach-group-policy \
    --group-name anihpj-devops \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess

# Attach to Admins group
aws iam attach-group-policy \
    --group-name anihpj-admins \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess

# Attach to ReadOnly group
aws iam attach-group-policy \
    --group-name anihpj-readonly \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/ReadOnlyAccess

# Verify group policies
aws iam list-attached-group-policies --group-name anihpj-developers
Step 5: Create IAM Users
Create Users for anihpj Team
# Create developer user
aws iam create-user --user-name john.dev

# Create console password for john.dev
aws iam create-login-profile \
    --user-name john.dev \
    --password 'TempPassword123!' \
    --password-reset-required

# Add john.dev to Developers group
aws iam add-user-to-group \
    --user-name john.dev \
    --group-name anihpj-developers

# Create access keys for CLI/SDK access
aws iam create-access-key --user-name john.dev
# Save output: AccessKeyId and SecretAccessKey

# Enable MFA (user must do this in console first time)
# Then enforce MFA with policy

# Create more users
aws iam create-user --user-name sarah.devops
aws iam create-login-profile --user-name sarah.devops --password 'TempPassword456!' --password-reset-required
aws iam add-user-to-group --user-name sarah.devops --group-name anihpj-devops

aws iam create-user --user-name alice.admin
aws iam create-login-profile --user-name alice.admin --password 'TempPassword789!' --password-reset-required
aws iam add-user-to-group --user-name alice.admin --group-name anihpj-admins

# List all users
aws iam list-users
Step 6: Create Roles for Services
Create EC2 Role for Django Application
# Step 6a: Create trust policy (who can assume the role)
cat > ec2-trust-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Step 6b: Create the role
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://ec2-trust-policy.json \
    --description "Role for anihpj EC2 instances to access RDS and S3"

# Step 6c: Attach policies to role
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-s3-uploads-policy

# Attach AWS managed policies
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore

# Step 6d: Create instance profile (wrapper for role)
aws iam create-instance-profile --instance-profile-name anihpj-ec2-profile

# Step 6e: Add role to instance profile
aws iam add-role-to-instance-profile \
    --instance-profile-name anihpj-ec2-profile \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-role

# Step 6f: Launch EC2 with this role
aws ec2 run-instances \
    --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
    --instance-type t3.small \
    --iam-instance-profile Name=anihpj-ec2-profile \
    --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-web-server-1},{Key=Environment,Value=production}]'
Create Lambda Role for Email Notifications
# Lambda trust policy
cat > lambda-trust-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Create Lambda role
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-email-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://lambda-trust-policy.json

# Attach Lambda execution policy
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-email-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole

# Attach SES send email policy
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-email-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSESFullAccess
Step 7: Set Up Password Policy
# Configure account password policy
aws iam update-account-password-policy \
    --minimum-password-length 14 \
    --require-symbols \
    --require-numbers \
    --require-uppercase-characters \
    --require-lowercase-characters \
    --allow-users-to-change-password \
    --max-password-age 90 \
    --password-reuse-prevention 5 \
    --hard-expiry

# View current password policy
aws iam get-account-password-policy
Step 8: Test Permissions
Verify User Can Access Resources
# As john.dev user (after configuring AWS CLI with their credentials)
aws configure --profile john-dev
# AWS Access Key ID: (john.dev's access key)
# AWS Secret Access Key: (john.dev's secret key)
# Default region: us-east-1
# Default output format: json

# Test S3 access
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-uploads/ --profile john-dev
# Should work ✅

# Test uploading file
echo "Test file" > test.txt
aws s3 cp test.txt s3://anihpj-uploads/test.txt --profile john-dev
# Should work ✅

# Try to delete production EC2 instance (should fail)
aws ec2 terminate-instances \
    --instance-ids i-productioninstance \
    --profile john-dev
# Should fail ❌ (DenyProductionTermination policy)

# Simulate policy (test without actually executing)
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
    --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/john.dev \
    --action-names ec2:TerminateInstances \
    --resource-arns arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456789012:instance/i-productioninstance
💡 anihpj Quick Start Summary:
  1. Secure root account with MFA, no access keys
  2. Create groups: Developers, DevOps, Admins, ReadOnly
  3. Create custom policies for S3, EC2, RDS access
  4. Attach policies to groups
  5. Create IAM users for each team member
  6. Add users to appropriate groups
  7. Create roles for EC2, Lambda, ECS services
  8. Enforce strong password policy
  9. Enable MFA for all users
  10. Test permissions before granting production access

🔐 IAM Security & Best Practices

🛡️ Security First: IAM is the foundation of AWS security. Proper IAM configuration is critical for protecting the anihpj job portal and preventing unauthorized access to resources.

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

MFA adds an extra layer of security by requiring a second form of authentication beyond password.

📱
Virtual MFA Device

Use smartphone apps like Google Authenticator, Authy, or Microsoft Authenticator

  • Free and convenient
  • Time-based OTP (TOTP)
  • Best for most users
🔑
Hardware MFA Device

Physical devices like YubiKey or Gemalto tokens

  • Maximum security
  • Cannot be cloned
  • Recommended for admins
🔐
U2F Security Key

FIDO-certified hardware keys (YubiKey, Titan)

  • Phishing-resistant
  • One-touch authentication
  • Works with multiple services
Enable Virtual MFA for Root Account
# Enable virtual MFA for root account
# 1. Sign in to AWS Console as root user
# 2. Navigate to IAM → Dashboard → Security recommendations
# 3. Click "Add MFA" for root account
# 4. Select "Virtual MFA device"
# 5. Scan QR code with your MFA app (Google Authenticator, Authy)
# 6. Enter two consecutive MFA codes to verify

# Enable MFA for IAM users via CLI
aws iam enable-mfa-device \
    --user-name alice.admin \
    --serial-number arn:aws:iam::123456789012:mfa/alice.admin \
    --authentication-code-1 123456 \
    --authentication-code-2 789012

# List MFA devices for user
aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name alice.admin

Least Privilege Principle

Grant only the permissions required to perform a task. Start with minimal permissions and add more as needed.

⚠️ Common Mistake: Granting AdministratorAccess to developers "just to get started". This violates least privilege and creates security risks. Instead, start with specific policies and expand gradually.
anihpj Least Privilege Example: Junior Developer Policy
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "ReadOnlyAccessToDevResources",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:ListBucket",
        "dynamodb:GetItem",
        "dynamodb:Query",
        "dynamodb:Scan",
        "ec2:Describe*",
        "rds:Describe*",
        "logs:GetLogEvents",
        "logs:FilterLogEvents"
      ],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "aws:RequestedRegion": "us-east-1"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "WriteToDevS3Bucket",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-dev-uploads/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyProductionAccess",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": "*",
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-prod-*",
        "arn:aws:rds:*:*:db:anihpj-prod-db",
        "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:instance/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "StringLike": {
          "ec2:ResourceTag/Environment": "production"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

IAM Policy Simulator

Test IAM policies before deployment to ensure they work as expected without risking production systems.

Test Policy with Policy Simulator
# Test if user can perform specific actions
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
    --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/john.dev \
    --action-names s3:PutObject s3:DeleteObject ec2:TerminateInstances \
    --resource-arns \
        arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-dev-uploads/test.txt \
        arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456789012:instance/i-prod123

# Test custom policy document
aws iam simulate-custom-policy \
    --policy-input-list file://junior-dev-policy.json \
    --action-names s3:PutObject dynamodb:GetItem ec2:TerminateInstances \
    --resource-arns \
        arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-dev-uploads/* \
        arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:123456789012:table/anihpj-jobs \
        arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456789012:instance/i-prod123

# Expected output shows allowed/denied for each action
# ✅ s3:PutObject on dev bucket: allowed
# ✅ dynamodb:GetItem: allowed  
# ❌ ec2:TerminateInstances on prod: denied
💡 Policy Simulator Use Cases for anihpj:
  1. Before granting access: Test if new developer policy allows S3 uploads but blocks production DB access
  2. Troubleshooting: User reports "Access Denied" - simulate to identify missing permissions
  3. Security audit: Verify that contractors cannot access production resources
  4. Policy changes: Test modified policies before deployment to avoid breaking existing workflows

IAM Access Analyzer

Continuously analyzes resource policies to identify resources shared with external entities, helping you maintain least privilege.

Enable and Use IAM Access Analyzer
# Create Access Analyzer (one per region)
aws accessanalyzer create-analyzer \
    --analyzer-name anihpj-access-analyzer \
    --type ACCOUNT \
    --region us-east-1

# List all analyzers
aws accessanalyzer list-analyzers

# Get findings (resources accessible outside your account)
aws accessanalyzer list-findings \
    --analyzer-arn arn:aws:access-analyzer:us-east-1:123456789012:analyzer/anihpj-access-analyzer

# Example finding: S3 bucket policy allows public read access
# {
#   "id": "12345",
#   "resourceType": "AWS::S3::Bucket",
#   "resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads",
#   "condition": {
#     "Principal": "*"
#   },
#   "action": ["s3:GetObject"],
#   "status": "ACTIVE"
# }

# Archive finding (mark as reviewed/accepted risk)
aws accessanalyzer update-findings \
    --analyzer-arn arn:aws:access-analyzer:us-east-1:123456789012:analyzer/anihpj-access-analyzer \
    --ids 12345 \
    --status ARCHIVED

# Validate policy before deployment
aws accessanalyzer validate-policy \
    --policy-document file://s3-bucket-policy.json \
    --policy-type RESOURCE_POLICY \
    --resource-type AWS::S3::Bucket

Credential Reports & Auditing

Generate regular credential reports to audit IAM users, access keys, MFA status, and password age.

Generate and Analyze Credential Report
# Generate credential report (takes a few seconds)
aws iam generate-credential-report

# Download credential report (CSV format)
aws iam get-credential-report --output text --query 'Content' | base64 -d > credential-report.csv

# View credential report
cat credential-report.csv

# Example output shows for each user:
# - user_name: john.dev
# - arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/john.dev
# - user_creation_time: 2024-01-15T10:30:00Z
# - password_enabled: true
# - password_last_used: 2024-03-20T14:25:00Z
# - password_last_changed: 2024-01-15T10:35:00Z
# - password_next_rotation: 2024-04-15T10:35:00Z
# - mfa_active: true
# - access_key_1_active: true
# - access_key_1_last_rotated: 2024-01-15T10:40:00Z
# - access_key_1_last_used_date: 2024-03-20T15:10:00Z
# - access_key_2_active: false

# Get last access details for services
aws iam get-service-last-accessed-details \
    --job-id $JOB_ID

# List users without MFA (security risk!)
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[*].[UserName,UserId]' --output text | while read user id; do
    mfa=$(aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name "$user" --query 'MFADevices' --output text)
    if [ -z "$mfa" ]; then
        echo "⚠️ $user does not have MFA enabled"
    fi
done

Service Control Policies (SCPs)

If using AWS Organizations, SCPs provide centralized control over maximum permissions for accounts in your organization.

📋 SCPs vs IAM Policies:
  • SCPs: Set maximum permissions for entire AWS accounts (apply at organization/OU level)
  • IAM Policies: Grant specific permissions to users/roles within an account
  • Effective permissions: Intersection of SCPs AND IAM policies (both must allow)
Example SCP: Prevent Region Restriction for anihpj
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "DenyAllOutsideAllowedRegions",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": "*",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringNotEquals": {
          "aws:RequestedRegion": [
            "us-east-1",
            "us-west-2"
          ]
        },
        "ArnNotLike": {
          "aws:PrincipalArn": [
            "arn:aws:iam::*:role/OrganizationAccountAccessRole"
          ]
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyRootAccountUsage",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": "*",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringLike": {
          "aws:PrincipalArn": "arn:aws:iam::*:root"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyEC2WithoutEncryption",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": [
        "ec2:RunInstances"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:volume/*",
      "Condition": {
        "Bool": {
          "ec2:Encrypted": "false"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyS3UnencryptedUploads",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": "s3:PutObject",
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringNotEquals": {
          "s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption": [
            "AES256",
            "aws:kms"
          ]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

Permission Boundaries

Advanced feature to set maximum permissions for IAM entities, preventing privilege escalation.

Create Permission Boundary for anihpj Developers
# Create permission boundary policy
cat > developer-boundary.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowedServices",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:*",
        "dynamodb:*",
        "lambda:*",
        "logs:*",
        "cloudwatch:*",
        "apigateway:*"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyIAMChanges",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": [
        "iam:CreateUser",
        "iam:DeleteUser",
        "iam:CreateRole",
        "iam:DeleteRole",
        "iam:AttachUserPolicy",
        "iam:AttachRolePolicy",
        "iam:PutUserPermissionsBoundary",
        "iam:DeleteUserPermissionsBoundary"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyBillingAccess",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": [
        "aws-portal:*",
        "budgets:*"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Create the policy
aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-developer-boundary \
    --policy-document file://developer-boundary.json

# Apply permission boundary to user
aws iam put-user-permissions-boundary \
    --user-name john.dev \
    --permissions-boundary arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-developer-boundary

# Apply permission boundary to role
aws iam put-role-permissions-boundary \
    --role-name anihpj-lambda-role \
    --permissions-boundary arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-developer-boundary

# Now even if john.dev has AdministratorAccess policy, 
# they can only perform actions allowed by the boundary

🛡️ IAM Security Checklist for anihpj

Security Item Priority Action Required
🔐 Root Account MFA CRITICAL Enable MFA on root account immediately
🔑 Root Access Keys CRITICAL Delete all root account access keys
👥 User MFA HIGH Require MFA for all IAM users, especially admins
📜 Least Privilege HIGH Review and restrict permissions to minimum required
🔄 Access Key Rotation HIGH Rotate access keys every 90 days
🔍 Access Analyzer MEDIUM Enable IAM Access Analyzer in all regions
📊 Credential Reports MEDIUM Review credential report monthly
🚫 Unused Credentials MEDIUM Disable credentials unused for 90+ days
📝 CloudTrail Logging HIGH Enable CloudTrail for all IAM API calls
🔒 Password Policy HIGH Enforce strong password policy (14+ chars, rotation)
🎭 Use IAM Roles HIGH Prefer roles over access keys for applications
🧪 Policy Simulator LOW Test policies before deployment
🏢 AWS Organizations MEDIUM Use SCPs for multi-account governance
🚪 Permission Boundaries MEDIUM Set boundaries for developers who create roles
📦 Service-Specific Policies LOW Use AWS managed policies as starting point
✅ anihpj Security Baseline Achieved:
  • Root account secured with MFA, no access keys
  • All IAM users organized into groups with least privilege
  • MFA enforced for alice.admin and sarah.devops
  • IAM roles used for EC2, Lambda, ECS services
  • Access Analyzer enabled in us-east-1
  • CloudTrail logging all IAM API calls
  • Credential reports reviewed monthly
  • Policy simulator used before production changes

🎯 Real-World Use Cases for anihpj

Practical scenarios demonstrating how IAM solves common access management challenges for the anihpj Django job portal.

🎓 Use Case 1: Onboarding New Developer

Scenario: New junior developer "mike.junior" joins the anihpj team. Need to grant appropriate access without over-privileging.

Step-by-Step: Onboard mike.junior
# Step 1: Create IAM user
aws iam create-user --user-name mike.junior

# Step 2: Add user to Developers group
aws iam add-user-to-group \
    --user-name mike.junior \
    --group-name anihpj-developers

# Step 3: Create login profile with temporary password
aws iam create-login-profile \
    --user-name mike.junior \
    --password "TempPassword123!" \
    --password-reset-required

# Step 4: Generate access key for CLI/SDK access
aws iam create-access-key --user-name mike.junior > mike-access-key.json

# Step 5: Tag user for better management
aws iam tag-user \
    --user-name mike.junior \
    --tags Key=Team,Value=Backend Key=Environment,Value=Development Key=OnboardDate,Value=2024-03-20

# Step 6: Send welcome email with:
# - AWS Console URL: https://anihpj.signin.aws.amazon.com/console
# - Username: mike.junior
# - Temporary password: TempPassword123!
# - Instructions to enable MFA immediately
# - Access key ID and secret (from mike-access-key.json)

# Step 7: Verify access (as mike.junior)
aws configure --profile mike-junior
# AWS Access Key ID: AKIAEXAMPLEMIKE
# AWS Secret Access Key: (from mike-access-key.json)

# Test S3 access (should work ✅)
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-dev-uploads/ --profile mike-junior

# Test production access (should fail ❌)
aws s3 ls s3://anihpj-prod-uploads/ --profile mike-junior
# Error: Access Denied (DenyProductionAccess policy)
📋 What mike.junior Can Do:
  • ✅ Read/write to dev S3 bucket (anihpj-dev-uploads)
  • ✅ Query/scan DynamoDB tables in development
  • ✅ View EC2 instances and RDS databases (read-only)
  • ✅ Deploy Lambda functions to dev environment
  • ❌ Cannot access production resources
  • ❌ Cannot create/delete IAM users or roles
  • ❌ Cannot modify security groups or VPC settings

🖥️ Use Case 2: EC2 Instance Accessing S3 & RDS

Scenario: anihpj Django application on EC2 needs to upload job posting images to S3 and connect to RDS database.

Setup: EC2 Role with S3 and RDS Access
# Step 1: Create trust policy for EC2
cat > ec2-trust-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Step 2: Create the role
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-app-server-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://ec2-trust-policy.json \
    --description "Role for anihpj Django application servers"

# Step 3: Create custom policy for S3 and RDS access
cat > anihpj-app-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "S3UploadJobPostingImages",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/job-postings/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption": "AES256"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "S3ListBucket",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "s3:ListBucket",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads",
      "Condition": {
        "StringLike": {
          "s3:prefix": "job-postings/*"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "RDSConnect",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "rds-db:connect"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:rds-db:us-east-1:123456789012:dbuser:*/anihpj_app"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "logs:CreateLogGroup",
        "logs:CreateLogStream",
        "logs:PutLogEvents"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/anihpj/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "SecretsManagerReadDBPassword",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "secretsmanager:GetSecretValue"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:anihpj/rds/password-*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Step 4: Create and attach policy
aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-app-server-policy \
    --policy-document file://anihpj-app-policy.json

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-app-server-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-app-server-policy

# Step 5: Create instance profile
aws iam create-instance-profile \
    --instance-profile-name anihpj-app-server-profile

aws iam add-role-to-instance-profile \
    --instance-profile-name anihpj-app-server-profile \
    --role-name anihpj-app-server-role

# Step 6: Launch EC2 with IAM role
aws ec2 run-instances \
    --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
    --instance-type t3.medium \
    --iam-instance-profile Name=anihpj-app-server-profile \
    --key-name anihpj-key \
    --security-group-ids sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0 \
    --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-app-server},{Key=Environment,Value=production}]'

# Step 7: From within EC2 instance - No AWS credentials needed!
# Django application automatically uses instance role

# Python code in Django app (views.py):
# import boto3
# s3 = boto3.client('s3')  # Automatically uses instance role
# s3.upload_file('/tmp/job-image.jpg', 'anihpj-uploads', 'job-postings/12345.jpg')

📧 Use Case 3: Lambda Function Sending Application Emails

Scenario: Lambda function sends confirmation emails via SES when users apply for jobs on anihpj portal.

Setup: Lambda Role with SES, DynamoDB, and Logs
# Step 1: Create Lambda execution role
cat > lambda-trust-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-email-sender-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://lambda-trust-policy.json

# Step 2: Create custom policy
cat > lambda-email-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "SESSendEmail",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ses:SendEmail",
        "ses:SendRawEmail"
      ],
      "Resource": "*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "ses:FromAddress": "notifications@anihpj.com"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DynamoDBReadApplications",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "dynamodb:GetItem",
        "dynamodb:Query"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:123456789012:table/anihpj-applications"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "CloudWatchLogsWrite",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "logs:CreateLogGroup",
        "logs:CreateLogStream",
        "logs:PutLogEvents"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:logs:us-east-1:123456789012:log-group:/aws/lambda/anihpj-*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-email-sender-policy \
    --policy-document file://lambda-email-policy.json

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-email-sender-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-email-sender-policy

# Step 3: Deploy Lambda function
aws lambda create-function \
    --function-name anihpj-send-application-email \
    --runtime python3.11 \
    --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-email-sender-role \
    --handler lambda_function.lambda_handler \
    --zip-file fileb://function.zip \
    --environment Variables={FROM_EMAIL=notifications@anihpj.com}

# Lambda function code automatically uses role permissions - no hardcoded credentials!

🔄 Use Case 4: Cross-Account Deployment (Dev → Prod)

Scenario: DevOps team in dev account (111111111111) needs to deploy to production account (222222222222).

Setup: Cross-Account IAM Role
# ===== IN PRODUCTION ACCOUNT (222222222222) =====

# Step 1: Create cross-account trust policy
cat > prod-cross-account-trust.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:root"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "sts:ExternalId": "anihpj-secure-external-id-12345"
        },
        "IpAddress": {
          "aws:SourceIp": ["203.0.113.0/24"]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Step 2: Create role in production account
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-cross-account-deploy \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://prod-cross-account-trust.json \
    --description "Allow dev account to deploy to production"

# Step 3: Attach deployment permissions
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-cross-account-deploy \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AWSCodeDeployDeployerAccess

cat > prod-deploy-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "S3DeploymentArtifacts",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:GetObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-prod-deployments/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "ECSUpdateService",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ecs:UpdateService",
        "ecs:DescribeServices",
        "ecs:RegisterTaskDefinition"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-prod-deploy-policy \
    --policy-document file://prod-deploy-policy.json

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-cross-account-deploy \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::222222222222:policy/anihpj-prod-deploy-policy


# ===== IN DEV ACCOUNT (111111111111) =====

# Step 4: Grant sarah.devops permission to assume the role
cat > assume-prod-role-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/anihpj-cross-account-deploy"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-assume-prod-role \
    --policy-document file://assume-prod-role-policy.json

aws iam attach-user-policy \
    --user-name sarah.devops \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::111111111111:policy/anihpj-assume-prod-role


# ===== USAGE: sarah.devops Deploys to Production =====

# Step 5: Assume cross-account role
aws sts assume-role \
    --role-arn arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/anihpj-cross-account-deploy \
    --role-session-name sarah-prod-deployment \
    --external-id anihpj-secure-external-id-12345 \
    --duration-seconds 3600 > assumed-role-credentials.json

# Extract credentials
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$(cat assumed-role-credentials.json | jq -r '.Credentials.AccessKeyId')
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(cat assumed-role-credentials.json | jq -r '.Credentials.SecretAccessKey')
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=$(cat assumed-role-credentials.json | jq -r '.Credentials.SessionToken')

# Step 6: Now deploy to production (using assumed role)
aws ecs update-service \
    --cluster anihpj-prod-cluster \
    --service anihpj-web-service \
    --task-definition anihpj-web:25 \
    --force-new-deployment

# Deployment successful! ✅

🔗 Use Case 5: GitHub Actions with OIDC (No Access Keys!)

Scenario: GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline needs AWS access without storing long-term credentials.

Setup: OIDC Trust for GitHub Actions
# Step 1: Create OIDC identity provider for GitHub
aws iam create-open-id-connect-provider \
    --url https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com \
    --client-id-list sts.amazonaws.com \
    --thumbprint-list 6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1

# Step 2: Create trust policy for GitHub Actions
cat > github-actions-trust.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud": "sts.amazonaws.com"
        },
        "StringLike": {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub": "repo:anihpj/anihpj-backend:*"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
EOF

# Step 3: Create role for GitHub Actions
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-github-actions-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://github-actions-trust.json

# Step 4: Attach deployment permissions
aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-github-actions-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPowerUser

cat > github-deploy-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:GetObject"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-artifacts/*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "ecs:UpdateService",
        "ecs:DescribeServices"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-github-deploy \
    --policy-document file://github-deploy-policy.json

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-github-actions-role \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-github-deploy

# Step 5: GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/deploy.yml)
cat > .github/workflows/deploy.yml << 'EOF'
name: Deploy anihpj Backend
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Configure AWS credentials (OIDC - No keys!)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v2
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-github-actions-role
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Login to Amazon ECR
        run: |
          aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
          docker login --username AWS --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

      - name: Build and push Docker image
        run: |
          docker build -t anihpj-backend .
          docker tag anihpj-backend:latest 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-backend:latest
          docker push 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/anihpj-backend:latest

      - name: Deploy to ECS
        run: |
          aws ecs update-service \
            --cluster anihpj-cluster \
            --service anihpj-backend \
            --force-new-deployment
EOF

# ✅ No AWS access keys stored in GitHub secrets!
# ✅ Temporary credentials issued per workflow run
# ✅ Automatically expires after deployment

⏱️ Use Case 6: Temporary Credentials for Contractors

Scenario: External contractor needs 2-hour access to debug production logs without creating permanent IAM user.

Generate Temporary Credentials with STS
# Step 1: Create read-only role for contractors
cat > contractor-trust.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/alice.admin"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole",
      "Condition": {
        "IpAddress": {
          "aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.50/32"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-contractor-readonly \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://contractor-trust.json \
    --max-session-duration 7200

# Step 2: Attach read-only permissions
cat > contractor-policy.json << 'EOF'
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "logs:GetLogEvents",
        "logs:FilterLogEvents",
        "logs:DescribeLogStreams",
        "cloudwatch:GetMetricData",
        "cloudwatch:GetMetricStatistics",
        "ec2:Describe*",
        "rds:Describe*"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Action": [
        "logs:DeleteLogGroup",
        "logs:DeleteLogStream",
        "ec2:*",
        "rds:*"
      ],
      "Resource": "*"
    }
  ]
}
EOF

aws iam create-policy \
    --policy-name anihpj-contractor-readonly \
    --policy-document file://contractor-policy.json

aws iam attach-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-contractor-readonly \
    --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-contractor-readonly

# Step 3: Generate temporary credentials (as alice.admin)
aws sts assume-role \
    --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-contractor-readonly \
    --role-session-name contractor-john-debug-session \
    --duration-seconds 7200 \
    --external-id anihpj-contractor-2024-03-20 > temp-creds.json

# Step 4: Extract and share credentials (expires in 2 hours)
echo "=== TEMPORARY AWS CREDENTIALS (Valid for 2 hours) ==="
cat temp-creds.json | jq -r '.Credentials | 
  "export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=" + .AccessKeyId,
  "export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=" + .SecretAccessKey,
  "export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=" + .SessionToken'

# Step 5: Contractor uses temporary credentials
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=ASIAEXAMPLETEMPCREDS
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=(from output above)
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=(from output above)

# View logs (works ✅)
aws logs tail /aws/anihpj/application --follow

# Try to delete logs (fails ❌)
aws logs delete-log-group --log-group-name /aws/anihpj/application
# Error: Access Denied

# After 2 hours, credentials automatically expire ⏱️

📋 Use Case 7: Compliance Audit Preparation

Scenario: anihpj needs to demonstrate IAM compliance for SOC 2 / ISO 27001 audit.

Generate Compliance Reports
# Step 1: Generate credential report
aws iam generate-credential-report
sleep 5  # Wait for report generation
aws iam get-credential-report --output text --query 'Content' | base64 -d > iam-credentials-$(date +%Y%m%d).csv

# Step 2: List all users and their MFA status
echo "=== USER MFA STATUS REPORT ===" > iam-compliance-report.txt
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[*].[UserName]' --output text | while read user; do
    echo -n "$user: " >> iam-compliance-report.txt
    mfa_devices=$(aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name "$user" --query 'MFADevices[*].SerialNumber' --output text)
    if [ -z "$mfa_devices" ]; then
        echo "❌ NO MFA" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
    else
        echo "✅ MFA Enabled ($mfa_devices)" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
    fi
done

# Step 3: List all roles and their trust relationships
echo -e "\n=== IAM ROLES TRUST POLICIES ===" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[*].[RoleName]' --output text | while read role; do
    echo -e "\nRole: $role" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
    aws iam get-role --role-name "$role" --query 'Role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument' >> iam-compliance-report.txt
done

# Step 4: Check for unused credentials (90+ days)
echo -e "\n=== UNUSED CREDENTIALS (90+ days) ===" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[*].[UserName]' --output text | while read user; do
    access_keys=$(aws iam list-access-keys --user-name "$user" --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[*].[AccessKeyId,CreateDate]' --output text)
    if [ ! -z "$access_keys" ]; then
        echo "$access_keys" | while read key_id create_date; do
            last_used=$(aws iam get-access-key-last-used --access-key-id "$key_id" --query 'AccessKeyLastUsed.LastUsedDate' --output text 2>/dev/null)
            if [ -z "$last_used" ] || [ "$last_used" == "None" ]; then
                echo "⚠️ $user: Key $key_id never used (created $create_date)" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
            fi
        done
    fi
done

# Step 5: Check for overly permissive policies
echo -e "\n=== OVERLY PERMISSIVE POLICIES ===" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
aws iam list-policies --scope Local --query 'Policies[*].[PolicyName,Arn]' --output text | while read policy_name policy_arn; do
    policy_doc=$(aws iam get-policy-version \
        --policy-arn "$policy_arn" \
        --version-id $(aws iam get-policy --policy-arn "$policy_arn" --query 'Policy.DefaultVersionId' --output text) \
        --query 'PolicyVersion.Document' --output json)
    
    # Check for wildcards
    wildcards=$(echo "$policy_doc" | jq '[.Statement[] | select(.Action == "*" or .Resource == "*")] | length')
    if [ "$wildcards" -gt 0 ]; then
        echo "⚠️ $policy_name: Contains wildcard permissions" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
    fi
done

# Step 6: IAM Access Analyzer findings
echo -e "\n=== IAM ACCESS ANALYZER FINDINGS ===" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
aws accessanalyzer list-findings \
    --analyzer-arn arn:aws:access-analyzer:us-east-1:123456789012:analyzer/anihpj-access-analyzer \
    --query 'findings[?status==`ACTIVE`].[resourceType,resource,principal]' \
    --output table >> iam-compliance-report.txt

# Step 7: Password policy compliance
echo -e "\n=== PASSWORD POLICY ===" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
aws iam get-account-password-policy >> iam-compliance-report.txt

# Step 8: Root account usage (should be ZERO)
echo -e "\n=== ROOT ACCOUNT USAGE (Last 90 days) ===" >> iam-compliance-report.txt
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
    --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root \
    --start-time $(date -d '90 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d) \
    --query 'Events[*].[EventTime,EventName]' \
    --output table >> iam-compliance-report.txt

echo -e "\n✅ Compliance report generated: iam-compliance-report.txt"
echo "📧 Send to auditors along with credential report CSV"
✅ Real-World IAM Benefits for anihpj:
  • Security: No hardcoded credentials in code, IAM roles for services, MFA enforcement
  • Scalability: Easy onboarding (users → groups), role-based access, permission boundaries
  • Auditability: Credential reports, CloudTrail integration, Access Analyzer findings
  • Cost: IAM is 100% FREE, no additional charges for users, roles, or policies
  • Automation: GitHub Actions OIDC, cross-account deployments, temporary credentials
  • Compliance: Automated compliance reports, audit trails, least privilege enforcement

💰 IAM Pricing & Cost Optimization

🎉

AWS IAM is 100% FREE!

No charges for IAM users, groups, roles, or policies

Create unlimited IAM entities for the anihpj job portal at no cost

IAM Core Features - Always Free

Feature Limit Cost
👤 IAM Users 5,000 per AWS account FREE
👥 IAM Groups 300 per AWS account FREE
🎭 IAM Roles 5,000 per AWS account FREE
📜 Customer Managed Policies 1,500 per AWS account FREE
🔑 Access Keys 2 per IAM user FREE
🔐 MFA Devices 8 per IAM user/root FREE
🔒 Policy Simulator Unlimited simulations FREE
📊 Credential Reports Unlimited reports FREE

Related AWS Services - Pricing

While IAM itself is free, some related identity and security services have costs:

✅ AWS Organizations

FREE

  • Centralized account management
  • Service Control Policies (SCPs)
  • Consolidated billing
  • No limit on accounts
✅ IAM Access Analyzer

FREE

  • External access findings
  • Unused access analysis
  • Policy validation
  • Continuous monitoring
💰 AWS IAM Identity Center

FREE + SSO costs

  • SSO service: FREE
  • External IdP (Okta, Azure AD): Variable
  • Up to 50 users: FREE with directory
  • Additional users: $0.015/user/month
💸 AWS CloudTrail

First trail FREE

  • First trail: FREE (90 days)
  • Additional trails: $2.00 per 100,000 events
  • S3 storage: Standard S3 pricing
  • CloudTrail Insights: $0.35 per 100,000 events
💸 AWS Secrets Manager

$0.40 per secret/month

  • $0.40 per secret per month
  • $0.05 per 10,000 API calls
  • 30-day free trial for new secrets
  • Automatic rotation included
📦 AWS Systems Manager (Parameter Store)

FREE (Standard)

  • Standard parameters: FREE
  • Up to 10,000 parameters
  • Advanced: $0.05 per parameter/month
  • Higher throughput available

💡 Cost Implications of IAM Decisions

While IAM is free, your IAM configuration can significantly impact costs in other AWS services:

Cost Scenario 1: Overly Permissive S3 Policies
# ❌ BAD: Public read access → unexpected data transfer costs
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "s3:GetObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*"
    }
  ]
}

# If bucket gets 10 TB of unauthorized downloads:
# - Data transfer out: 10 TB × $0.09/GB = $900/month 💸
# - GET requests: 10M requests × $0.0004/1000 = $40/month 💸
# Total unexpected cost: $940/month!

# ✅ GOOD: Restrict to authenticated users only
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ec2-role"
      },
      "Action": "s3:GetObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*"
    }
  ]
}

# Only anihpj application can access → controlled costs ✅
Cost Scenario 2: Over-Provisioned EC2 Instances
# ❌ BAD: Developer has ec2:* permission
# - Can launch expensive r5.24xlarge instances ($6.048/hour)
# - Forgets to terminate after testing
# - Runs for 1 month: $6.048 × 730 hours = $4,415 💸

# ✅ GOOD: Restrict instance types and enforce auto-termination tags
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "ec2:RunInstances",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:instance/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "ec2:InstanceType": [
            "t3.micro",
            "t3.small",
            "t3.medium"
          ]
        },
        "StringLike": {
          "aws:RequestTag/Environment": "development"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": "ec2:RunInstances",
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:subnet/*",
        "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:security-group/*",
        "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:key-pair/*",
        "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:volume/*",
        "arn:aws:ec2:*::image/*"
      ]
    }
  ]
}

# Developer limited to t3.medium max ($0.0416/hour)
# Even if left running: $0.0416 × 730 = $30.37/month (92% savings!) ✅
Cost Scenario 3: Hardcoded Credentials Leaked
# ❌ DISASTER: Access keys committed to public GitHub repo
# - Crypto miners find keys within minutes
# - Launch hundreds of p3.16xlarge GPU instances ($24.48/hour each)
# - Run for 1 week before detected
# - Cost: 100 instances × $24.48 × 168 hours = $411,264 💸💸💸

# ✅ PREVENTION: Use IAM roles instead of access keys
# - EC2 instances use IAM roles (no keys to leak)
# - Lambda functions use execution roles (no keys to leak)
# - GitHub Actions uses OIDC (no long-term credentials)
# - If breach occurs, role can be instantly revoked

# Additional safeguards:
# - Enable AWS Budgets alerts (notify at $100, $500, $1000)
# - Set up CloudWatch billing alarms
# - Use SCPs to deny expensive instance types
# - Enable CloudTrail for audit logging

🎯 Cost Optimization Strategies for anihpj

Strategy IAM Implementation Cost Impact
Use IAM Roles for Services Attach roles to EC2, Lambda, ECS instead of embedding access keys Prevents leaked key incidents ($0 - $500K saved)
Least Privilege Policies Restrict developers to t3.* instances, deny production deletion Prevents accidental expensive launches (80-95% savings)
Temporary Credentials Use STS AssumeRole with 2-8 hour expiration Limits damage window if compromised (98% risk reduction)
Resource Tagging Enforcement Require Environment, Owner, CostCenter tags on all resources Enables cost tracking and auto-shutdown (20-40% savings)
Region Restrictions Use SCPs to deny resource creation outside us-east-1, us-west-2 Prevents accidental high-cost region usage (5-15% savings)
S3 Access Restrictions Block public access, require authenticated requests Eliminates unauthorized data transfer costs ($0-$10K saved)
MFA for Sensitive Operations Require MFA for production deletions, billing changes Prevents accidental data/resource deletion (immeasurable)
Permission Boundaries Set maximum permissions for developers who create roles Prevents privilege escalation (eliminates backdoor risk)
💡 anihpj Cost Optimization Example:

By implementing least privilege IAM policies and using roles instead of access keys, anihpj reduced their AWS bill from $8,500/month to $2,100/month (75% savings!):

  • $4,200 saved: Restricted developers to t3.medium, prevented accidental m5.24xlarge launches
  • $1,500 saved: Blocked public S3 access, eliminated unauthorized data transfer
  • $600 saved: Auto-shutdown dev instances after 6pm using tagged resources
  • $100 saved: Consolidated IAM users, removed unused access keys

📊 anihpj Monthly Cost Breakdown (IAM-Related)

Service Usage Monthly Cost
IAM (Users, Roles, Policies) 15 users, 25 roles, 40 policies $0.00
IAM Access Analyzer Continuous monitoring, 2 regions $0.00
AWS Organizations 3 AWS accounts, SCPs enabled $0.00
CloudTrail (IAM API logging) First trail, 500K events/month $0.00
S3 (CloudTrail logs storage) 10 GB logs $0.23
Secrets Manager 5 secrets (RDS passwords, API keys) $2.00
Parameter Store (Standard) 50 parameters $0.00
TOTAL IAM-RELATED COSTS $2.23/month
✅ IAM Pricing Summary for anihpj:
  • 🎉 IAM Core Features: 100% FREE (users, roles, groups, policies, MFA, Access Analyzer)
  • 💰 Total IAM-related costs: $2.23/month (Secrets Manager + S3 storage)
  • 💸 Cost savings from IAM best practices: $6,400/month (75% reduction)
  • 📊 ROI of IAM implementation: $6,400 saved monthly for $2.23 spent = 287,000% ROI!
  • 🛡️ Security value: Priceless - prevents credential theft, data breaches, compliance violations

🛠️ IAM Tools & Utilities

Tool Description Use Case
🧪 IAM Policy Simulator
policysim.aws.amazon.com		 Test IAM policies before deployment without affecting production Verify developer can upload to S3 but not delete production EC2
📝 AWS Policy Generator
awspolicygen.s3.amazonaws.com		 Visual tool to generate IAM, S3, VPC, and SQS policies Create S3 bucket policy allowing EC2 role access
🔍 IAM Policy Validator
GitHub - cfn-policy-validator		 Validate IAM policies for syntax errors, security issues CI/CD pipeline validation before deployment
📊 AWS IAM Access Analyzer
AWS Console → IAM → Access Analyzer		 Identify resources shared with external entities Find S3 buckets accessible by public or other AWS accounts
🔐 AWS Secrets Manager
aws.amazon.com/secrets-manager		 Securely store, rotate, and manage database credentials and API keys Store RDS password, auto-rotate every 30 days
📦 AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store
Parameter Store Docs		 Free hierarchical parameter storage with IAM integration Store /anihpj/prod/db_host, /anihpj/dev/api_url
🔄 AWS CLI
