🐍 Boto3 (AWS SDK for Python)
boto3 IAM Documentation		 Python library for IAM automation in Django apps anihpj backend creates temporary S3 upload URLs

📖 IAM Glossary

Term Definition
❌ Access Denied Missing IAM permission Use Policy Simulator to identify missing action/resource
Principal Entity that can make requests to AWS (IAM user, role, AWS service, or AWS account)
Action Specific operation allowed or denied (e.g., s3:PutObject, ec2:RunInstances)
Resource AWS object that the action affects, specified by ARN (e.g., arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*)
ARN Amazon Resource Name - unique identifier for AWS resources (e.g., arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ec2-role)
Trust Policy JSON document defining who can assume an IAM role (attached to roles only)
STS AWS Security Token Service - generates temporary credentials for IAM roles
AssumeRole STS API call to obtain temporary credentials for an IAM role
Session Temporary credentials period when assuming a role (configurable 15min - 12 hours)
External ID Secret identifier used in cross-account roles to prevent "confused deputy" attacks
MFA Multi-Factor Authentication - second authentication factor (TOTP, hardware token, or U2F)
TOTP Time-based One-Time Password - MFA codes from apps like Google Authenticator
❌ Session Expired Temporary credentials expired Re-run aws sts assume-role to get new credentials
❌ InvalidAccessKeyId Access key deleted or typo Create new access key: aws iam create-access-key
❌ User Already Exists Username conflict Check existing users: aws iam list-users
❌ Policy Too Large Exceeds 6,144 chars (managed) or 10,240 (inline) Split into multiple managed policies
❌ Cannot Delete User User has attached policies, access keys, MFA Detach all policies, delete keys/MFA first
❌ Implicit Deny No explicit Allow statement Add Allow statement for required action
❌ Explicit Deny Wins Deny statement overrides Allow Remove Deny or add condition to exclude specific case

❓ Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Q: Should I use IAM users or IAM roles for my anihpj application?

A: Always use IAM roles for applications running on AWS services (EC2, Lambda, ECS). Only use IAM users for human team members. Roles provide temporary credentials and don't require storing access keys in your code.

Q: How often should I rotate access keys?

A: Every 90 days is the AWS recommendation. Better yet, eliminate long-term access keys entirely by using IAM roles or temporary credentials via STS.

Q: Can I grant temporary access to external contractors?

A: Yes! Use STS AssumeRole to generate temporary credentials (2-12 hours). Create a role with read-only permissions and allow your admin user to assume it. Share the temporary credentials with the contractor - they'll automatically expire.

Q: What's the difference between IAM and AWS Organizations?

A: IAM manages users/roles within a single AWS account. AWS Organizations manages multiple AWS accounts and uses Service Control Policies (SCPs) to set maximum permissions across accounts. Use both together for multi-account governance.

Q: How can I allow GitHub Actions to deploy without storing AWS keys?

A: Use OIDC (OpenID Connect) with GitHub Actions. Create an IAM role that trusts GitHub's OIDC provider, then configure GitHub Actions to assume that role. No long-term credentials needed!

Q: Should I use AWS managed policies or create custom policies?

A: Start with AWS managed policies for common scenarios (e.g., AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess). Create custom policies when you need specific resource restrictions (e.g., only access anihpj-uploads/* bucket).

Q: What happens if I lose my root account password?

A: Use the "Forgot password" link on the sign-in page. AWS will send a password reset link to the root account email. This is why it's critical to maintain access to the root account email address.

Q: Can I restrict IAM users to specific IP addresses?

A: Yes! Use the aws:SourceIp condition in your policies. Example: "Condition": {"IpAddress": {"aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.0/24"}} - This forces users to connect from your office IP range.

Q: How do I prevent developers from creating expensive EC2 instances?

A: Use the ec2:InstanceType condition to restrict allowed types. Example: Only allow ["t3.micro", "t3.small", "t3.medium"]. Developers can't launch m5.24xlarge or other expensive types.

Q: What's a permission boundary and when should I use one?

A: A permission boundary sets the maximum permissions for an IAM entity, even if policies grant more. Use when allowing developers to create their own roles - the boundary prevents them from escalating privileges beyond what you allow.

Q: Can I share IAM roles across AWS accounts?

A: Yes! Cross-account roles allow users in Account A to assume a role in Account B. Update the role's trust policy to include the external account ARN. Always use External ID for additional security.

Q: How do I audit who accessed what resources?

A: Enable AWS CloudTrail to log all API calls. Use IAM Access Analyzer for continuous monitoring. Generate credential reports monthly. Set up CloudWatch alarms for suspicious activity (e.g., root account usage).

Q: What's the difference between resource-based and identity-based policies?

A: Identity-based policies attach to users/roles (IAM policies). Resource-based policies attach to resources (S3 bucket policies, SNS topic policies). Use identity-based for most cases; resource-based for cross-account access.

Q: Is it safe to use AdministratorAccess for development?

A: No! Even in dev, follow least privilege. If credentials leak or mistakes happen, AdministratorAccess can delete production databases, rack up huge bills, or cause major outages. Grant only what's needed.

Q: How many IAM users/roles can I create?

A: 5,000 users and 5,000 roles per AWS account (soft limits, can be increased). 300 groups and 1,500 customer-managed policies. If you hit these limits, consider AWS IAM Identity Center or multiple accounts.

📖 IAM Glossary

Principal Entity that can make requests to AWS (IAM user, role, AWS service, or AWS account)
Action Specific operation allowed or denied (e.g., s3:PutObject, ec2:RunInstances)
Resource AWS object that the action affects, specified by ARN (e.g., arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/*)
ARN Amazon Resource Name - unique identifier for AWS resources (e.g., arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ec2-role)
Trust Policy JSON document defining who can assume an IAM role (attached to roles only)
STS AWS Security Token Service - generates temporary credentials for IAM roles
AssumeRole STS API call to obtain temporary credentials for an IAM role
Session Temporary credentials period when assuming a role (configurable 15min - 12 hours)
External ID Secret identifier used in cross-account roles to prevent "confused deputy" attacks
MFA Multi-Factor Authentication - second authentication factor (TOTP, hardware token, or U2F)
TOTP Time-based One-Time Password - MFA codes from apps like Google Authenticator
Inline Policy Policy directly embedded in a single user, group, or role (1:1 relationship)
Managed Policy Standalone policy that can be attached to multiple entities (reusable)
AWS Managed Pre-built policies created by AWS (e.g., AmazonS3FullAccess)
Customer Managed Custom policies you create and control (e.g., anihpj-developer-policy)
Permission Boundary Maximum permissions limit for an IAM entity, prevents privilege escalation
SCP Service Control Policy - organization-level policy that sets account-wide permission limits
Explicit Deny Deny statement in policy - always overrides Allow statements
Implicit Deny Default deny when no Allow statement exists (least privilege by default)
Least Privilege Security principle: grant minimum permissions required to perform a task
OIDC OpenID Connect - identity layer for federated authentication (GitHub Actions, Google)
Federation Allowing external identities (SAML, OIDC) to access AWS without creating IAM users
Root User AWS account owner with unrestricted access (should rarely be used, MFA required)
Access Key Long-term credentials (Access Key ID + Secret Access Key) for programmatic access
Session Token Temporary credential component (required for STS temporary credentials)
Policy Evaluation Logic AWS checks: 1) Explicit Deny → 2) Explicit Allow → 3) Implicit Deny

❓ Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Q: Should I use IAM users or IAM roles for my anihpj application?

A: Always use IAM roles for applications running on AWS services (EC2, Lambda, ECS). Only use IAM users for human team members. Roles provide temporary credentials and don't require storing access keys in your code.

Q: How often should I rotate access keys?

A: Every 90 days is the AWS recommendation. Better yet, eliminate long-term access keys entirely by using IAM roles or temporary credentials via STS.

Q: Can I grant temporary access to external contractors?

A: Yes! Use STS AssumeRole to generate temporary credentials (2-12 hours). Create a role with read-only permissions and allow your admin user to assume it. Share the temporary credentials with the contractor - they'll automatically expire.

Q: What's the difference between IAM and AWS Organizations?

A: IAM manages users/roles within a single AWS account. AWS Organizations manages multiple AWS accounts and uses Service Control Policies (SCPs) to set maximum permissions across accounts. Use both together for multi-account governance.

Q: How can I allow GitHub Actions to deploy without storing AWS keys?

A: Use OIDC (OpenID Connect) with GitHub Actions. Create an IAM role that trusts GitHub's OIDC provider, then configure GitHub Actions to assume that role. No long-term credentials needed!

Q: Should I use AWS managed policies or create custom policies?

A: Start with AWS managed policies for common scenarios (e.g., AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess). Create custom policies when you need specific resource restrictions (e.g., only access anihpj-uploads/* bucket).

Q: What happens if I lose my root account password?

A: Use the "Forgot password" link on the sign-in page. AWS will send a password reset link to the root account email. This is why it's critical to maintain access to the root account email address.

Q: Can I restrict IAM users to specific IP addresses?

A: Yes! Use the aws:SourceIp condition in your policies. Example: "Condition": {"IpAddress": {"aws:SourceIp": "203.0.113.0/24"}} - This forces users to connect from your office IP range.

Q: How do I prevent developers from creating expensive EC2 instances?

A: Use the ec2:InstanceType condition to restrict allowed types. Example: Only allow ["t3.micro", "t3.small", "t3.medium"]. Developers can't launch m5.24xlarge or other expensive types.

Q: What's a permission boundary and when should I use one?

A: A permission boundary sets the maximum permissions for an IAM entity, even if policies grant more. Use when allowing developers to create their own roles - the boundary prevents them from escalating privileges beyond what you allow.

Q: Can I share IAM roles across AWS accounts?

A: Yes! Cross-account roles allow users in Account A to assume a role in Account B. Update the role's trust policy to include the external account ARN. Always use External ID for additional security.

Q: How do I audit who accessed what resources?

A: Enable AWS CloudTrail to log all API calls. Use IAM Access Analyzer for continuous monitoring. Generate credential reports monthly. Set up CloudWatch alarms for suspicious activity (e.g., root account usage).

Q: What's the difference between resource-based and identity-based policies?

A: Identity-based policies attach to users/roles (IAM policies). Resource-based policies attach to resources (S3 bucket policies, SNS topic policies). Use identity-based for most cases; resource-based for cross-account access.

Q: Is it safe to use AdministratorAccess for development?

A: No! Even in dev, follow least privilege. If credentials leak or mistakes happen, AdministratorAccess can delete production databases, rack up huge bills, or cause major outages. Grant only what's needed.

Q: How many IAM users/roles can I create?

A: 5,000 users and 5,000 roles per AWS account (soft limits, can be increased). 300 groups and 1,500 customer-managed policies. If you hit these limits, consider AWS IAM Identity Center or multiple accounts.

🔧 Troubleshooting

🐛 Common IAM Issues

Problem Cause Solution
❌ Session Expired Temporary credentials expired Re-run aws sts assume-role to get new credentials
❌ InvalidAccessKeyId Access key deleted or typo Create new access key: aws iam create-access-key
❌ User Already Exists Username conflict Check existing users: aws iam list-users
❌ Policy Too Large Exceeds 6,144 chars (managed) or 10,240 (inline) Split into multiple managed policies
❌ Cannot Delete User User has attached policies, access keys, MFA Detach all policies, delete keys/MFA first
❌ Implicit Deny No explicit Allow statement Add Allow statement for required action
❌ Explicit Deny Wins Deny statement overrides Allow Remove Deny or add condition to exclude specific case

🚫 Debugging Access Denied Errors

Access Denied is the most common IAM error. Follow this systematic debugging approach to quickly identify and fix permission issues in anihpj applications.

Step-by-Step Debugging Process
  1. Identify the Action: Check the error message for the specific action denied (e.g., s3:GetObject, ec2:DescribeInstances)
  2. Verify Identity: Confirm who/what is making the request (IAM user, role, service)
  3. Check Resource ARN: Ensure the resource you're accessing matches the policy's Resource element
  4. Review Policy: Use IAM Policy Simulator to test permissions
  5. Look for Denies: Explicit Deny always wins - check SCPs, permission boundaries, session policies
  6. Check Conditions: Verify conditions like IP address, MFA, time of day aren't blocking access
Example: anihpj Developer Can't Upload Resumes to S3
# Error Message
An error occurred (AccessDenied) when calling the PutObject operation:
Access Denied

# Step 1: Identify Action
Action needed: s3:PutObject
Resource: arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/uploads/*

# Step 2: Check Current Policy (Developer Role)
aws iam get-role-policy --role-name anihpj-developer --policy-name S3Access

# Problem Found: Policy only allows s3:GetObject, missing PutObject
{
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"],
    "Resource": ["arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*"]
  }]
}

# Step 3: Fix - Add PutObject Permission
{
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject", "s3:ListBucket"],
    "Resource": ["arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*"]
  }]
}

# Step 4: Test with Policy Simulator
https://policysim.aws.amazon.com/
Action: s3:PutObject
Resource: arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/uploads/john-resume.pdf
Result: ✅ Allowed
⚠️ Common Access Denied Pitfalls for anihpj:
  • Resource Mismatch: Policy allows anihpj-uploads/* but code accesses anihpj-resumes/*
  • Missing Action: Allowed s3:GetObject but need s3:ListBucket to list files
  • Bucket vs Object: ListBucket requires arn:aws:s3:::bucket (no /*), GetObject requires arn:aws:s3:::bucket/*
  • Cross-Account Trust: Role exists but trust policy doesn't allow your account to assume it
  • Session Duration: Assumed role with 1-hour session, but script runs for 2 hours

🔍 IAM Debugging Tools & Techniques

Tool Purpose When to Use
🧪 IAM Policy Simulator Test whether a policy allows or denies specific actions Before deploying new policies, debugging Access Denied errors
📊 CloudTrail Logs View all IAM API calls, including who, when, what, and result Auditing access, finding unauthorized actions, debugging "who did this?"
🔍 Access Analyzer Identify resources shared with external entities Security audits, finding overly permissive bucket/role policies
📝 Credential Reports CSV report of all users, access keys, MFA status, last usage Compliance audits, finding unused credentials, MFA enforcement
🔧 AWS CLI `--debug` Show detailed API calls, signatures, request/response headers Debugging signature errors, seeing exact API calls your code makes
📋 `aws sts get-caller-identity` Show current IAM identity (User ARN, Account ID, User ID) Verify you're using correct credentials/role
Debugging Workflow for anihpj Applications
# Step 1: Confirm your identity
aws sts get-caller-identity
# Output: Shows if you're using the right role/user

# Step 2: Test specific permission
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda \
  --action-names s3:PutObject \
  --resource-arns arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-uploads/resume.pdf

# Step 3: Enable CloudTrail if not already enabled
aws cloudtrail create-trail --name anihpj-audit \
  --s3-bucket-name anihpj-cloudtrail-logs

# Step 4: Check recent failed API calls
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
  --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=PutObject \
  --max-results 10

# Step 5: Generate credential report (for user/key audits)
aws iam generate-credential-report
aws iam get-credential-report > credential-report.csv

# Step 6: Use Access Analyzer to find external access
aws accessanalyzer create-analyzer \
  --analyzer-name anihpj-analyzer \
  --type ACCOUNT

aws accessanalyzer list-findings \
  --analyzer-arn arn:aws:access-analyzer:us-east-1:123456789012:analyzer/anihpj-analyzer
💡 Pro Tip for anihpj Debugging:

Enable CloudTrail in all regions and send logs to a centralized S3 bucket with lifecycle policies (move to Glacier after 90 days, delete after 7 years). This costs ~$2/month but provides complete audit trail for compliance and debugging.

Set up CloudWatch alarms for critical events: root account usage, console sign-in failures, policy changes, role assumption from unknown accounts.

⌨️ API & CLI Reference

Comprehensive guide to managing IAM programmatically using AWS CLI, Boto3 SDK, and Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tools. All examples are tailored for the anihpj job portal platform.

🖥️ AWS CLI Commands

Essential AWS CLI commands for managing IAM users, groups, roles, and policies for anihpj. All commands assume you have AWS CLI configured with appropriate credentials.

👤 User Management
# Create new IAM user for anihpj developer
aws iam create-user --user-name john-developer

# Add user to developers group
aws iam add-user-to-group --user-name john-developer --group-name anihpj-developers

# Create access key for programmatic access
aws iam create-access-key --user-name john-developer

# List all users
aws iam list-users --output table

# Get specific user details
aws iam get-user --user-name john-developer

# Delete user (must remove dependencies first)
aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name john-developer --group-name anihpj-developers
aws iam delete-access-key --user-name john-developer --access-key-id AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE
aws iam delete-user --user-name john-developer

# Enable MFA for user
aws iam create-virtual-mfa-device --virtual-mfa-device-name john-mfa \
  --outfile /tmp/john-qr.png --bootstrap-method QRCodePNG
aws iam enable-mfa-device --user-name john-developer \
  --serial-number arn:aws:iam::123456789012:mfa/john-mfa \
  --authentication-code-1 123456 --authentication-code-2 789012
👥 Group Management
# Create group for anihpj developers
aws iam create-group --group-name anihpj-developers

# Attach managed policy to group
aws iam attach-group-policy --group-name anihpj-developers \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess

# Create and attach custom inline policy
aws iam put-group-policy --group-name anihpj-developers \
  --policy-name anihpj-dev-policy \
  --policy-document file://dev-policy.json

# List groups
aws iam list-groups

# List users in group
aws iam get-group --group-name anihpj-developers

# Detach policy from group
aws iam detach-group-policy --group-name anihpj-developers \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess

# Delete group
aws iam delete-group --group-name anihpj-developers
🎭 Role Management
# Create role for EC2 instances (trust policy required)
aws iam create-role --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
  --assume-role-policy-document file://ec2-trust-policy.json

# Trust policy file (ec2-trust-policy.json):
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal": {"Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"},
    "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
  }]
}

# Attach managed policy to role
aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3FullAccess

# Create custom policy for role
aws iam create-policy --policy-name anihpj-s3-uploads-policy \
  --policy-document file://s3-uploads-policy.json

# Assume role to get temporary credentials
aws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ec2-role \
  --role-session-name john-session

# List roles
aws iam list-roles

# Get role details
aws iam get-role --role-name anihpj-ec2-role

# Delete role (remove dependencies first)
aws iam detach-role-policy --role-name anihpj-ec2-role \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3FullAccess
aws iam delete-role --role-name anihpj-ec2-role
📜 Policy Management
# Create custom managed policy
aws iam create-policy --policy-name anihpj-resume-upload-policy \
  --policy-document file://resume-upload-policy.json

# Policy file (resume-upload-policy.json):
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["s3:PutObject", "s3:PutObjectAcl"],
    "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*",
    "Condition": {
      "StringEquals": {"s3:x-amz-acl": "private"}
    }
  }]
}

# List all customer-managed policies
aws iam list-policies --scope Local

# Get policy details
aws iam get-policy --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-resume-upload-policy

# Get policy version (to see actual JSON)
aws iam get-policy-version --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-resume-upload-policy \
  --version-id v1

# Simulate policy (test before deployment)
aws iam simulate-custom-policy --policy-input-list file://resume-upload-policy.json \
  --action-names s3:PutObject \
  --resource-arns arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/john-resume.pdf

# Delete policy
aws iam delete-policy --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-resume-upload-policy
💡 anihpj CLI Best Practices:
  • Use --profile anihpj-prod to avoid accidentally running commands in wrong account
  • Always test policies with simulate-custom-policy before applying
  • Store policy JSON files in version control (Git) for audit trail
  • Use --output json | jq for better formatting and filtering
  • Set up AWS CLI aliases in ~/.aws/cli/alias for common anihpj commands

🐍 Boto3 SDK (Python)

Integrate IAM management into anihpj Django backend using Boto3 SDK. These examples show common IAM automation tasks for the job portal.

Complete anihpj IAM Management Module
# anihpj/utils/iam_manager.py
import boto3
import json
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError

class AnihpjIAMManager:
    """IAM management for anihpj job portal"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.iam = boto3.client('iam')
        self.sts = boto3.client('sts')
    
    def create_developer_user(self, username, email):
        """Create new developer with standard permissions"""
        try:
            # Create user
            response = self.iam.create_user(
                UserName=username,
                Tags=[
                    {'Key': 'Email', 'Value': email},
                    {'Key': 'Project', 'Value': 'anihpj'},
                    {'Key': 'Environment', 'Value': 'dev'}
                ]
            )
            
            # Add to developers group
            self.iam.add_user_to_group(
                UserName=username,
                GroupName='anihpj-developers'
            )
            
            # Create access key
            key_response = self.iam.create_access_key(UserName=username)
            
            return {
                'success': True,
                'user_arn': response['User']['Arn'],
                'access_key_id': key_response['AccessKey']['AccessKeyId'],
                'secret_access_key': key_response['AccessKey']['SecretAccessKey']
            }
        except ClientError as e:
            return {'success': False, 'error': str(e)}
    
    def generate_s3_upload_credentials(self, user_id, duration=3600):
        """Generate temporary credentials for S3 resume uploads"""
        policy = {
            "Version": "2012-10-17",
            "Statement": [{
                "Effect": "Allow",
                "Action": ["s3:PutObject", "s3:PutObjectAcl"],
                "Resource": f"arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/user-{user_id}/*",
                "Condition": {
                    "StringEquals": {"s3:x-amz-acl": "private"}
                }
            }]
        }
        
        try:
            response = self.sts.assume_role(
                RoleArn='arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-resume-uploader',
                RoleSessionName=f'user-{user_id}-upload',
                Policy=json.dumps(policy),
                DurationSeconds=duration
            )
            
            credentials = response['Credentials']
            return {
                'access_key_id': credentials['AccessKeyId'],
                'secret_access_key': credentials['SecretAccessKey'],
                'session_token': credentials['SessionToken'],
                'expiration': credentials['Expiration'].isoformat()
            }
        except ClientError as e:
            return {'error': str(e)}
    
    def rotate_access_keys(self, username):
        """Rotate access keys for user (security best practice)"""
        try:
            # List current keys
            keys = self.iam.list_access_keys(UserName=username)
            
            # Create new key
            new_key = self.iam.create_access_key(UserName=username)
            
            # Deactivate old keys (keep for 24 hours for rollback)
            for key in keys['AccessKeyMetadata']:
                if key['AccessKeyId'] != new_key['AccessKey']['AccessKeyId']:
                    self.iam.update_access_key(
                        UserName=username,
                        AccessKeyId=key['AccessKeyId'],
                        Status='Inactive'
                    )
            
            return {
                'success': True,
                'new_access_key_id': new_key['AccessKey']['AccessKeyId'],
                'new_secret_access_key': new_key['AccessKey']['SecretAccessKey']
            }
        except ClientError as e:
            return {'success': False, 'error': str(e)}
    
    def audit_unused_credentials(self, days=90):
        """Find credentials not used in X days (compliance requirement)"""
        import datetime
        
        unused_users = []
        credential_report = self.iam.generate_credential_report()
        
        # Wait for report generation
        while credential_report['State'] == 'INPROGRESS':
            time.sleep(2)
            credential_report = self.iam.get_credential_report()
        
        # Parse report
        import csv
        import io
        
        report_csv = credential_report['Content'].decode('utf-8')
        reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(report_csv))
        
        for row in reader:
            if row['password_last_used'] != 'N/A':
                last_used = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(row['password_last_used'])
                if (datetime.datetime.now(tz=last_used.tzinfo) - last_used).days > days:
                    unused_users.append({
                        'username': row['user'],
                        'last_used': row['password_last_used'],
                        'mfa_active': row['mfa_active']
                    })
        
        return unused_users

# Usage in Django view
from .iam_manager import AnihpjIAMManager

def upload_resume_view(request):
    iam_manager = AnihpjIAMManager()
    
    # Generate temporary S3 credentials for user
    credentials = iam_manager.generate_s3_upload_credentials(
        user_id=request.user.id,
        duration=900  # 15 minutes
    )
    
    return JsonResponse({
        's3_credentials': credentials,
        'bucket': 'anihpj-resumes',
        'key_prefix': f'user-{request.user.id}/'
    })
⚠️ Security Best Practices for Boto3:
  • Never hardcode AWS credentials - use IAM roles for EC2/Lambda
  • Use boto3.Session() for multi-threaded applications
  • Implement exponential backoff for rate-limited IAM API calls
  • Log all IAM changes to database for audit trail
  • Encrypt temporary credentials before storing in session

📦 CloudFormation Templates

Define anihpj IAM infrastructure as code using AWS CloudFormation. This ensures repeatable, version-controlled deployments across dev, staging, and production environments.

Complete anihpj IAM Stack (CloudFormation YAML)
# anihpj-iam-stack.yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: 'IAM resources for anihpj job portal platform'

Parameters:
  Environment:
    Type: String
    AllowedValues: [dev, staging, prod]
    Default: dev
    Description: Deployment environment

Resources:
  # EC2 Instance Role for anihpj Web Servers
  AnihpjEC2Role:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      RoleName: !Sub 'anihpj-ec2-${Environment}'
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Service: ec2.amazonaws.com
            Action: 'sts:AssumeRole'
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - 'arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy'
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: S3Access
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 's3:GetObject'
                  - 's3:PutObject'
                  - 's3:DeleteObject'
                Resource: !Sub 'arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-${Environment}-uploads/*'
        - PolicyName: RDSAccess
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 'rds-db:connect'
                Resource: !Sub 'arn:aws:rds-db:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:dbuser:*/anihpj_app'
      Tags:
        - Key: Project
          Value: anihpj
        - Key: Environment
          Value: !Ref Environment

  # Instance Profile for EC2
  AnihpjEC2InstanceProfile:
    Type: AWS::IAM::InstanceProfile
    Properties:
      InstanceProfileName: !Sub 'anihpj-ec2-${Environment}'
      Roles:
        - !Ref AnihpjEC2Role

  # Lambda Execution Role for Background Jobs
  AnihpjLambdaRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      RoleName: !Sub 'anihpj-lambda-${Environment}'
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Service: lambda.amazonaws.com
            Action: 'sts:AssumeRole'
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - 'arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole'
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: SESAccess
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 'ses:SendEmail'
                  - 'ses:SendRawEmail'
                Resource: '*'
                Condition:
                  StringEquals:
                    'ses:FromAddress': 'noreply@anihpj.com'
      Tags:
        - Key: Project
          Value: anihpj

  # Developers Group
  AnihpjDevelopersGroup:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Group
    Properties:
      GroupName: !Sub 'anihpj-developers-${Environment}'
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - 'arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/ReadOnlyAccess'
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: DeveloperAccess
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Sid: AllowEC2Development
                Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 'ec2:StartInstances'
                  - 'ec2:StopInstances'
                  - 'ec2:RebootInstances'
                Resource: '*'
                Condition:
                  StringEquals:
                    'ec2:ResourceTag/Environment': !Ref Environment
              - Sid: PreventExpensiveInstances
                Effect: Deny
                Action: 'ec2:RunInstances'
                Resource: 'arn:aws:ec2:*:*:instance/*'
                Condition:
                  StringNotLike:
                    'ec2:InstanceType':
                      - 't3.*'
                      - 't2.*'

  # GitHub Actions OIDC Provider
  GitHubOIDCProvider:
    Type: AWS::IAM::OIDCProvider
    Properties:
      Url: https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com
      ClientIdList:
        - sts.amazonaws.com
      ThumbprintList:
        - '6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1'

  # GitHub Actions Deployment Role
  GitHubActionsRole:
    Type: AWS::IAM::Role
    Properties:
      RoleName: !Sub 'anihpj-github-actions-${Environment}'
      AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              Federated: !GetAtt GitHubOIDCProvider.Arn
            Action: 'sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity'
            Condition:
              StringEquals:
                'token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud': 'sts.amazonaws.com'
              StringLike:
                'token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub': 'repo:anihpj/backend:*'
      ManagedPolicyArns:
        - 'arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPowerUser'
      Policies:
        - PolicyName: DeploymentAccess
          PolicyDocument:
            Version: '2012-10-17'
            Statement:
              - Effect: Allow
                Action:
                  - 's3:PutObject'
                  - 's3:GetObject'
                Resource: !Sub 'arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-${Environment}-deployments/*'

Outputs:
  EC2RoleArn:
    Description: ARN of EC2 instance role
    Value: !GetAtt AnihpjEC2Role.Arn
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${AWS::StackName}-EC2RoleArn'
  
  LambdaRoleArn:
    Description: ARN of Lambda execution role
    Value: !GetAtt AnihpjLambdaRole.Arn
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${AWS::StackName}-LambdaRoleArn'
  
  GitHubActionsRoleArn:
    Description: ARN for GitHub Actions OIDC role
    Value: !GetAtt GitHubActionsRole.Arn
    Export:
      Name: !Sub '${AWS::StackName}-GitHubActionsRoleArn'
Deploying anihpj IAM Stack
# Deploy to dev environment
aws cloudformation create-stack \
  --stack-name anihpj-iam-dev \
  --template-body file://anihpj-iam-stack.yaml \
  --parameters ParameterKey=Environment,ParameterValue=dev \
  --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM

# Deploy to production
aws cloudformation create-stack \
  --stack-name anihpj-iam-prod \
  --template-body file://anihpj-iam-stack.yaml \
  --parameters ParameterKey=Environment,ParameterValue=prod \
  --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM

# Update existing stack
aws cloudformation update-stack \
  --stack-name anihpj-iam-dev \
  --template-body file://anihpj-iam-stack.yaml \
  --parameters ParameterKey=Environment,ParameterValue=dev \
  --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM

# Delete stack (cleanup)
aws cloudformation delete-stack --stack-name anihpj-iam-dev
✅ CloudFormation Benefits for anihpj:
  • Version Control: All IAM changes tracked in Git
  • Repeatable: Deploy identical IAM setup in dev/staging/prod
  • Rollback: Easy to revert changes if something breaks
  • Change Sets: Preview IAM changes before applying
  • Drift Detection: Identify manual changes made outside CloudFormation

🏗️ Terraform

Manage anihpj IAM infrastructure using Terraform (HCL). Terraform provides a cloud-agnostic IaC approach with excellent state management and planning capabilities.

Complete anihpj IAM Configuration (Terraform HCL)
# terraform/iam/main.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "anihpj"
      ManagedBy   = "Terraform"
      Environment = var.environment
    }
  }
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment"
  type        = string
  default     = "dev"
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

# EC2 Instance Role
resource "aws_iam_role" "ec2" {
  name = "anihpj-ec2-${var.environment}"
  
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com"
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "ec2_s3_access" {
  name = "s3-access"
  role = aws_iam_role.ec2.id
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Action = [
        "s3:GetObject",
        "s3:PutObject",
        "s3:DeleteObject"
      ]
      Resource = "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-${var.environment}-uploads/*"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_instance_profile" "ec2" {
  name = "anihpj-ec2-${var.environment}"
  role = aws_iam_role.ec2.name
}

# Lambda Execution Role
resource "aws_iam_role" "lambda" {
  name = "anihpj-lambda-${var.environment}"
  
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "lambda.amazonaws.com"
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "lambda_basic" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.lambda.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole"
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "lambda_ses" {
  name = "ses-access"
  role = aws_iam_role.lambda.id
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Action = [
        "ses:SendEmail",
        "ses:SendRawEmail"
      ]
      Resource = "*"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "ses:FromAddress" = "noreply@anihpj.com"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

# Developers Group
resource "aws_iam_group" "developers" {
  name = "anihpj-developers-${var.environment}"
}

resource "aws_iam_group_policy_attachment" "developers_readonly" {
  group      = aws_iam_group.developers.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/ReadOnlyAccess"
}

resource "aws_iam_group_policy" "developers_custom" {
  name  = "developer-permissions"
  group = aws_iam_group.developers.name
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "AllowEC2Development"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "ec2:StartInstances",
          "ec2:StopInstances",
          "ec2:RebootInstances"
        ]
        Resource = "*"
        Condition = {
          StringEquals = {
            "ec2:ResourceTag/Environment" = var.environment
          }
        }
      },
      {
        Sid    = "PreventExpensiveInstances"
        Effect = "Deny"
        Action = "ec2:RunInstances"
        Resource = "arn:aws:ec2:*:*:instance/*"
        Condition = {
          StringNotLike = {
            "ec2:InstanceType" = ["t3.*", "t2.*"]
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}

# GitHub Actions OIDC
resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github" {
  url = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
  
  client_id_list = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  
  thumbprint_list = ["6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1"]
}

resource "aws_iam_role" "github_actions" {
  name = "anihpj-github-actions-${var.environment}"
  
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud" = "sts.amazonaws.com"
        }
        StringLike = {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub" = "repo:anihpj/backend:*"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

# Outputs
output "ec2_role_arn" {
  description = "ARN of EC2 instance role"
  value       = aws_iam_role.ec2.arn
}

output "lambda_role_arn" {
  description = "ARN of Lambda execution role"
  value       = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
}

output "github_actions_role_arn" {
  description = "ARN for GitHub Actions OIDC role"
  value       = aws_iam_role.github_actions.arn
}
Deploying anihpj IAM with Terraform
# Initialize Terraform
cd terraform/iam
terraform init

# Plan changes (preview what will be created)
terraform plan -var="environment=dev"

# Apply changes (create IAM resources)
terraform apply -var="environment=dev" -auto-approve

# Show current state
terraform show

# List all resources
terraform state list

# Destroy all IAM resources (cleanup)
terraform destroy -var="environment=dev"

# Use workspaces for multiple environments
terraform workspace new dev
terraform workspace new staging
terraform workspace new prod

# Switch to prod workspace
terraform workspace select prod
terraform apply -var="environment=prod"
💡 Terraform Best Practices for anihpj:
  • Remote State: Store terraform.tfstate in S3 with DynamoDB locking
  • Modules: Create reusable IAM modules for common patterns
  • Variables: Use .tfvars files for environment-specific values
  • Import: Import existing IAM resources with terraform import
  • Formatting: Run terraform fmt before committing

🔗 AWS Service Integration

Learn how IAM integrates with core AWS services for the anihpj job portal. Covers EC2, Lambda, S3, and RDS integration patterns with practical examples.

🖥️ EC2 Integration

EC2 instances use IAM roles via instance profiles to access other AWS services without storing credentials on the instance. This is the recommended approach for anihpj web servers.

Setting Up EC2 Instance Profile for anihpj Web Server
# Step 1: Create IAM role for EC2
aws iam create-role --role-name anihpj-webserver \
  --assume-role-policy-document '{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [{
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {"Service": "ec2.amazonaws.com"},
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  }'

# Step 2: Attach policies for S3 and SES access
aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name anihpj-webserver \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-s3-access

aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name anihpj-webserver \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-ses-access

# Step 3: Create instance profile
aws iam create-instance-profile --instance-profile-name anihpj-webserver

# Step 4: Add role to instance profile
aws iam add-role-to-instance-profile \
  --instance-profile-name anihpj-webserver \
  --role-name anihpj-webserver

# Step 5: Launch EC2 instance with instance profile
aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0 \
  --instance-type t3.medium \
  --iam-instance-profile Name=anihpj-webserver \
  --key-name anihpj-keypair \
  --security-group-ids sg-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --subnet-id subnet-0123456789abcdef0 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=anihpj-web-prod}]'

# Step 6: Verify instance can assume role
# SSH into instance and run:
aws sts get-caller-identity
# Output shows EC2 role ARN
Using EC2 Instance Role in anihpj Django App
# anihpj/settings.py
import boto3

# Boto3 automatically uses EC2 instance role credentials
# No need to configure access keys!
s3_client = boto3.client('s3')
ses_client = boto3.client('ses', region_name='us-east-1')

# anihpj/utils/s3_handler.py
import boto3
from django.conf import settings

class S3Handler:
    def __init__(self):
        # Automatically uses EC2 instance role
        self.s3 = boto3.client('s3')
        self.bucket = 'anihpj-uploads'
    
    def upload_resume(self, file, user_id):
        """Upload resume to S3 using instance role credentials"""
        key = f'resumes/user-{user_id}/{file.name}'
        
        self.s3.put_object(
            Bucket=self.bucket,
            Key=key,
            Body=file,
            ContentType=file.content_type,
            Metadata={
                'user-id': str(user_id),
                'uploaded-at': datetime.now().isoformat()
            }
        )
        
        return f'https://{self.bucket}.s3.amazonaws.com/{key}'
    
    def generate_presigned_url(self, key, expiration=3600):
        """Generate temporary download URL (1 hour)"""
        return self.s3.generate_presigned_url(
            'get_object',
            Params={'Bucket': self.bucket, 'Key': key},
            ExpiresIn=expiration
        )
💡 EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS):

EC2 instances retrieve role credentials from the metadata service at http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ROLE_NAME. Credentials automatically rotate every 6 hours. Always use IMDSv2 (session-oriented) for enhanced security.

⚡ Lambda Integration

Lambda functions require an execution role defining what AWS services they can access. anihpj uses Lambda for background jobs like sending emails and processing resume uploads.

Creating Lambda Execution Role for anihpj
# Trust policy for Lambda (trust-policy.json)
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal": {"Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com"},
    "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
  }]
}

# Create Lambda execution role
aws iam create-role --role-name anihpj-lambda-email \
  --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-policy.json

# Attach AWS managed policy for CloudWatch Logs
aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name anihpj-lambda-email \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole

# Create custom policy for SES (ses-policy.json)
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["ses:SendEmail", "ses:SendRawEmail"],
    "Resource": "*",
    "Condition": {
      "StringEquals": {
        "ses:FromAddress": "noreply@anihpj.com"
      }
    }
  }]
}

# Attach custom SES policy
aws iam put-role-policy --role-name anihpj-lambda-email \
  --policy-name SESAccess \
  --policy-document file://ses-policy.json

# Create Lambda function with execution role
aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name anihpj-send-application-email \
  --runtime python3.11 \
  --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-lambda-email \
  --handler lambda_function.lambda_handler \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip \
  --environment Variables={FROM_EMAIL=noreply@anihpj.com}
anihpj Lambda Function Using Execution Role
# lambda_function.py
import boto3
import json
import os

# Automatically uses Lambda execution role
ses = boto3.client('ses', region_name='us-east-1')

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    """Send application confirmation email"""
    
    # Extract data from event
    applicant_email = event['applicant_email']
    job_title = event['job_title']
    company = event['company']
    
    # Send email using SES (authorized by execution role)
    response = ses.send_email(
        Source=os.environ['FROM_EMAIL'],
        Destination={'ToAddresses': [applicant_email]},
        Message={
            'Subject': {
                'Data': f'Application Received: {job_title} at {company}'
            },
            'Body': {
                'Html': {
                    'Data': f'''
                    
Thank you for applying!

                    
We've received your application for {job_title} 
                    at {company}.

                    
Our team will review your resume and get back to you within 3-5 business days.

                    
Best regards,
anihpj Team

                    '''
                }
            }
        }
    )
    
    return {
        'statusCode': 200,
        'body': json.dumps({
            'message': 'Email sent successfully',
            'message_id': response['MessageId']
        })
    }
⚠️ Lambda IAM Best Practices for anihpj:
  • Create separate roles for each Lambda function (least privilege)
  • Use resource-based policies to allow S3/SNS to invoke Lambda
  • Enable AWS X-Ray for tracing IAM permission issues
  • Set ReservedConcurrentExecutions to prevent runaway costs
  • Use Lambda layers for shared IAM/boto3 code across functions

📦 S3 Integration

S3 uses both IAM policies (identity-based) and bucket policies (resource-based) to control access. anihpj stores resumes, company logos, and uploaded documents in S3.

IAM Policy for anihpj S3 Access
# IAM policy attached to EC2 role (identity-based)
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowResumeUploads",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:PutObject", "s3:PutObjectAcl"],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "s3:x-amz-acl": "private"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "AllowLogoDownloads",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:GetObject"],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-logos/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "AllowListBuckets",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": ["s3:ListBucket"],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes",
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-logos"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
S3 Bucket Policy for anihpj (Resource-Based)
# Bucket policy attached to anihpj-resumes bucket
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowEC2RoleAccess",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/anihpj-ec2"
      },
      "Action": ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*"
    },
    {
      "Sid": "AllowCloudFrontAccess",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Service": "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"
      },
      "Action": "s3:GetObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/public/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:cloudfront::123456789012:distribution/E1234567890ABC"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyUnencryptedUploads",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "s3:PutObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*",
      "Condition": {
        "StringNotEquals": {
          "s3:x-amz-server-side-encryption": "AES256"
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "Sid": "DenyPublicAccess",
      "Effect": "Deny",
      "Principal": "*",
      "Action": "s3:*",
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes",
        "arn:aws:s3:::anihpj-resumes/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "Bool": {
          "aws:SecureTransport": "false"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}

# Apply bucket policy
aws s3api put-bucket-policy --bucket anihpj-resumes \
  --policy file://bucket-policy.json
Pre-Signed URLs for Secure anihpj Downloads
# anihpj/views.py
import boto3
from datetime import timedelta

def download_resume(request, application_id):
    """Generate temporary download link for resume"""
    
    application = Application.objects.get(id=application_id)
    
    # Verify user is authorized (employer or applicant)
    if request.user != application.user and \
       request.user != application.job.employer:
        return HttpResponseForbidden()
    
    # Generate pre-signed URL (valid for 1 hour)
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    url = s3.generate_presigned_url(
        'get_object',
        Params={
            'Bucket': 'anihpj-resumes',
            'Key': application.resume_key,
            'ResponseContentDisposition': f'attachment; filename="{application.resume_filename}"'
        },
        ExpiresIn=3600  # 1 hour
    )
    
    return JsonResponse({'download_url': url})
✅ S3 + IAM Security Checklist for anihpj:
  • ✅ Block all public access at bucket level
  • ✅ Enable S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS)
  • ✅ Enforce HTTPS-only access (deny aws:SecureTransport: false)
  • ✅ Use pre-signed URLs for temporary access, not public ACLs
  • ✅ Enable S3 versioning for accidental deletion protection
  • ✅ Set up S3 access logging to track downloads

🗄️ RDS Integration

RDS supports IAM database authentication eliminating the need to store database passwords. anihpj uses PostgreSQL with IAM authentication for enhanced security.

Enable IAM Authentication on anihpj RDS Instance
# Step 1: Enable IAM authentication on RDS instance
aws rds modify-db-instance \
  --db-instance-identifier anihpj-postgres-prod \
  --enable-iam-database-authentication \
  --apply-immediately

# Step 2: Create IAM policy for RDS access
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": ["rds-db:connect"],
    "Resource": "arn:aws:rds-db:us-east-1:123456789012:dbuser:db-ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRST/anihpj_app"
  }]
}

aws iam create-policy --policy-name anihpj-rds-iam-auth \
  --policy-document file://rds-policy.json

# Step 3: Attach policy to EC2 role
aws iam attach-role-policy --role-name anihpj-ec2 \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:policy/anihpj-rds-iam-auth

# Step 4: Create database user (run in PostgreSQL)
CREATE USER anihpj_app WITH LOGIN;
GRANT rds_iam TO anihpj_app;
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE anihpj TO anihpj_app;
Django Settings for IAM Database Authentication
# anihpj/settings.py
import boto3

def get_rds_auth_token():
    """Generate RDS IAM auth token (valid for 15 minutes)"""
    rds = boto3.client('rds', region_name='us-east-1')
    
    token = rds.generate_db_auth_token(
        DBHostname='anihpj-postgres-prod.c9akciq32.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com',
        Port=5432,
        DBUsername='anihpj_app',
        Region='us-east-1'
    )
    
    return token

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'NAME': 'anihpj',
        'USER': 'anihpj_app',
        'PASSWORD': get_rds_auth_token(),  # Generated IAM token
        'HOST': 'anihpj-postgres-prod.c9akciq32.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com',
        'PORT': '5432',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'sslmode': 'require',  # Enforce SSL
            'sslrootcert': '/path/to/rds-ca-2019-root.pem'
        }
    }
}

# Note: Token expires every 15 minutes, so connection pooling must regenerate
# Use custom database backend to handle token rotation
Custom Django Database Backend for Token Rotation
# anihpj/db_backends/postgresql_iam.py
from django.db.backends.postgresql import base
import boto3

class DatabaseWrapper(base.DatabaseWrapper):
    """PostgreSQL backend with IAM token rotation"""
    
    def get_connection_params(self):
        params = super().get_connection_params()
        
        # Generate fresh IAM auth token
        rds = boto3.client('rds', region_name='us-east-1')
        params['password'] = rds.generate_db_auth_token(
            DBHostname=params['host'],
            Port=params['port'],
            DBUsername=params['user'],
            Region='us-east-1'
        )
        
        return params

# Update settings.py
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'anihpj.db_backends.postgresql_iam',  # Custom backend
        'NAME': 'anihpj',
        'USER': 'anihpj_app',
        'HOST': 'anihpj-postgres-prod.c9akciq32.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com',
        'PORT': '5432',
        'OPTIONS': {'sslmode': 'require'}
    }
}
💡 RDS IAM Authentication Benefits for anihpj:
  • No Password Storage: Eliminates hardcoded passwords in code/environment
  • Automatic Rotation: Tokens expire after 15 minutes, reducing credential leak impact
  • Centralized Access Control: Manage DB access through IAM policies
  • Audit Trail: CloudTrail logs all DB connection attempts
  • Integration: Works seamlessly with EC2 instance profiles
⚠️ RDS IAM Authentication Limitations:
  • Connection limit: 200 connections/second per database user
  • Only supported for PostgreSQL and MySQL (not SQL Server or Oracle)
  • Tokens expire every 15 minutes - connection pooling must handle rotation
  • Performance overhead: ~10ms to generate token vs instant password auth
🎓 Next Steps for anihpj IAM Mastery:
  1. ✅ Complete Security Baseline: Ensure all critical items in security checklist are implemented
  2. 📚 Review AWS IAM Best Practices: Read official AWS documentation weekly
  3. 🧪 Practice with Policy Simulator: Test all new policies before production deployment
  4. 📊 Enable CloudTrail: Log all IAM API calls for compliance and debugging
  5. 🔍 Monthly Audits: Review credential reports, unused permissions, Access Analyzer findings
  6. 🎯 Automate Onboarding: Create scripts for consistent IAM user/role creation
  7. 🏆 Certify Your Team: AWS Certified Security – Specialty for advanced IAM knowledge

🛡️ Security & Compliance Certifications

IAM is certified and compliant with major security standards, making it suitable for highly regulated industries. For anihpj, this means your job portal can handle sensitive data (resumes, personal information) with confidence.

✅ PCI DSS Level 1

Payment Card Industry

  • Safe for payment processing
  • Credit card data protection
  • Suitable for job board subscriptions
✅ HIPAA Eligible

Healthcare Compliance

  • BAA available from AWS
  • PHI data protection
  • Healthcare job postings secure
✅ SOC 1, 2, 3

Service Organization Control

  • Third-party audited
  • Controls effectiveness verified
  • Annual reports available
✅ ISO 27001

Information Security

  • Global security standard
  • Risk management framework
  • Continuous improvement model
✅ GDPR Compliant

EU Data Protection

  • EU citizen data protection
  • Right to be forgotten support
  • Data residency controls
✅ FedRAMP Authorized

U.S. Government

  • Government cloud security
  • High/Moderate/Low baselines
  • Continuous monitoring

🏆 anihpj Production Checklist

Ensure your IAM configuration is production-ready before launching

🔐

Root Account

  • ✅ MFA enabled (virtual or hardware)
  • ✅ No access keys exist
  • ✅ Strong password (14+ chars)
  • ✅ Used only for billing/emergency
  • ✅ Recovery contacts set
👥

Users & Groups

  • ✅ Individual users (no shared accounts)
  • ✅ MFA for all users
  • ✅ Groups: Dev, DevOps, Admin, ReadOnly
  • ✅ Password policy enforced
  • ✅ Access keys rotated (90 days)
🎭

Roles

  • ✅ anihpj-ec2-role (RDS + S3)
  • ✅ anihpj-lambda-role (SES)
  • ✅ anihpj-ecs-task-role
  • ✅ anihpj-codebuild-role
  • ✅ Trust policies verified
📜

Policies

  • ✅ Least privilege applied
  • ✅ No wildcard (*) permissions
  • ✅ Resource-level restrictions
  • ✅ Tested with Policy Simulator
  • ✅ Version controlled (Git)
📊

Monitoring

  • ✅ CloudTrail enabled (multi-region)
  • ✅ CloudWatch alarms configured
  • ✅ SNS notifications for failures
  • ✅ Credential reports monthly
  • ✅ Access Analyzer enabled
🔍

Compliance

  • ✅ Quarterly permission audits
  • ✅ Unused users removed
  • ✅ Documentation up to date
  • ✅ Incident response plan
  • ✅ SOC 2 controls implemented

💡 Pro Tip for anihpj

IAM is FREE but mistakes are expensive! A misconfigured S3 bucket policy cost one company $80M in breach penalties. Spend 2 hours setting up IAM correctly once, or spend weeks recovering from a security incident. The choice is clear! 🎯

🚀 Get Started with anihpj IAM

  1. Secure root account (10 min)
  2. Create admin user with MFA (15 min)
  3. Set up groups and policies (30 min)
  4. Create service roles for EC2/Lambda (30 min)
  5. Enable CloudTrail logging (10 min)
  6. Test with Policy Simulator (15 min)
  7. Deploy to production (5 min)
  8. Schedule quarterly audits (ongoing)

Total Time: ~2 hours | Cost: $0 | Security Improvement: 10x

💼 Ready to Secure anihpj with IAM?

You now have a comprehensive understanding of AWS IAM. Start with the Quick Start Guide above, follow the Production Checklist, and remember: IAM is FREE - there's no excuse not to secure your job portal properly! 🔐

Security first, features second. Your users' trust depends on it! 🎯

AWS Cognito (User Authentication Service)

Category: Security & Identity  |  Use Case: User Authentication & Authorization  |  Pricing: Free tier: 50,000 MAUs, then $0.0055/MAU  |  Last Updated: January 2026  |  Documentation: AWS Cognito Docs

Secure user authentication and authorization service for your anihpj Django job portal with social logins, multi-factor authentication, and user management capabilities.

📋 Overview & Introduction

AWS Cognito is a managed identity service that provides authentication, authorization, and user management for your web and mobile applications. For your anihpj Django job portal, Cognito handles user sign-up, sign-in, social logins, and secure access control.

🚀 Quick Start Guide for anihpj (15 Minutes):
  1. Create User Pool: AWS Console → Cognito → Create user pool → "anihpj-users-prod"
  2. Configure sign-in: Email address + Password (keep it simple)
  3. Add custom attributes: user_type, company_name, skills, experience_level
  4. Enable MFA: Optional for job seekers, Required for employers
  5. Create App Client: anihpj-django-client with client secret
  6. Configure callbacks: localhost:8000/auth/callback/ and anihpj.com/auth/callback/
  7. Install Django packages: pip install boto3 python-jose django-cognito-jwt
  8. Add settings.py config: AWS_COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID, CLIENT_ID, CLIENT_SECRET
  9. Create auth backend: CognitoBackend class for JWT validation
  10. Test authentication: Login flow works end-to-end!
🎯 Cognito at a Glance
💰 Free Tier
50K MAUs
Monthly Active Users
Then $0.0055/MAU
🔐 Security
MFA
SMS, TOTP, Email
Adaptive Auth
🌐 Social Login
OAuth 2.0
LinkedIn, Google
Facebook, SAML
🎫 JWT Tokens
3 Types
ID, Access,
Refresh
👥 User Pools
Managed
User directory
Custom attrs
🔗 Identity Pools
AWS Access
Temp credentials
S3, DynamoDB
🎯 Key Benefits for anihpj
🔐
Managed Authentication

Handle user sign-up, sign-in, and account recovery automatically for job seekers and employers

🌐
Social Identity Providers

LinkedIn, Google, Facebook integration for professional profiles and job portal

🛡️
Multi-Factor Authentication

SMS, TOTP, and email verification to secure employer accounts and sensitive operations

📈
Scalable Infrastructure

From startup (free 50K users) to enterprise with millions of users seamlessly

🏷️
Custom Attributes

Define job seeker vs employer profiles with skills, experience_level, company_name fields

⚡
Lambda Triggers

Customize auth flows with serverless functions for validation, emails, and custom claims

💡 When to Use Cognito for anihpj:

Perfect for your Django job portal when you need separate user types (job seekers vs employers), social login integration, email verification, and scalable user management. Choose Cognito over Django's built-in auth when you need social logins, MFA, or plan to add mobile apps later. LinkedIn integration is essential for professional job portals!

⚖️ Cognito vs. Other Auth Services
Service Type Pros Cons anihpj Fit
AWS Cognito AWS Managed Free 50K MAUs, social logins, MFA, AWS integration Learning curve, AWS lock-in ⭐ Best Choice
Auth0 Third-party SaaS Easy setup, great docs, customization Expensive at scale ($23K+/year) ⚠️ Cost prohibitive
Firebase Auth Google Managed Easy to use, mobile-friendly Limited enterprise features, Google ecosystem ⚠️ Mobile apps only
Django Auth Built-in Simple, no external dependency No social logins, no MFA, manual scaling ❌ Too basic
Okta Enterprise SaaS Enterprise features, SSO, compliance Very expensive ($8+/user/month) ⚠️ Enterprise only
Custom JWT Self-built Full control, no vendor lock-in High maintenance, security risks ❌ Too risky
