🔧 Core Concepts

🔑 KMS Keys (formerly CMKs)

KMS keys are the primary resource in AWS KMS. You can use KMS keys to encrypt, decrypt, and re-encrypt data, and to generate data keys that you can use outside of KMS.

📋 KMS Key Components
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        KMS KEY ANATOMY                            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  Key ID: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abcd1234    │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  │
│  │ 🔐 Key Material │  │ 📜 Key Policy   │  │ 📝 Metadata     │  │
│  │ (Never exposed) │  │ (Who can use)   │  │ (ID, ARN, Alias)│  │
│  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  │
│           │                    │                    │            │
│           ▼                    ▼                    ▼            │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │                    Cryptographic Operations                  │ │
│  │    Encrypt | Decrypt | GenerateDataKey | Sign | Verify      │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Property Description Example
Key ID Unique identifier (UUID format) abcd1234-56ef-78gh-90ij-klmnopqrstuv
Key ARN Amazon Resource Name arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abcd1234
Alias Friendly name for the key alias/anihpj-production-key
Key State Current status of the key Enabled, Disabled, PendingDeletion

🏷️ Key Types

🏢 AWS Managed Keys
  • Created automatically by AWS services
  • Format: alias/aws/service
  • Rotated automatically every year
  • No key management needed
  • Free (no monthly charge)
Examples:
• aws/ebs - EBS encryption
• aws/s3 - S3 SSE-KMS
• aws/rds - RDS encryption
👤 Customer Managed Keys
  • You create, manage, and control
  • Full control over key policy
  • Can enable/disable rotation
  • Can share across accounts
  • $1/month per key
Use for anihpj:
• alias/anihpj-db-key
• alias/anihpj-secrets-key
• alias/anihpj-s3-key
🔒 AWS Owned Keys
  • AWS owns and manages
  • Used by AWS services internally
  • Not visible in your account
  • No management or control
  • Free to use
Used by:
• DynamoDB default encryption
• SSE-S3 encryption
• Various AWS services
Feature AWS Owned AWS Managed Customer Managed
Key Policy ❌ No access 👁️ View only ✅ Full control
Key Rotation ✅ Auto ✅ Auto (yearly) ⚙️ Optional
Cross-Account ❌ No ❌ No ✅ Yes
CloudTrail Logging ❌ No ✅ Yes ✅ Yes
Monthly Cost Free Free $1/key

🏗️ Key Hierarchy

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        KMS KEY HIERARCHY                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│                    ┌─────────────────────────┐                          │
│                    │     🛡️ HSM Domain Key   │ ← Never leaves HSM      │
│                    │    (Root of Trust)      │                          │
│                    └───────────┬─────────────┘                          │
│                                │                                         │
│                    ┌───────────▼─────────────┐                          │
│                    │     🔐 KMS Key          │ ← Your master key        │
│                    │   (Customer Master Key) │   Protected by HSM       │
│                    └───────────┬─────────────┘                          │
│                                │                                         │
│            ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐                    │
│            ▼                   ▼                   ▼                    │
│   ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐          │
│   │  📦 Data Key 1  │ │  📦 Data Key 2  │ │  📦 Data Key 3  │          │
│   │  (EBS Volume)   │ │  (S3 Object)    │ │  (RDS)          │          │
│   └────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘ └────────┬────────┘          │
│            │                   │                   │                    │
│            ▼                   ▼                   ▼                    │
│   ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐          │
│   │  📁 Encrypted   │ │  📁 Encrypted   │ │  📁 Encrypted   │          │
│   │     Data        │ │     Data        │ │     Data        │          │
│   └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘          │
│                                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📦 Envelope Encryption

Envelope encryption is the practice of encrypting plaintext data with a data key, and then encrypting the data key with a top-level KMS key. This is how AWS services encrypt your data at rest.

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       ENVELOPE ENCRYPTION PROCESS                             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                               │
│  ENCRYPTION FLOW:                                                             │
│  ================                                                             │
│                                                                               │
│  Step 1: Generate Data Key                                                    │
│  ┌────────────┐      GenerateDataKey       ┌────────────┐                    │
│  │  Your App  │ ─────────────────────────▶ │    KMS     │                    │
│  └────────────┘                            └─────┬──────┘                    │
│                                                  │                            │
│                    Returns: Plaintext Data Key + Encrypted Data Key          │
│                                                  │                            │
│  Step 2: Encrypt Your Data Locally              ▼                            │
│  ┌────────────┐      AES-256-GCM         ┌────────────┐                      │
│  │ Plaintext  │ ────────────────────────▶│ Ciphertext │                      │
│  │   Data     │    (using plaintext key) │   Data     │                      │
│  └────────────┘                          └────────────┘                      │
│                                                                               │
│  Step 3: Store Encrypted Data Key with Ciphertext                            │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                     │
│  │  📦 Storage (S3, EBS, etc.)                         │                     │
│  │  ├── Encrypted Data Key (from KMS)                  │                     │
│  │  └── Encrypted Data (your content)                  │                     │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘                     │
│                                                                               │
│  ⚠️  IMPORTANT: Plaintext data key is discarded after encryption!           │
│                                                                               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  DECRYPTION FLOW:                                                             │
│  ================                                                             │
│                                                                               │
│  Step 1: Send Encrypted Data Key to KMS                                       │
│  ┌────────────┐       Decrypt            ┌────────────┐                      │
│  │  Your App  │ ────────────────────────▶│    KMS     │                      │
│  │            │   (encrypted data key)   └─────┬──────┘                      │
│  └────────────┘                                │                              │
│                    Returns: Plaintext Data Key │                              │
│                                                ▼                              │
│  Step 2: Decrypt Your Data Locally                                            │
│  ┌────────────┐      AES-256-GCM         ┌────────────┐                      │
│  │ Ciphertext │ ────────────────────────▶│ Plaintext  │                      │
│  │   Data     │    (using plaintext key) │   Data     │                      │
│  └────────────┘                          └────────────┘                      │
│                                                                               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Why Envelope Encryption?
  • Performance: Encrypt data locally without network latency for each operation
  • Security: KMS key never leaves the HSM - only data keys are exported
  • Scalability: Generate unlimited data keys from a single KMS key
  • Compliance: Audit all data key requests through CloudTrail

🚀 Getting Started

📋 Prerequisites

✅ AWS Account Setup
  • Active AWS account
  • IAM user with KMS permissions
  • AWS CLI configured (optional)
🔐 Required IAM Permissions
  • kms:CreateKey
  • kms:DescribeKey
  • kms:Encrypt / kms:Decrypt
📜 Recommended IAM Policy for anihpj
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "AnihpjKMSAccess",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Action": [
                "kms:Encrypt",
                "kms:Decrypt",
                "kms:GenerateDataKey",
                "kms:DescribeKey"
            ],
            "Resource": "arn:aws:kms:*:*:key/*",
            "Condition": {
                "StringEquals": {
                    "kms:RequestAlias": "alias/anihpj-*"
                }
            }
        },
        {
            "Sid": "AnihpjKMSListKeys",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Action": [
                "kms:ListKeys",
                "kms:ListAliases"
            ],
            "Resource": "*"
        }
    ]
}

🎯 Create Your First KMS Key

Console Method (Step-by-Step)
1
Navigate to KMS
AWS Console → Services → KMS → Create key
2
Configure Key
Key type: Symmetric | Key usage: Encrypt and decrypt | Advanced: KMS
3
Add Labels
Alias: alias/anihpj-production-key | Tags: Project=anihpj, Environment=production
4
Define Key Administrative Permissions
Select IAM users/roles who can manage this key
5
Define Key Usage Permissions
Select IAM users/roles who can use this key for cryptographic operations
✓
Review and Create
Review key policy and click "Finish"
🖥️ AWS CLI Method
# Create a new symmetric KMS key for anihpj
aws kms create-key \
    --description "Production encryption key for anihpj job portal" \
    --key-usage ENCRYPT_DECRYPT \
    --key-spec SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT \
    --tags TagKey=Project,TagValue=anihpj TagKey=Environment,TagValue=production

# Output will include:
# {
#     "KeyMetadata": {
#         "KeyId": "abcd1234-56ef-78gh-90ij-klmnopqrstuv",
#         "Arn": "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abcd1234...",
#         "KeyState": "Enabled",
#         ...
#     }
# }

# Create an alias for easier reference
aws kms create-alias \
    --alias-name alias/anihpj-production-key \
    --target-key-id abcd1234-56ef-78gh-90ij-klmnopqrstuv

# Verify the key was created
aws kms describe-key --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key

🔐 Encrypt & Decrypt Data

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        ENCRYPT & DECRYPT WITH KMS                          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  DIRECT ENCRYPTION (< 4KB data):                                           │
│  ┌─────────────┐       kms:Encrypt        ┌─────────────┐                  │
│  │ "password"  │ ─────────────────────▶   │ AQICAHh...  │                  │
│  │ (plaintext) │                          │ (ciphertext)│                  │
│  └─────────────┘                          └─────────────┘                  │
│                                                                             │
│  ┌─────────────┐       kms:Decrypt        ┌─────────────┐                  │
│  │ AQICAHh...  │ ─────────────────────▶   │ "password"  │                  │
│  │ (ciphertext)│                          │ (plaintext) │                  │
│  └─────────────┘                          └─────────────┘                  │
│                                                                             │
│  ENVELOPE ENCRYPTION (> 4KB data):                                         │
│  ┌─────────────┐   GenerateDataKey     ┌───────────────────────┐           │
│  │    App      │ ─────────────────────▶│ Plaintext Key +       │           │
│  └─────────────┘                       │ Encrypted Key         │           │
│        │                               └───────────────────────┘           │
│        │                                                                    │
│        └───▶ Use Plaintext Key to encrypt large data locally               │
│             Store Encrypted Key alongside encrypted data                    │
│                                                                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ AWS CLI
# Encrypt a string
aws kms encrypt \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key \
    --plaintext "MySecretPassword123" \
    --output text \
    --query CiphertextBlob

# Encrypt a file
aws kms encrypt \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key \
    --plaintext fileb://secrets.json \
    --output text \
    --query CiphertextBlob | base64 --decode > secrets.enc

# Decrypt ciphertext
aws kms decrypt \
    --ciphertext-blob fileb://secrets.enc \
    --output text \
    --query Plaintext | base64 --decode
🐍 Python (Boto3)
import boto3
import base64

kms = boto3.client('kms')

# Encrypt data
def encrypt_data(plaintext: str) -> str:
    response = kms.encrypt(
        KeyId='alias/anihpj-production-key',
        Plaintext=plaintext.encode()
    )
    return base64.b64encode(
        response['CiphertextBlob']
    ).decode()

# Decrypt data
def decrypt_data(ciphertext: str) -> str:
    response = kms.decrypt(
        CiphertextBlob=base64.b64decode(ciphertext)
    )
    return response['Plaintext'].decode()

# Usage
encrypted = encrypt_data("db_password")
original = decrypt_data(encrypted)
🎯 anihpj Django Integration Example
# anihpj/utils/encryption.py
import boto3
import base64
from django.conf import settings

class KMSEncryption:
    def __init__(self):
        self.kms = boto3.client('kms', region_name=settings.AWS_REGION)
        self.key_id = settings.KMS_KEY_ALIAS  # 'alias/anihpj-production-key'
    
    def encrypt_sensitive_data(self, plaintext: str) -> str:
        """Encrypt sensitive data like SSN, salary info for job applications"""
        response = self.kms.encrypt(
            KeyId=self.key_id,
            Plaintext=plaintext.encode(),
            EncryptionContext={
                'purpose': 'anihpj-sensitive-data',
                'environment': settings.ENVIRONMENT
            }
        )
        return base64.b64encode(response['CiphertextBlob']).decode()
    
    def decrypt_sensitive_data(self, ciphertext: str) -> str:
        """Decrypt sensitive data"""
        response = self.kms.decrypt(
            CiphertextBlob=base64.b64decode(ciphertext),
            EncryptionContext={
                'purpose': 'anihpj-sensitive-data',
                'environment': settings.ENVIRONMENT
            }
        )
        return response['Plaintext'].decode()

# Usage in models.py
from anihpj.utils.encryption import KMSEncryption

kms = KMSEncryption()
encrypted_salary = kms.encrypt_sensitive_data(applicant.expected_salary)

🔑 Configure IAM Permissions

Proper IAM configuration is essential for secure KMS access. Here's how to set up IAM policies for your anihpj application.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      IAM + KMS PERMISSION FLOW                              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   ┌──────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────────┐       │
│   │   EC2/ECS    │ ─────▶ │  IAM Role   │ ─────▶ │   KMS Key       │       │
│   │   Instance   │        │             │        │                 │       │
│   └──────────────┘        └─────────────┘        └─────────────────┘       │
│                                  │                       │                  │
│                                  ▼                       ▼                  │
│                           ┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐          │
│                           │ IAM Policy  │         │ Key Policy  │          │
│                           │ (attached)  │         │ (on key)    │          │
│                           └─────────────┘         └─────────────┘          │
│                                                                             │
│   ⚠️ BOTH must allow access! IAM policy + Key policy = Access Granted      │
│                                                                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📜 IAM Policy for anihpj EC2 Role
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Sid": "AllowKMSEncryptDecrypt",
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Action": [
        "kms:Encrypt",
        "kms:Decrypt",
        "kms:GenerateDataKey",
        "kms:DescribeKey"
      ],
      "Resource": [
        "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/*"
      ],
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "kms:RequestAlias": "alias/anihpj-*"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
🏷️ Least Privilege Actions
Use Case Required Actions
Encrypt data kms:Encrypt
Decrypt data kms:Decrypt
EBS encryption kms:CreateGrant
Envelope encryption kms:GenerateDataKey
Check key status kms:DescribeKey
🚀 Quick Setup Commands
# Create IAM role for EC2
aws iam create-role \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-kms-role \
    --assume-role-policy-document file://trust-policy.json

# Attach KMS policy to role
aws iam put-role-policy \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-kms-role \
    --policy-name KMSAccessPolicy \
    --policy-document file://kms-policy.json

# Attach role to EC2 instance profile
aws iam create-instance-profile --instance-profile-name anihpj-profile
aws iam add-role-to-instance-profile \
    --instance-profile-name anihpj-profile \
    --role-name anihpj-ec2-kms-role

🔑 Key Features

📜 Key Policies

Key policies are the primary way to control access to KMS keys. Every KMS key must have exactly one key policy, and it determines who can use and manage the key.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         KEY POLICY STRUCTURE                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │  📜 KEY POLICY                                                         │ │
│  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │
│  │                                                                         │ │
│  │  Statement 1: Root Account Access                                       │ │
│  │  ├── Principal: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:root                        │ │
│  │  ├── Actions: kms:*                                                    │ │
│  │  └── Effect: Allow                                                     │ │
│  │                                                                         │ │
│  │  Statement 2: Key Administrators                                        │ │
│  │  ├── Principal: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminRole              │ │
│  │  ├── Actions: kms:Create*, kms:Delete*, kms:Put*, kms:Update*         │ │
│  │  └── Effect: Allow                                                     │ │
│  │                                                                         │ │
│  │  Statement 3: Key Users                                                 │ │
│  │  ├── Principal: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/EC2Role                │ │
│  │  ├── Actions: kms:Encrypt, kms:Decrypt, kms:GenerateDataKey           │ │
│  │  └── Effect: Allow                                                     │ │
│  │                                                                         │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 IAM policies alone cannot grant access - key policy must allow it!      │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📋 Sample Key Policy for anihpj
{
    "Version": "2012-10-17",
    "Id": "anihpj-key-policy",
    "Statement": [
        {
            "Sid": "Enable IAM User Permissions",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:root"
            },
            "Action": "kms:*",
            "Resource": "*"
        },
        {
            "Sid": "Allow Key Administrators",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AnihpjAdminRole"
            },
            "Action": [
                "kms:Create*", "kms:Describe*", "kms:Enable*",
                "kms:List*", "kms:Put*", "kms:Update*",
                "kms:Revoke*", "kms:Disable*", "kms:Get*",
                "kms:Delete*", "kms:TagResource", "kms:UntagResource",
                "kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion", "kms:CancelKeyDeletion"
            ],
            "Resource": "*"
        },
        {
            "Sid": "Allow anihpj EC2 Instance Usage",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AnihpjEC2Role"
            },
            "Action": [
                "kms:Encrypt",
                "kms:Decrypt",
                "kms:GenerateDataKey",
                "kms:GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext",
                "kms:DescribeKey"
            ],
            "Resource": "*"
        },
        {
            "Sid": "Allow AWS Services",
            "Effect": "Allow",
            "Principal": {
                "Service": [
                    "secretsmanager.amazonaws.com",
                    "rds.amazonaws.com"
                ]
            },
            "Action": [
                "kms:Encrypt",
                "kms:Decrypt",
                "kms:GenerateDataKey*"
            ],
            "Resource": "*",
            "Condition": {
                "StringEquals": {
                    "kms:CallerAccount": "123456789012"
                }
            }
        }
    ]
}

🎫 Grants

Grants are an alternative to key policies for providing temporary permissions to use a KMS key. They're ideal for temporary access without modifying key policies.

✅ When to Use Grants
  • Temporary key access
  • Delegate encryption in workflows
  • Cross-service operations
  • Avoid key policy modifications
⚠️ Grant Limits
  • Max 50,000 grants per key
  • Cannot be revoked by grantee
  • Eventually consistent (2-5 min)
  • Adds complexity to audit
🖥️ Create a Grant (CLI)
# Create a grant for a Lambda function to decrypt
aws kms create-grant \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key \
    --grantee-principal arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AnihpjLambdaRole \
    --operations Decrypt \
    --retiring-principal arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AnihpjAdminRole

# List grants on a key
aws kms list-grants --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key

# Revoke a grant
aws kms revoke-grant \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key \
    --grant-id 0ab12cd34ef56gh78ij90kl

🔄 Automatic Key Rotation

AWS KMS can automatically rotate KMS keys every year. When key rotation is enabled, KMS generates new cryptographic material for the key annually.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         KEY ROTATION PROCESS                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  Year 1                   Year 2                   Year 3                   │
│  ┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────┐     │
│  │ 🔑 Key Material │      │ 🔑 Key Material │      │ 🔑 Key Material │     │
│  │    Version 1    │      │    Version 2    │      │    Version 3    │     │
│  │   (Created)     │      │   (Rotated)     │      │   (Rotated)     │     │
│  └────────┬────────┘      └────────┬────────┘      └────────┬────────┘     │
│           │                        │                        │               │
│           └────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┘               │
│                                    │                                         │
│                           ┌────────▼────────┐                               │
│                           │  🆔 Same Key ID │ ← Key ID never changes!      │
│                           │ & Key ARN       │                               │
│                           └─────────────────┘                               │
│                                                                              │
│  📌 How it works:                                                           │
│  • NEW encryptions use the LATEST key material                              │
│  • OLD ciphertexts are decrypted with the ORIGINAL key material             │
│  • All versions are retained indefinitely                                   │
│  • No re-encryption needed!                                                 │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅ Enable Rotation 
aws kms enable-key-rotation \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key
🔍 Check Rotation Status 
aws kms get-key-rotation-status \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-production-key
Key Type Auto Rotation Period Configurable
Customer Managed (Symmetric) Optional 90-2560 days ✅ Yes
AWS Managed ✅ Automatic 365 days ❌ No
Asymmetric Keys ❌ Not supported Manual only N/A
HMAC Keys ❌ Not supported Manual only N/A

🌍 Multi-Region Keys

Multi-Region keys are KMS keys in different AWS Regions that can be used interchangeably, with identical key material and key IDs. They're ideal for disaster recovery, global applications, and compliance requirements.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      MULTI-REGION KEY ARCHITECTURE                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   US-EAST-1 (Primary)              US-WEST-2 (Replica)                      │
│   ┌─────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────┐                  │
│   │  🔑 Primary Key     │   sync   │  🔑 Replica Key     │                  │
│   │                     │ ◀──────▶ │                     │                  │
│   │  mrk-1234abcd...   │          │  mrk-1234abcd...   │                  │
│   │  (Same Key ID!)    │          │  (Same Key ID!)    │                  │
│   └──────────┬──────────┘          └──────────┬──────────┘                  │
│              │                                 │                             │
│   ┌──────────▼──────────┐          ┌──────────▼──────────┐                  │
│   │  📦 S3 Bucket       │          │  📦 S3 Bucket       │                  │
│   │  (us-east-1)        │          │  (us-west-2)        │                  │
│   │  Encrypted with     │          │  Can decrypt with   │                  │
│   │  primary key        │          │  replica key!       │                  │
│   └─────────────────────┘          └─────────────────────┘                  │
│                                                                              │
│   EU-WEST-1 (Replica)                                                        │
│   ┌─────────────────────┐                                                    │
│   │  🔑 Replica Key     │  ← Same key material in all regions              │
│   │  mrk-1234abcd...   │                                                   │
│   └─────────────────────┘                                                    │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 Use Cases: DR, Global apps, Data residency compliance                   │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Create Multi-Region Key for anihpj
# Create primary multi-region key in us-east-1
aws kms create-key \
    --description "anihpj multi-region key for global deployment" \
    --multi-region \
    --region us-east-1

# Note the key ID (starts with mrk-)
# KEY_ID=mrk-1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd

# Create alias
aws kms create-alias \
    --alias-name alias/anihpj-global-key \
    --target-key-id mrk-1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd \
    --region us-east-1

# Replicate to us-west-2 for DR
aws kms replicate-key \
    --key-id mrk-1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd \
    --replica-region us-west-2 \
    --region us-east-1

# Create alias in replica region
aws kms create-alias \
    --alias-name alias/anihpj-global-key \
    --target-key-id mrk-1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd1234abcd \
    --region us-west-2

💰 Pricing & Cost Optimization

💵 Pricing Models

🔑 Key Storage
Customer Managed Key $1.00/month
AWS Managed Key Free
Custom Key Store (HSM) $1.00/month
Multi-Region Replica $1.00/month/region
📊 API Request Pricing
Encrypt, Decrypt, GenerateDataKey $0.03/10K
RSA requests $0.03/10K
Elliptic Curve requests $0.03/10K
Custom Key Store requests $0.15/10K
🎁 Free Tier
  • 20,000 free requests/month
  • AWS managed keys are free
  • Includes both symmetric and asymmetric
  • No expiration
💡 Tip: 20K free requests covers most small to medium applications!

💡 Cost Optimization Strategies

🔄 Use Data Key Caching

Cache data keys locally to reduce API calls. The AWS Encryption SDK provides built-in caching.

# Python: Use AWS Encryption SDK with caching
from aws_encryption_sdk import LocalCryptoMaterialsCache
cache = LocalCryptoMaterialsCache(capacity=100)
📦 Batch Operations

Use GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext for batch encryption. Generate keys in bulk.

🏢 Use AWS Managed Keys

For non-compliance workloads, AWS managed keys (aws/s3, aws/ebs) are free!

🗑️ Delete Unused Keys

Schedule deletion for unused keys. Each key costs $1/month.

📊 anihpj KMS Cost Estimate

Item Quantity Unit Price Monthly Cost
Customer Managed Keys 3 keys $1.00/key $3.00
API Requests (Encrypt/Decrypt) ~15,000/month $0.03/10K $0.00 (Free tier)
AWS Managed Keys (EBS, S3) N/A Free $0.00
TOTAL MONTHLY COST ~$3.00/month
💡 Recommendation for anihpj:
  • Use 1 key for Secrets Manager encryption (alias/anihpj-secrets-key)
  • Use 1 key for application data encryption (alias/anihpj-app-key)
  • Use AWS managed keys for EBS/S3 default encryption (free)
  • Total: ~$2-3/month

🔒 Security & Compliance

🛡️ HSM Security

AWS KMS uses hardware security modules (HSMs) to protect the confidentiality and integrity of your keys. The HSMs are FIPS 140-2 validated.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                           KMS HSM ARCHITECTURE                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │                      AWS KMS SERVICE LAYER                             │ │
│  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │ │
│  │  │                  Authentication & Authorization                  │  │ │
│  │  │          IAM Policies | Key Policies | Grants                   │  │ │
│  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                    │                                         │
│                                    ▼                                         │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │                    FIPS 140-2 VALIDATED HSM CLUSTER                    │ │
│  │  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐          │ │
│  │  │  HSM 1    │  │  HSM 2    │  │  HSM 3    │  │  HSM N    │          │ │
│  │  │  🔐       │  │  🔐       │  │  🔐       │  │  🔐       │          │ │
│  │  │ Level 2/3 │  │ Level 2/3 │  │ Level 2/3 │  │ Level 2/3 │          │ │
│  │  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘          │ │
│  │                                                                        │ │
│  │  • Keys never leave HSM in plaintext                                  │ │
│  │  • All cryptographic operations inside HSM                            │ │
│  │  • Tamper-evident and tamper-resistant                               │ │
│  │  • Hardware entropy for key generation                                │ │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔐
FIPS 140-2 Level 2

Standard KMS keys

🛡️
FIPS 140-2 Level 3

CloudHSM keys

🔒
Never Exported

Keys stay in HSM

📊 Auditing & CloudTrail

All KMS API calls are logged to AWS CloudTrail, providing a complete audit trail of key usage for compliance and security analysis.

📋 Events Logged
  • CreateKey, ScheduleKeyDeletion
  • Encrypt, Decrypt, GenerateDataKey
  • CreateGrant, RetireGrant, RevokeGrant
  • EnableKey, DisableKey
  • PutKeyPolicy, CreateAlias
📝 Sample CloudTrail Event
{
  "eventName": "Decrypt",
  "eventSource": "kms.amazonaws.com",
  "userIdentity": {
    "type": "AssumedRole",
    "arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AnihpjEC2Role"
  },
  "requestParameters": {
    "encryptionContext": {
      "aws:secretsmanager:SecretARN": "..."
    }
  }
}
🔍 Athena Query for KMS Usage Analysis
-- Find all Decrypt calls for anihpj keys in the last 7 days
SELECT 
    eventTime,
    userIdentity.arn as caller,
    requestParameters.keyId as key_id,
    sourceIPAddress,
    errorCode
FROM cloudtrail_logs
WHERE eventSource = 'kms.amazonaws.com'
  AND eventName = 'Decrypt'
  AND requestParameters.keyId LIKE '%anihpj%'
  AND eventTime > date_add('day', -7, current_date)
ORDER BY eventTime DESC;

📜 Compliance Standards

🏥
HIPAA

Healthcare data

💳
PCI DSS

Payment data

🇪🇺
GDPR

EU data protection

🔒
SOC 2

Security controls

🏛️
FedRAMP

Government

📋
ISO 27001

Info security

🎓 Advanced Topics

🏦 Custom Key Store (CloudHSM)

A custom key store lets you protect your KMS keys in AWS CloudHSM clusters that you control. Keys are generated and stored in your dedicated HSMs (FIPS 140-2 Level 3).

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CUSTOM KEY STORE ARCHITECTURE                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│   │     AWS KMS          │         │     YOUR CloudHSM Cluster            │ │
│   │  ┌────────────────┐  │         │  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐     │ │
│   │  │ Custom Key     │  │ ───────▶│  │   HSM 1    │  │   HSM 2    │     │ │
│   │  │ Store          │  │         │  │  FIPS L3   │  │  FIPS L3   │     │ │
│   │  └────────────────┘  │         │  └────────────┘  └────────────┘     │ │
│   └──────────────────────┘         │                                      │ │
│                                     │  🔑 Keys stored HERE (not in KMS)   │ │
│                                     │  🔐 You control the HSM cluster     │ │
│   Your VPC                          │  💰 ~$1.50/hour per HSM             │ │
│   ┌────────────────────────────┐   └──────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│   │  EC2    │  Lambda  │  etc  │                                            │
│   └────────────────────────────┘                                            │
│                                                                              │
│   💡 Use when: Regulatory requirements mandate dedicated HSMs               │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅ Pros
  • FIPS 140-2 Level 3
  • Single-tenant HSM
  • Full control over keys
⚠️ Cons
  • ~$1.50/hour per HSM
  • Higher latency
  • Complex to manage

🔗 External Key Store (XKS)

XKS lets you protect your AWS data using keys stored in your own key management infrastructure outside of AWS. Your keys never leave your HSMs.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      EXTERNAL KEY STORE (XKS)                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   AWS Cloud                              Your Data Center / HSM Provider     │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐               ┌─────────────────────────────────┐│
│   │     AWS KMS          │               │      External Key Manager       ││
│   │  ┌────────────────┐  │    HTTPS      │  ┌─────────────────────────┐   ││
│   │  │ XKS Proxy      │──┼──────────────▶│  │    XKS Proxy Endpoint   │   ││
│   │  │ Endpoint       │  │               │  └──────────┬──────────────┘   ││
│   │  └────────────────┘  │               │             │                   ││
│   └──────────────────────┘               │  ┌──────────▼──────────────┐   ││
│                                          │  │   Your HSM / KMS        │   ││
│   S3, EBS, RDS, etc.                     │  │   (Thales, Entrust,    │   ││
│   use KMS keys backed                    │  │    Fortanix, etc.)      │   ││
│   by external HSMs                       │  └─────────────────────────┘   ││
│                                          └─────────────────────────────────┘│
│                                                                              │
│   💡 "Hold Your Own Keys" - Ultimate control, keys never enter AWS          │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔀 Asymmetric Keys

KMS supports asymmetric keys for encryption/decryption and signing/verification operations. The private key never leaves KMS.

Key Type Algorithm Use Cases
RSA_2048 / RSA_4096 RSAES_OAEP, RSASSA_PSS Encrypt data, Sign documents
ECC_NIST_P256/P384/P521 ECDSA Sign/verify (compact signatures)
ECC_SECG_P256K1 ECDSA Blockchain/cryptocurrency
SM2 (China) SM2DSA, SM2PKE China compliance
🖥️ Create RSA Key for JWT Signing
# Create RSA asymmetric key for signing
aws kms create-key \
    --key-usage SIGN_VERIFY \
    --key-spec RSA_2048 \
    --description "anihpj JWT signing key"

# Get public key (can be shared)
aws kms get-public-key --key-id alias/anihpj-jwt-key

🏷️ HMAC Keys

KMS HMAC keys generate and verify hash-based message authentication codes (HMACs). Perfect for API authentication, message integrity, and token validation.

# Create HMAC key
aws kms create-key \
    --key-spec HMAC_256 \
    --key-usage GENERATE_VERIFY_MAC \
    --description "anihpj API authentication HMAC key"

# Generate MAC for a message
aws kms generate-mac \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-hmac-key \
    --mac-algorithm HMAC_SHA_256 \
    --message "api_request_body"

# Verify MAC
aws kms verify-mac \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-hmac-key \
    --mac-algorithm HMAC_SHA_256 \
    --message "api_request_body" \
    --mac <base64_mac>

💾 Data Key Caching

Cache data keys locally to reduce KMS API calls and improve performance. The AWS Encryption SDK provides built-in caching support.

🐍 Python: AWS Encryption SDK with Caching
import aws_encryption_sdk
from aws_encryption_sdk.caches.local import LocalCryptoMaterialsCache
from aws_encryption_sdk.key_providers.kms import KMSMasterKeyProvider

# Create a local cache (stores data keys in memory)
cache = LocalCryptoMaterialsCache(capacity=100)

# Create key provider with caching
key_provider = KMSMasterKeyProvider(
    key_ids=['alias/anihpj-production-key']
)

# Create caching CMM (Cryptographic Materials Manager)
caching_cmm = aws_encryption_sdk.CachingCryptoMaterialsManager(
    master_key_provider=key_provider,
    cache=cache,
    max_age=300.0,           # Cache data keys for 5 minutes
    max_messages_encrypted=100  # Re-use key for up to 100 encryptions
)

# Encrypt with caching - reduces KMS API calls significantly!
client = aws_encryption_sdk.EncryptionSDKClient()
ciphertext, _ = client.encrypt(
    source=b"sensitive_job_application_data",
    materials_manager=caching_cmm
)
💡 Performance Impact: With caching, you can encrypt 1000s of items with only 1-2 KMS API calls (instead of 1000s).

👥 Cross-Account Access

Share KMS keys across AWS accounts for centralized key management or multi-account architectures.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                       CROSS-ACCOUNT KEY ACCESS                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   Account A (Key Owner)                Account B (Key User)                 │
│   ┌─────────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────────────────┐         │
│   │ KMS Key Policy:         │          │ IAM Policy:             │         │
│   │ Allow Account B         │  ───▶    │ Allow kms:Encrypt       │         │
│   │ Principal to use key    │          │ on Account A's key      │         │
│   └─────────────────────────┘          └─────────────────────────┘         │
│                                                                              │
│   Key Policy Statement:                                                      │
│   {                                                                          │
│     "Sid": "AllowExternalAccount",                                          │
│     "Effect": "Allow",                                                       │
│     "Principal": {                                                           │
│       "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_B_ID:root"                               │
│     },                                                                       │
│     "Action": ["kms:Encrypt", "kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"],        │
│     "Resource": "*"                                                          │
│   }                                                                          │
│                                                                              │
│   ⚠️  Both key policy AND IAM policy must allow the action!                │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✨ Best Practices

🔒 Security Best Practices

Follow these security best practices to ensure your KMS implementation is robust and compliant.

🔑 Key Separation
  • Use different keys for different environments (dev/staging/prod)
  • Separate keys by data classification level
  • Create dedicated keys for each AWS service integration
  • Never share keys across AWS accounts (use cross-account grants instead)
🛡️ Least Privilege Access
  • Grant only necessary KMS actions (Encrypt vs Decrypt)
  • Use encryption context for additional access control
  • Avoid kms:* in policies (except root)
  • Regularly audit and revoke unused permissions
🔄 Key Rotation
  • Enable automatic annual rotation for symmetric keys
  • Consider more frequent rotation for high-risk data
  • Old key material retained for decryption
  • Asymmetric keys require manual rotation
📊 Monitoring & Alerting
  • Enable CloudTrail logging for all KMS operations
  • Set up alarms for unusual DecryptError patterns
  • Monitor for ScheduleKeyDeletion events
  • Alert on unauthorized access attempts

🔒 Security Checklist

🔑 Key Management
  • ☐ Use customer managed keys for sensitive data
  • ☐ Enable automatic key rotation
  • ☐ Set appropriate key deletion wait period (7-30 days)
  • ☐ Use separate keys for different environments
  • ☐ Implement key naming conventions
📜 Access Control
  • ☐ Apply least privilege to key policies
  • ☐ Use conditions in key policies (VPC, IP, tags)
  • ☐ Separate key administrators from key users
  • ☐ Review key policies regularly
  • ☐ Use grants for temporary access
📊 Monitoring
  • ☐ Enable CloudTrail for KMS API logging
  • ☐ Create CloudWatch alarms for unusual activity
  • ☐ Monitor for Decrypt failures (access issues)
  • ☐ Set up alerts for key deletion schedules
  • ☐ Review access patterns monthly

⚡ Performance Optimization

✅ Do
  • Use data key caching (AWS Encryption SDK)
  • Use GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext for async
  • Batch encryption operations
  • Use regional endpoints closest to workload
  • Implement retry logic with exponential backoff
❌ Don't
  • Call KMS for every encryption (use data keys)
  • Encrypt large data directly with KMS (4KB limit)
  • Share keys across unrelated applications
  • Ignore rate limits (5,500-30,000 req/sec/region)
  • Use synchronous calls when async is possible
📊 KMS Request Rate Limits
Operation Rate Limit (requests/sec)
Encrypt, Decrypt, GenerateDataKey 5,500 - 30,000
Sign, Verify (RSA) 500 - 2,000
Sign, Verify (ECC) 300 - 1,500
Custom Key Store operations 100 - 1,800

🗝️ Key Management Strategy for anihpj

📋 Recommended Key Structure
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    anihpj KMS KEY STRATEGY                                  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  Production Environment (us-east-1)                                         │
│  ├── alias/anihpj-prod-secrets-key     → Secrets Manager encryption        │
│  ├── alias/anihpj-prod-app-key         → Application data encryption       │
│  └── alias/anihpj-prod-backup-key      → Backup/archive encryption         │
│                                                                             │
│  Staging Environment (us-east-1)                                            │
│  └── alias/anihpj-staging-key          → All staging encryption            │
│                                                                             │
│  Development Environment                                                     │
│  └── (Use AWS managed keys)            → aws/secretsmanager, aws/s3        │
│                                                                             │
│  Cross-Region DR (us-west-2)                                                │
│  └── Replicate production keys         → Multi-region keys for DR          │
│                                                                             │
│  Shared Services                                                             │
│  └── aws/s3, aws/ebs                   → Default service encryption        │
│                                                                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🏷️ Tagging Strategy

Tag Key Example Value Purpose
Project anihpj Cost allocation
Environment production, staging, dev Environment identification
Purpose secrets, application, backup Key usage tracking
Owner security-team Ownership tracking
DataClassification confidential, internal Compliance
✅ DO
  • Use aliases for key references
  • Enable automatic key rotation
  • Use envelope encryption for large data
  • Implement data key caching
  • Tag all keys for cost/compliance tracking
  • Use separate keys per environment
  • Monitor with CloudTrail + CloudWatch
❌ DON'T
  • Directly encrypt data > 4KB with KMS
  • Share keys across unrelated apps
  • Use kms:* wildcard permissions
  • Delete keys without backup/audit
  • Ignore rate limits (30K/sec max)
  • Use production keys in dev/test
  • Skip encryption context

💼 Common Use Cases

💾 EBS Volume Encryption

Encrypt your EC2 instance storage to protect data at rest. Perfect for the anihpj application server.

📋 Enable Default Encryption
# Enable default EBS encryption
aws ec2 enable-ebs-encryption-by-default

# Set default KMS key for EBS
aws ec2 modify-ebs-default-kms-key-id \
    --kms-key-id alias/anihpj-prod-app-key
🚀 Launch Encrypted Instance
# Launch EC2 with encrypted EBS
aws ec2 run-instances \
    --image-id ami-12345 \
    --instance-type t3.medium \
    --block-device-mappings '[{
        "DeviceName": "/dev/xvda",
        "Ebs": {
            "Encrypted": true,
            "KmsKeyId": "alias/anihpj-prod-app-key",
            "VolumeSize": 50
        }
    }]'

📦 S3 Bucket Encryption (SSE-KMS)

Encrypt resumes, profile photos, and static assets uploaded to the anihpj S3 bucket.

# Enable default bucket encryption with KMS
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption \
    --bucket anihpj-uploads \
    --server-side-encryption-configuration '{
        "Rules": [{
            "ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault": {
                "SSEAlgorithm": "aws:kms",
                "KMSMasterKeyID": "alias/anihpj-prod-app-key"
            },
            "BucketKeyEnabled": true
        }]
    }'

# Upload file - automatically encrypted with KMS
aws s3 cp resume.pdf s3://anihpj-uploads/resumes/
💡 Tip: Enable S3 Bucket Keys to reduce KMS request costs by up to 99%!

🗄️ RDS Database Encryption

Encrypt your PostgreSQL database for the anihpj job portal at rest.

# Create encrypted RDS instance
aws rds create-db-instance \
    --db-instance-identifier anihpj-postgres \
    --db-instance-class db.t3.medium \
    --engine postgres \
    --master-username anihpj_admin \
    --master-user-password "$DB_PASSWORD" \
    --allocated-storage 100 \
    --storage-encrypted \
    --kms-key-id alias/anihpj-prod-app-key \
    --vpc-security-group-ids sg-12345 \
    --db-subnet-group-name anihpj-db-subnet

# ⚠️  Note: RDS encryption must be enabled at creation time!
# Existing unencrypted DB requires snapshot + restore to encrypt

🔐 Secrets Manager Integration

KMS automatically encrypts secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager. Use a customer managed key for compliance.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SECRETS MANAGER + KMS FLOW                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│  1. Store Secret:                                                            │
│  ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐   │
│  │ anihpj Django   │ ────▶ │ Secrets Manager │ ────▶ │      KMS        │   │
│  │ settings.py     │       │ PutSecretValue  │       │ Encrypt with    │   │
│  │                 │       │                 │       │ CMK             │   │
│  └─────────────────┘       └─────────────────┘       └─────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
│  2. Retrieve Secret:                                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐   │
│  │ EC2 Instance    │ ────▶ │ Secrets Manager │ ────▶ │      KMS        │   │
│  │ (anihpj app)    │       │ GetSecretValue  │       │ Decrypt with    │   │
│  │                 │◀────  │                 │◀────  │ CMK             │   │
│  └─────────────────┘       └─────────────────┘       └─────────────────┘   │
│                                                                              │
│  💡 EC2 role needs both secretsmanager:GetSecretValue AND kms:Decrypt       │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Create Secret with Custom KMS Key
aws secretsmanager create-secret \
    --name "anihpj/production/db-credentials" \
    --kms-key-id alias/anihpj-prod-secrets-key \
    --secret-string '{"username":"anihpj_admin","password":"SecurePass123!"}'

🔧 Troubleshooting

❌ Access Denied Errors

🔴 Error: AccessDeniedException
An error occurred (AccessDeniedException) when calling the Decrypt operation: 
User: arn:aws:sts::123456789012:assumed-role/AnihpjEC2Role/i-xxx is not authorized 
to perform: kms:Decrypt on resource: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/xxx
Solutions:
  1. Check IAM policy has kms:Decrypt permission
  2. Check key policy allows the IAM role/user
  3. Verify encryption context matches (if used)
  4. Check if key is in a different region
🔴 Error: KMSInvalidStateException
Key 'arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/xxx' is pending deletion
Solutions:
  1. Cancel key deletion: aws kms cancel-key-deletion --key-id <key-id>
  2. Re-enable the key: aws kms enable-key --key-id <key-id>

🔌 Key Unavailable Issues

Error Cause Solution
DisabledException Key is disabled aws kms enable-key --key-id <key-id>
KMSKeyNotFound Wrong key ID or region Verify key exists: aws kms describe-key
Unavailable Custom key store disconnected Check CloudHSM cluster health
ImportToken expired Import token > 24 hours old Request new import parameters

🐌 Performance Issues

⏱️ High Latency

If KMS calls are slow:

  • Use data key caching (AWS Encryption SDK)
  • Check network connectivity to KMS endpoint
  • Use regional endpoint in same region as workload
  • Consider VPC endpoints for private access
🚫 ThrottlingException

Hitting rate limits:

  • Implement exponential backoff
  • Use data key caching to reduce API calls
  • Request quota increase via Service Quotas
  • Distribute load across multiple keys

🐛 Debugging Commands

Essential commands for troubleshooting KMS issues in your anihpj deployment.

🔍 Check Key Status
# Describe key details
aws kms describe-key \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key

# Check key rotation status
aws kms get-key-rotation-status \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key

# Get key policy
aws kms get-key-policy \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key \
    --policy-name default
📜 Check IAM Permissions
# Simulate IAM permissions
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
    --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456:role/anihpj-role \
    --action-names kms:Decrypt \
    --resource-arns arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456:key/abc

# Get current caller identity
aws sts get-caller-identity

# List grants on key
aws kms list-grants \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key
📊 CloudTrail Logs
# Search for KMS events in CloudTrail
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
    --lookup-attributes \
      AttributeKey=EventSource,AttributeValue=kms.amazonaws.com \
    --max-results 10

# Filter by event name
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
    --lookup-attributes \
      AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=Decrypt \
    --start-time 2026-01-15T00:00:00Z
🧪 Test Encryption
# Quick encryption test
echo "test" | aws kms encrypt \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key \
    --plaintext fileb:///dev/stdin \
    --query CiphertextBlob \
    --output text

# Verify you can decrypt
aws kms decrypt \
    --ciphertext-blob fileb://test.enc \
    --query Plaintext \
    --output text | base64 -d

❓ Frequently Asked Questions

❓ Can I export my KMS key material?

No. KMS key material can never be exported from the HSM. This is a security feature. If you need exportable keys, consider:

  • AWS CloudHSM (you control the HSM)
  • External Key Store (XKS) - keys stored in your infrastructure
  • Generate keys locally and import to KMS (with EXTERNAL origin)
❓ What happens if I delete a KMS key?

All data encrypted with that key becomes permanently unrecoverable!

  • KMS enforces a 7-30 day waiting period before deletion
  • You can cancel deletion during this period
  • Set up CloudWatch alarms for ScheduleKeyDeletion events
  • Before deleting, search for resources using the key
❓ How do I encrypt data larger than 4KB?

Use envelope encryption:

  1. Call GenerateDataKey to get a data key
  2. Use the plaintext data key to encrypt your large data locally
  3. Store the encrypted data key alongside the ciphertext
  4. Discard the plaintext data key

The AWS Encryption SDK handles this automatically!

❓ Should I use AWS managed or customer managed keys?

AWS Managed Keys (free):

  • Simple setup, auto-rotation
  • Good for dev/test and non-sensitive data
  • Cannot customize key policy

Customer Managed Keys ($1/month):

  • Full control over key policy
  • Required for compliance (HIPAA, PCI, GDPR)
  • Can share across accounts
  • Custom rotation schedule

Recommendation for anihpj: Customer managed for production, AWS managed for dev.

⌨️ API & CLI Reference

🚀 AWS CLI Commands

🔑 Key Management
# Create key
aws kms create-key --description "anihpj key"

# List keys
aws kms list-keys

# Describe key
aws kms describe-key --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key

# Enable/disable key
aws kms enable-key --key-id <key-id>
aws kms disable-key --key-id <key-id>

# Schedule deletion (7-30 day wait)
aws kms schedule-key-deletion --key-id <key-id> --pending-window-in-days 30

# Cancel deletion
aws kms cancel-key-deletion --key-id <key-id>
🔐 Cryptographic Operations
# Encrypt data (< 4KB)
aws kms encrypt \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key \
    --plaintext "MySecret"

# Decrypt data
aws kms decrypt \
    --ciphertext-blob fileb://encrypted.bin

# Generate data key (for envelope encryption)
aws kms generate-data-key \
    --key-id alias/anihpj-prod-key \
    --key-spec AES_256

# Re-encrypt with different key
aws kms re-encrypt \
    --ciphertext-blob fileb://encrypted.bin \
    --destination-key-id alias/new-key
🏷️ Alias Management
# Create alias
aws kms create-alias \
    --alias-name alias/anihpj-prod-key \
    --target-key-id <key-id>

# List aliases
aws kms list-aliases

# Update alias (point to different key)
aws kms update-alias \
    --alias-name alias/anihpj-prod-key \
    --target-key-id <new-key-id>

# Delete alias
aws kms delete-alias --alias-name alias/old-key

🐍 Python Boto3

import boto3
import base64

# Initialize KMS client
kms = boto3.client('kms', region_name='us-east-1')

# ============ Key Management ============
# Create a new key
response = kms.create_key(
    Description='anihpj production encryption key',
    KeyUsage='ENCRYPT_DECRYPT',
    KeySpec='SYMMETRIC_DEFAULT',
    Tags=[
        {'TagKey': 'Project', 'TagValue': 'anihpj'},
        {'TagKey': 'Environment', 'TagValue': 'production'}
    ]
)
key_id = response['KeyMetadata']['KeyId']

# Create alias for the key
kms.create_alias(
    AliasName='alias/anihpj-prod-key',
    TargetKeyId=key_id
)

# ============ Encryption/Decryption ============
# Encrypt small data (< 4KB)
def encrypt_data(plaintext: str, key_alias: str = 'alias/anihpj-prod-key') -> str:
    response = kms.encrypt(
        KeyId=key_alias,
        Plaintext=plaintext.encode(),
        EncryptionContext={'purpose': 'anihpj-data'}
    )
    return base64.b64encode(response['CiphertextBlob']).decode()

# Decrypt data
def decrypt_data(ciphertext_b64: str) -> str:
    response = kms.decrypt(
        CiphertextBlob=base64.b64decode(ciphertext_b64),
        EncryptionContext={'purpose': 'anihpj-data'}
    )
    return response['Plaintext'].decode()

# ============ Envelope Encryption ============
# Generate data key for large file encryption
def get_data_key(key_alias: str = 'alias/anihpj-prod-key'):
    response = kms.generate_data_key(
        KeyId=key_alias,
        KeySpec='AES_256'
    )
    return {
        'plaintext_key': response['Plaintext'],      # Use this to encrypt
        'encrypted_key': response['CiphertextBlob']  # Store this with data
    }

# Example usage
encrypted = encrypt_data("user_ssn_123456789")
original = decrypt_data(encrypted)
print(f"Encrypted: {encrypted[:50]}...")
print(f"Decrypted: {original}")

📦 CloudFormation Templates

Infrastructure as Code for deploying KMS keys consistently across environments.

🔑 KMS Key with Alias
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: KMS Key for anihpj Job Portal

Resources:
  AnihpjKMSKey:
    Type: AWS::KMS::Key
    Properties:
      Description: Encryption key for anihpj production data
      EnableKeyRotation: true
      PendingWindowInDays: 30
      KeyPolicy:
        Version: '2012-10-17'
        Statement:
          - Sid: AllowRootAccess
            Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              AWS: !Sub 'arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:root'
            Action: 'kms:*'
            Resource: '*'
          - Sid: AllowEC2Role
            Effect: Allow
            Principal:
              AWS: !Sub 'arn:aws:iam::${AWS::AccountId}:role/anihpj-ec2-role'
            Action:
              - kms:Encrypt
              - kms:Decrypt
              - kms:GenerateDataKey
            Resource: '*'
      Tags:
        - Key: Project
          Value: anihpj
        - Key: Environment
          Value: production

  AnihpjKMSAlias:
    Type: AWS::KMS::Alias
    Properties:
      AliasName: alias/anihpj-production-key
      TargetKeyId: !Ref AnihpjKMSKey

Outputs:
  KMSKeyArn:
    Description: ARN of the KMS Key
    Value: !GetAtt AnihpjKMSKey.Arn
    Export:
      Name: anihpj-kms-key-arn

🏗️ Terraform Configuration

# terraform/kms.tf

# KMS Key for anihpj
resource "aws_kms_key" "anihpj" {
  description             = "KMS key for anihpj job portal encryption"
  deletion_window_in_days = 30
  enable_key_rotation     = true
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid       = "AllowRootAccess"
        Effect    = "Allow"
        Principal = { AWS = "arn:aws:iam::${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:root" }
        Action    = "kms:*"
        Resource  = "*"
      },
      {
        Sid       = "AllowEC2Role"
        Effect    = "Allow"
        Principal = { AWS = aws_iam_role.anihpj_ec2.arn }
        Action    = ["kms:Encrypt", "kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"]
        Resource  = "*"
      }
    ]
  })
  
  tags = {
    Project     = "anihpj"
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# Key Alias
resource "aws_kms_alias" "anihpj" {
  name          = "alias/anihpj-${var.environment}-key"
  target_key_id = aws_kms_key.anihpj.key_id
}

# Output
output "kms_key_arn" {
  value       = aws_kms_key.anihpj.arn
  description = "ARN of the KMS key"
}

🔗 AWS Integration

🖥️ EC2 Integration

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         EC2 + KMS INTEGRATION                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                              │
│   EC2 Instance Profile (IAM Role)                                           │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│   │  "Action": [                                                         │  │
│   │    "kms:Encrypt",                                                    │  │
│   │    "kms:Decrypt",                                                    │  │
│   │    "kms:GenerateDataKey"                                            │  │
│   │  ],                                                                  │  │
│   │  "Resource": "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/*"             │  │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                              │
│   Used for:                                                                  │
│   • EBS volume encryption                                                   │
│   • Secrets Manager decryption                                              │
│   • Application-level encryption                                            │
│   • S3 object encryption (SSE-KMS)                                          │
│                                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

⚡ Lambda Integration

Lambda functions can use KMS to decrypt environment variables and encrypt sensitive data.

# Lambda function with KMS-encrypted environment variables
import boto3
import os

kms = boto3.client('kms')

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    # Decrypt environment variable (auto-decrypted by Lambda)
    encrypted_db_password = os.environ['ENCRYPTED_DB_PASSWORD']
    
    # Or decrypt manually
    response = kms.decrypt(
        CiphertextBlob=base64.b64decode(encrypted_db_password)
    )
    db_password = response['Plaintext'].decode()
    
    # Use the decrypted password
    return {"status": "success"}

📋 CloudTrail Integration

All KMS API calls are automatically logged to CloudTrail for audit and compliance.

🔍 Query KMS Events with CloudTrail Lake
-- Find all failed KMS Decrypt attempts (potential security issue)
SELECT 
    eventTime,
    userIdentity.principalId,
    requestParameters.keyId,
    errorCode,
    errorMessage,
    sourceIPAddress
FROM cloudtrail_events
WHERE eventSource = 'kms.amazonaws.com'
  AND eventName = 'Decrypt'
  AND errorCode IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY eventTime DESC
LIMIT 100;

👤 IAM Integration

KMS uses both key policies and IAM policies for access control. Both must allow the action for it to succeed.

📜 IAM Policy Example
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [{
    "Effect": "Allow",
    "Action": [
      "kms:Encrypt",
      "kms:Decrypt"
    ],
    "Resource": "arn:aws:kms:*:*:key/*",
    "Condition": {
      "StringEquals": {
        "kms:RequestAlias": "alias/anihpj-*"
      }
    }
  }]
}
📋 Key Policy + IAM
Key Policy: "Allow IAM policies"
    │
    ▼
IAM Policy: "Allow kms:Decrypt"
    │
    ▼
✅ Access Granted!

Key Policy: ❌ Denies user
    │
    ▼
IAM Policy: "Allow kms:Decrypt"
    │
    ▼
❌ Access Denied!

  • AWS Encryption CLI - Command-line encryption tool
  • AWS CloudFormation - IaC for KMS resources

  • Boto3 (Python) - AWS SDK for Python
  • aws-encryption-sdk-python - Envelope encryption
  • Django Fernet Fields - Field-level encryption
🔍 Monitoring Tools
  • CloudTrail - API logging and audit
  • CloudWatch - Metrics and alarms
  • AWS Config - Compliance monitoring

📝 KMS Glossary

KMS Key (CMK)

Customer Master Key - the primary cryptographic key in KMS. Never leaves HSM.

Data Key

Generated key used to encrypt data locally. Encrypted by KMS key.

Envelope Encryption

Two-tier encryption: KMS key encrypts data key, data key encrypts data.

Key Policy

Resource-based policy attached to KMS key controlling access.

Grant

Temporary permission to use KMS key, often for AWS service integration.

HSM (Hardware Security Module)

Tamper-resistant hardware that securely generates and stores keys.

FIPS 140-2

US government security standard for cryptographic modules (Level 2/3).

Encryption Context

Key-value pairs for additional authenticated data in encryption operations.

Key Rotation

Automatic annual generation of new key material (old retained for decrypt).

Multi-Region Key

KMS keys replicated across regions for DR and low-latency access.

✅ KMS Production Checklist for anihpj

🔑 Key Setup
  • ☐ Create alias/anihpj-prod-secrets-key
  • ☐ Create alias/anihpj-prod-app-key
  • ☐ Enable automatic key rotation
  • ☐ Add required tags (Project, Environment)
  • ☐ Set up multi-region replica for DR
🔒 Security
  • ☐ Configure key policies (least privilege)
  • ☐ Separate admin and user permissions
  • ☐ Review IAM policies for kms:* actions
  • ☐ Enable encryption context
  • ☐ Test access from EC2/Lambda
🔗 Integration
  • ☐ Configure Secrets Manager with CMK
  • ☐ Enable EBS default encryption
  • ☐ Configure S3 bucket encryption (SSE-KMS)
  • ☐ Enable RDS encryption
  • ☐ Update Django settings for KMS
📊 Monitoring
  • ☐ Enable CloudTrail KMS logging
  • ☐ Create CloudWatch alarm for failures
  • ☐ Set up deletion scheduled alerts
  • ☐ Monitor API request costs
  • ☐ Review access logs monthly
